topics.title: Food Product Development - Development of New Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Product Development - Development of New Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Product Development (FPD)** is the systematic, multidisciplinary process of conceiving, formulating, optimizing, testing, and commercializing a new food item — from initial idea generation through market launch and post-launch evaluation. It integrates principles of food chemistry, microbiology, engineering, nutrition, sensory science, and consumer behavior to deliver safe, palatable, shelf-stable, and commercially viable products.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **New Product Development (NPD)** is treated as a structured **Stage-Gate Innovation Model** where each phase (stage) is governed by a decision checkpoint (gate) ensuring technical, regulatory, and economic viability before progression.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of NPD like building a house:**
> - **Idea (Blueprint)** → You imagine the house.
> - **Screening (Soil Test)** → You check if the land and budget allow it.
> - **Development (Construction)** → You actually build the prototype.
> - **Testing (Trial Stay)** → You live in it briefly to find flaws.
> - **Launch (Inauguration)** → You open it to the public.
> - **Post-Launch (Maintenance)** → You keep improving it based on feedback.

In a food company (think *Maggi*, *Britannia*, *Amul*), every new product — be it a low-sugar biscuit or a ready-to-eat curry — passes through this identical funnel.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams)

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-to-Market** | **12–36 months** for new food products | Industry benchmark |
| **Success Rate of NPD** | **< 10 %** (most products fail) | Strategic risk indicator |
| **R&D Budget Allocation** | **1–3 %** of company turnover | Innovation intensity |
| **Consumer Test Panel Size** | **75–150 trained/untrained panelists** | Statistical sensory reliability |
| **Shelf-life Target (RTE foods)** | **6–18 months** | Commercial viability window |
| **Hedonic 9-Point Scale** | **1 (dislike extremely) – 9 (like extremely)** | Sensory acceptance measurement |

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often award marks for quoting these standard figures. Memorize the **9-point hedonic scale** and the **Stage-Gate terminology** — they appear almost every year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stage-Gate Model of New Product Development

The Stage-Gate process (popularized by **Robert G. Cooper**) is the industry-standard framework used by multinational food companies. KTU examiners expect students to describe **8 stages** with their corresponding **gates**.

### Stage-Wise Breakdown

- **Stage 1 — Idea Generation (Discovery)**
  - *Why:* Every product starts as an idea; without ideation, no innovation exists.
  - *How:* Sources include consumer insights, R&D labs, competitor analysis, market trends, sales force feedback, and idea crowdsourcing platforms.

- **Gate 1 — Idea Screening**
  - *Why:* Eliminates weak, impractical, or legally non-compliant ideas early.
  - *How:* Uses feasibility matrices scoring idea on **strategic fit, technical feasibility, market potential, and regulatory clearance**.

- **Stage 2 — Concept Development & Definition**
  - *Why:* Translates abstract idea into a tangible product concept.
  - *How:* Defines target consumer, positioning statement, key benefits, packaging format, price band.

- **Gate 2 — Second Screen (Go/Kill Decision)**

- **Stage 3 — Business & Technical Feasibility**
  - *Why:* Ensures the product can be made profitably at scale.
  - *How:* Conducts preliminary cost analysis, raw material sourcing, capacity planning.

- **Stage 4 — Product Development (Formulation & Prototype)**
  - *Why:* Converts concept into a working laboratory/bench prototype.
  - *How:* Iterative bench trials, ingredient optimization, process selection.

- **Gate 3 — Development Gate**

- **Stage 5 — In-House Testing & Consumer Testing**
  - *Why:* Validates sensory, nutritional, and safety attributes.
  - *How:* Uses **hedonic tests, preference tests, triangle tests, and accelerated shelf-life studies**.

- **Stage 6 — Market Testing (Test Marketing)**
  - *Why:* Real-world validation in a limited geography.
  - *How:* Sells the product in selected cities/stores; measures trial, repeat purchase, distribution.

- **Gate 4 — Decision Gate (Launch/Postpone)**

- **Stage 7 — Commercialization (Launch)**
  - *Why:* Full-scale production and distribution.
  - *How:* Production scale-up, packaging line-up, marketing campaigns, distribution roll-out.

- **Stage 8 — Post-Launch Review**
  - *Why:* Continuous improvement and line extensions.
  - *How:* Sales tracking, consumer complaint analysis, formula refinement.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting New Product Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme specifically asks students to enumerate factors influencing NPD. Examiners look for a structured answer (4–6 factors with brief explanations).

1. **Consumer Preferences & Demographics** — Changing lifestyle, health consciousness, regional taste variations.
2. **Technological Advancements** — High-pressure processing (HPP), pulsed electric field (PEF), 3D food printing, nanotechnology in encapsulation.
3. **Raw Material Availability & Cost** — Seasonal availability, import dependence, sustainability.
4. **Regulatory Environment** — FSSAI (India), FDA (USA), EFSA (Europe) approvals and labeling laws.
5. **Competitive Pressure** — First-mover advantage, me-too product strategies.
6. **Nutritional & Health Trends** — Functional foods, fortified foods, plant-based proteins, low-GI formulations.
7. **Distribution & Cold-Chain Infrastructure** — Determines feasibility of frozen/refrigerated products.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the high-yield formula sheet. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breakage.

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **NPD Success Rate** | $\text{Success \%} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Launches}}{\text{Total Launches}} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage |
| **Sensory Acceptance (Mean Hedonic Score)** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i}{n}$ | $n$ = panelists; $H_i$ = score on 9-pt scale |
| **Product Trial Rate** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Consumers who tried}}{\text{Total reached}} \times 100$ | Marketing KPI, dimensionless |
| **Repeat Purchase Rate** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Repeat buyers}}{\text{Trial buyers}} \times 100$ | Marketing KPI, dimensionless |
| **Shelf-life Acceleration (Q₁₀ Rule)** | $Q_{10} = \dfrac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T} \approx 2$ | Reaction rate doubles per **10 °C** rise |
| **Break-Even Point (Units)** | $BE = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$ | Commercial feasibility |
| **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** | $\text{COGS} = \text{Raw Materials} + \text{Labour} + \text{Overheads}$ | Currency unit |
| **Gross Margin** | $GM = \dfrac{\text{Selling Price} - \text{COGS}}{\text{Selling Price}} \times 100$ | Expressed as percentage |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Stage-Gate model is not academic theory — it is actively used in:
- **PepsiCo & ITC Foods** for snack and beverage innovation pipelines.
- **Amul's product diversification** from dairy to chocolates, ice-creams, and paneer variants.
- **Nestlé R&D centers** in India (Nanjangud, Manesar) for Maggi variant development.
- **Start-up food incubators** (CFTRI Mysore, IIT-Kharagpur Food Tech Lab) for founder-stage product validation.

> [!TIP]
> In 14-mark KTU questions, always **diagram the Stage-Gate model using a flow chart** and **bold the gate-decision points**. Examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Shelf-Life Using the Q₁₀ Rule

The **Q₁₀ rule** is a thermodynamic shortcut for predicting shelf-life under accelerated temperature conditions. It is one of the most frequently asked derivations in KTU Food Technology papers.

### Statement of the Rule
> [!NOTE]
> For most food deterioration reactions, the rate constant $k$ approximately **doubles for every 10 °C increase in temperature**. This empirical relationship is described by the Q₁₀ factor.

### Mathematical Foundation

Let the reaction rate constant at temperature $T_1$ be $k_1$, and at a higher temperature $T_2$ be $k_2$. The Q₁₀ is defined as:

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm on both sides to linearize:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{10}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

**Step 2:** From the **Arrhenius equation**, the rate constant is:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{RT}}$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ is the universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K), and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

**Step 3:** Substitute the Arrhenius expressions for $k_1$ and $k_2$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \ln(k_2) - \ln(k_1) = -\frac{E_a}{R\,T_2} - \left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_1}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the difference:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the log-linearized Q₁₀ equation:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{10}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $(T_2 - T_1)$ from numerator and denominator:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for activation energy $E_a$:

$$E_a = \frac{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot \ln(Q_{10})}{10}$$

**Step 8:** The shelf-life ratio between two temperatures follows the inverse of the rate-constant ratio:

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = (Q_{10})^{-\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}}$$

where $\theta$ denotes shelf-life duration at the corresponding temperature.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A ready-to-drink (RTD) beverage was stored at **27 °C** for **6 months** before showing unacceptable sensory changes. Using $Q_{10} = 2$, predict its shelf-life at **7 °C** (refrigerated storage).

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 27 \,°C = 300 \,K$
- $T_2 = 7 \,°C = 280 \,K$
- $\theta_1 = 6$ months
- $Q_{10} = 2$

**Step 1:** Compute the temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 27 - 7 = 20 \,°C$$

**Step 2:** Apply the shelf-life ratio formula:

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = (Q_{10})^{\frac{T_1 - T_2}{10}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = (2)^{\frac{20}{10}} = 2^2 = 4$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\theta_2$:

$$\theta_2 = 4 \times \theta_1 = 4 \times 6 = 24 \text{ months}$$

**Conclusion:** The same beverage, when stored at **7 °C**, will have a shelf-life of **24 months** — exactly **4 times** the room-temperature shelf-life. This validates the business logic of cold-chain investment for premium beverages.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensory Evaluation Decision Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the student on **when to use which sensory test**. The following decision matrix is high-yield.

| Objective of Study | Correct Sensory Test | Type of Data | Statistical Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| Detect if a difference exists between two products | **Triangle Test** | Discrete (correct/incorrect) | Binomial probability |
| Measure degree of liking | **9-Point Hedonic Test** | Ordinal (1–9 scale) | ANOVA, mean ± SD |
| Determine preference between two products | **Paired Preference Test** | Nominal | Chi-square ($\chi^2$) |
| Rank multiple products on a single attribute | **Ranking Test** | Ordinal | Friedman test |
| Quantify specific attribute intensity | **Descriptive Analysis (QDA)** | Interval | Two-way ANOVA, PCA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stage-Gate New Product Development Flow

The diagram below maps the complete Stage-Gate process adapted for the food industry. Each **stage** is a work phase; each **gate** is a go/kill/recycle/hold decision checkpoint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STAGE 1 IDEA GENERATION"]:::stageNode
    G1{{"GATE 1 IDEA SCREENING"}}:::gateNode
    B["STAGE 2 CONCEPT DEVELOPMENT"]:::stageNode
    G2{{"GATE 2 SECOND SCREEN GO OR KILL"}}:::gateNode
    C["STAGE 3 BUSINESS AND TECHNICAL FEASIBILITY"]:::stageNode
    D["STAGE 4 PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT PROTOTYPE FORMULATION"]:::stageNode
    G3{{"GATE 3 DEVELOPMENT CHECK"}}:::gateNode
    E["STAGE 5 IN HOUSE AND CONSUMER SENSORY TESTING"]:::stageNode
    F["STAGE 6 TEST MARKETING LIMITED GEOGRAPHY"]:::stageNode
    G4{{"GATE 4 LAUNCH DECISION"}}:::gateNode
    H["STAGE 7 COMMERCIALIZATION FULL LAUNCH"]:::stageNode
    I["STAGE 8 POST LAUNCH REVIEW AND LINE EXTENSION"]:::stageNode

    A --> G1 --> B --> G2 --> C --> D --> G3 --> E --> F --> G4 --> H --> I

    classDef stageNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef gateNode fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#E65100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Blue rectangles** represent work stages where innovation activities occur.
> - **Orange diamonds** represent decision gates where senior management reviews and approves, recycles, or kills the project.
> - The arrows show unidirectional progression under a successful pathway; in reality, gates often send projects back for rework.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — NPD Risk-Reduction Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["1000 IDEAS"]:::broadNode
    P2["100 SCREENED CONCEPTS"]:::midNode
    P3["10 FORMULATED PROTOTYPES"]:::narrowNode
    P4["3 TEST MARKET PRODUCTS"]:::narrowerNode
    P5["1 COMMERCIAL LAUNCH"]:::successNode

    P1 -->|"Gate 1 Filter"| P2
    P2 -->|"Gate 2 Filter"| P3
    P3 -->|"Gate 3 Filter"| P4
    P4 -->|"Gate 4 Filter"| P5

    classDef broadNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
    classDef midNode fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F,color:#33691E
    classDef narrowNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#F57F17
    classDef narrowerNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#EF6C00,color:#E65100
    classDef successNode fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#B71C1C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **1000 → 1 funnel** illustrates a harsh reality: only **one product in a thousand ideas** typically becomes a successful commercial launch. KTU examiners use this statistic to test whether students grasp the **innovation risk** inherent in food NPD.

## 4.3 Decision Matrix for Product Development Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["NEW PRODUCT CONCEPT"]:::startNode
    Q1{"IS TECHNOLOGY AVAILABLE IN HOUSE"}:::decisionNode
    Q2{"IS MARKET SIZE GREATER THAN 10 CRORE INR"}:::decisionNode
    Q3{"IS SHELF LIFE GREATER THAN 6 MONTHS"}:::decisionNode

    A1["IN HOUSE FULL DEVELOPMENT"]:::actionNode
    A2["CO DEVELOPMENT WITH CONTRACT RESEARCH"]:::actionNode
    A3["LICENSING OR ACQUISITION"]:::actionNode
    A4["DROP OR PIVOT STRATEGY"]:::actionNode

    S --> Q1
    Q1 -->|"YES"| Q2
    Q1 -->|"NO"| A3
    Q2 -->|"YES"| Q3
    Q2 -->|"NO"| A4
    Q3 -->|"YES"| A1
    Q3 -->|"NO"| A2

    classDef startNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#E65100
    classDef actionNode fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#33691E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Define food product development. List any four factors influencing the development of a new food product.**

**Model Answer:**
Food product development is the systematic, multi-stage process through which a new food item is conceptualized, formulated, tested, and launched in the market using scientific, technological, and consumer-oriented principles.

**Four factors influencing new product development:**
1. Changing consumer preferences (e.g., demand for low-sugar, organic, vegan).
2. Technological advancements (e.g., HPP, pulsed electric field, 3D food printing).
3. Raw material availability and seasonal cost fluctuations.
4. Regulatory framework (FSSAI licensing, labeling, health claims).

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**What is the Stage-Gate model in NPD? Name any three gates used in this process.**

**Model Answer:**
The Stage-Gate model, developed by Robert G. Cooper, is a project management framework that divides the new product development process into discrete stages of activity separated by decision gates. Each gate acts as a quality checkpoint where management reviews progress, allocates resources, and decides whether the project proceeds, is recycled for revision, or is terminated.

**Three gates:**
1. Gate 1 — Idea Screening (initial filtering of raw ideas).
2. Gate 2 — Second Screen (go/kill decision after concept development).
3. Gate 3 — Development Check (post-prototype review).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Stage-Gate model of new product development** as applied to the food industry. Describe any **five stages** and **four gates** with their decision criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **consumer testing** and **sensory evaluation** in new food product development. Explain the **9-point hedonic scale** with its applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Stage-Gate Model

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Stage-Gate model is a structured innovation framework that divides NPD into discrete stages separated by go/kill decision gates.

**Five Stages (4 marks):**
1. **Idea Generation** — Sources: consumer insights, R&D brainstorming, competitor benchmarking, trend analysis.
2. **Concept Development** — Translates the screened idea into a defined product concept (target consumer, positioning, benefits).
3. **Business and Technical Feasibility** — Examines raw material supply, capital investment, projected ROI, and technical capability.
4. **Product Development** — Bench-scale formulation, ingredient optimization, prototype preparation, packaging trials.
5. **Commercialization** — Plant-scale production, marketing rollout, distribution network activation, post-launch tracking.

**Four Gates with Decision Criteria (2 marks):**
| Gate | Decision Criteria |
|---|---|
| Gate 1 — Idea Screening | Strategic fit, technical feasibility, preliminary market potential |
| Gate 2 — Second Screen | Concept attractiveness, competitive advantage, regulatory clarity |
| Gate 3 — Development Check | Prototype acceptability, cost targets met, shelf-life achieved |
| Gate 4 — Launch Decision | Test-market results, profitability projection, manufacturing readiness |

#### Model Solution for (b) — Consumer Testing & Hedonic Scale

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Consumer testing validates that the developed product meets the expectations of the target consumer segment in terms of sensory quality, acceptance, and purchase intent.

**Sensory Tests Used (2 marks):**
- **Discriminative tests** — Triangle test, duo-trio test (detects differences).
- **Descriptive tests** — Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA), flavor profile method.
- **Affective tests** — Hedonic tests, preference tests (measure liking).

**9-Point Hedonic Scale Explained (3 marks):**
- A standardized affective scale with anchors **1 = Dislike Extremely** to **9 = Like Extremely**, with a midpoint of **5 = Neither Like nor Dislike**.
- Administered to **75–150 untrained consumers** representative of the target market.
- Data analyzed using **mean hedonic score** $\bar{H}$ and **ANOVA** to detect significant differences between formulations.
- **Application:** Used in formulation optimization, comparing prototype variants, validating final formulation before launch, and benchmarking against competitors.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Consumer testing closes the feedback loop between R&D and the marketplace, reducing commercial failure risk.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**(a)** Describe the **complete process of new food product development** from idea generation to commercialization. Explain the **1000-to-1 innovation funnel** in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **shelf-life**. Using the **Q₁₀ rule**, derive an expression to predict the shelf-life of a food product stored at a lower temperature when its shelf-life at a higher temperature is known. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Complete NPD Process

**Process Stages (5 marks):**

1. **Idea Generation** — Brainstorming sessions, consumer surveys, market intelligence, sales force inputs.
2. **Screening** — Filters ideas against feasibility, profitability, and alignment with company strategy.
3. **Concept Testing** — Concept is presented to a small consumer panel for feedback on appeal and relevance.
4. **Business Analysis** — Estimates development cost, production cost, selling price, break-even volume.
5. **Product Development** — Recipe formulation, prototype creation, packaging selection, nutritional profiling.
6. **Consumer Testing** — Hedonic and preference tests; identifies best-performing variant.
7. **Test Marketing** — Limited regional launch to gauge trial and repeat-purchase rates.
8. **Commercialization** — Full-scale production, mass distribution, advertising campaign launch.
9. **Post-Launch Monitoring** — Sales tracking, consumer complaint analysis, line extension planning.

**1000-to-1 Innovation Funnel (2 marks):**
The innovation funnel describes the statistical attrition of ideas as they pass through successive gates. Out of every **1000 raw ideas** generated, only about **100 survive initial screening**, **10 reach the prototype stage**, **3 are test-marketed**, and **only 1 is successfully commercialized**. This demonstrates the **high-risk, high-investment** nature of food NPD and justifies rigorous gate-based decision-making.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Shelf-Life & Q₁₀ Derivation

**Definition of Shelf-Life (1 mark):**
Shelf-life is the period during which a food product retains acceptable sensory, nutritional, microbiological, and functional qualities under specified storage conditions.

**Statement of the Q₁₀ Rule (1 mark):**
The rate of most food deterioration reactions **doubles for every 10 °C increase** in temperature; mathematically, $Q_{10} \approx 2$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the definition:

$$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{10}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)$$

From the Arrhenius equation, the rate constants at $T_1$ and $T_2$ are:

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_1}} \qquad \text{and} \qquad k_2 = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_2}}$$

Their ratio gives:

$$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)} = e^{\frac{E_a (T_2 - T_1)}{R\,T_1 T_2}}$$

Taking logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a (T_2 - T_1)}{R\,T_1 T_2}$$

Substituting back:

$$\ln(Q_{10}) = \frac{10}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \frac{E_a (T_2 - T_1)}{R\,T_1 T_2} = \frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R\,T_1 T_2}$$

Since shelf-life $\theta$ is inversely proportional to the rate constant:

$$\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1} = \frac{k_1}{k_2} = (Q_{10})^{-\frac{T_2 - T_1}{10}}$$

Therefore, the shelf-life at temperature $T_2$ is:

$$\boxed{\theta_2 = \theta_1 \cdot (Q_{10})^{\frac{T_1 - T_2}{10}}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
This expression allows food technologists to compress years of shelf-life data into weeks of accelerated testing, enabling rapid product release decisions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Stages" with "Gates"** — Many students inter-mingle them. Stages are *work*; gates are *decisions*. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing hedonic scale endpoints as "1=like, 9=dislike"** — The correct anchors are **1 = Dislike Extremely, 9 = Like Extremely**. Reversing them costs full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the units in Q₁₀ derivation** — Always state $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K and temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius. Examiners specifically check unit conversion.
> 4. **Ignoring regulatory body names** — Always mention **FSSAI (India)** when discussing regulatory factors; vague answers like "government rules" lose marks.
> 5. **Diagrams without decision labels** — In Stage-Gate flowcharts, label each gate with its **decision criterion**. Unlabeled diagrams fetch only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- ✅ **Definition (must memorize verbatim):** FPD is the systematic, multidisciplinary process from idea generation to commercialization of a new food product.
- ✅ **Stage-Gate model:** Know all **8 stages** and **5 gates** by name and order.
- ✅ **Idea Generation sources:** Consumer insight, R&D, competitor analysis, sales force, crowdsourcing.
- ✅ **7 Factors influencing NPD:** Consumer trends, technology, raw materials, regulation, competition, nutrition trends, distribution.
- ✅ **Sensory test selection:** Discriminative (triangle), Descriptive (QDA), Affective (hedonic, preference).
- ✅ **9-Point Hedonic Scale:** Anchors are **1 = Dislike Extremely → 9 = Like Extremely**; middle **5 = Neither Like nor Dislike**.
- ✅ **Q₁₀ Rule formula:** $\theta_2 = \theta_1 \cdot (Q_{10})^{\frac{T_1 - T_2}{10}}$; typical $Q_{10} = 2$.
- ✅ **Always convert temperature to Kelvin** ($K = °C + 273$) in shelf-life derivations.
- ✅ **Innovation Funnel ratio:** **1000 ideas → 1 commercial launch** is the industry benchmark.
- ✅ **Regulatory Body for India:** **FSSAI** — must be cited in any regulatory-related answer.
- ✅ **COGS formula:** Raw Materials + Labour + Overheads.
- ✅ **Break-Even formula:** $\text{BE Units} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Price} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit}}$.
- ✅ **Draw a labeled flow chart** in every 7-mark question on NPD — visual answers score higher.
- ✅ **Real-world examples** (Maggi variants, Amul products, Britannia biscuits) earn **bonus impression marks** from examiners.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Need for Developing New Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of food technology and the consumer and convenience food industry, **new product development (NPD)** is defined as the complete, cross-functional process of bringing a novel or significantly improved food product from conceptual ideation through formulation, sensory evaluation, shelf-life testing, regulatory clearance, packaging, and commercial market launch. A "new product" in the food sector may range from a genuinely novel invention (e.g., edible cutlery, plant-based "meats") to a line extension, a reformulation, a new package size, or a re-launched repositioned product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** New product development in convenience foods is the strategic and scientific activity of identifying unmet consumer needs, generating ideas, screening them, developing prototypes, conducting tests, and commercialising food products that deliver added value in terms of convenience, nutrition, safety, and shelf life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a mother working in a corporate office in Kochi who returns home at 8 PM. She has **15 minutes** to feed her family something hot, tasty, and reasonably healthy. Twenty years ago, her options were limited — perhaps just a packet of instant noodles or nothing. Today, shelves are filled with ready-to-eat (RTE) idlis that steam in 2 minutes, heat-and-eat parathas, and 30-second microwaveable dals.

> **Analogy:** The development of new convenience food products is like designing a "time machine for the kitchen." Every new product essentially gives back time to the consumer, who trades culinary effort for convenience. Food technologists are the "engineers" of this time machine — they compress hours of preparation into minutes without losing the sensory pleasure, nutrition, and safety of home-cooked food.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why in One Line:** We develop new products because the world is changing — lifestyles, incomes, demographics, health awareness, and technology — and existing food products cannot keep up.

## 1.3 Key Drivers at a Glance

The food industry is one of the most dynamic sectors globally. The **need for new product development** arises from a convergence of the following fundamental forces:

- **Changing Consumer Lifestyles** — urbanisation, dual-income households, time scarcity.
- **Rising Health & Wellness Consciousness** — low-sugar, low-salt, high-protein, functional foods.
- **Demographic Shifts** — ageing population, Gen Z preferences, growing middle class.
- **Technological Advancement** — HPP (High Pressure Processing), MAP, ohmic heating, 3D food printing.
- **Economic Pressures** — globalisation, saturated markets, intense competition.
- **Regulatory & Safety Drivers** — FSSAI standards, food adulteration scandals, traceability demands.
- **Sustainability Concerns** — plant-based proteins, reduced food waste, eco-friendly packaging.
- **Shelf-Life & Distribution Challenges** — reaching remote markets without cold chains.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook (Mnemonic — C.H.A.N.G.E.S.):**
> **C** — Consumer demands shift
> **H** — Health awareness rises
> **A** — Ageing & demographics evolve
> **N** — New technologies emerge
> **G** — Globalisation & competition grow
> **E** — Environmental concerns intensify
> **S** — Safety & regulatory standards tighten
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of Need for New Product Development

Each pillar below represents a category of *necessity* — without addressing these, food companies risk becoming obsolete.

### Pillar 1 — Changing Consumer Lifestyles & Time Poverty

- Urbanisation in India has crossed **35%** of the population, and the share is rising annually.
- Nuclear families and working women have reduced average cooking time.
- **Resulting Need:** Products requiring less preparation, less skill, and minimal cleanup.

### Pillar 2 — Rising Disposable Income & Aspirational Consumption

- Growth of the Indian middle class is creating new willingness to pay for branded, packaged, premium foods.
- **Resulting Need:** Premium variants, gourmet convenience foods, single-serve packs.

### Pillar 3 — Health, Nutrition & Wellness Demands

- WHO estimates that non-communicable diseases account for **~71%** of global deaths, much linked to diet.
- Consumers seek products with reduced sugar, salt, saturated fats, and additives.
- **Resulting Need:** Fortified foods, probiotic foods, low-GI products, clean-label foods.

### Pillar 4 — Demographic Transitions

- Ageing populations in developed economies need soft, easy-to-chew, nutrient-dense foods.
- Younger consumers (Gen Z, Millennials) seek novel experiences, fusion flavours, and sustainable brands.
- **Resulting Need:** Age-specific nutrition (paediatric, geriatric), adventurous flavours, sustainable sourcing.

### Pillar 5 — Technological Enablement

- Technologies like **HPP** (High Pressure Processing, 400–600 MPa), **PEF** (Pulsed Electric Field), **MAP** (Modified Atmosphere Packaging), and **ohmic heating** now allow minimally processed, fresh-like convenience foods.
- **Resulting Need:** Products that leverage these technologies for better quality, safety, and shelf life.

### Pillar 6 — Economic Survival & Competitive Pressure

- Retail shelf space is finite. A product that does not innovate gets delisted within 12–24 months.
- **Resulting Need:** Continuous product refresh, line extensions, brand re-invigoration.

### Pillar 7 — Regulatory & Food Safety Drivers

- After incidents like the **Maggi noodles ban (2015)** in India over lead and MSG concerns, FSSAI tightened norms.
- Consumers now demand transparency in ingredients, origin, and manufacturing.
- **Resulting Need:** Reformulation, clean labels, traceability systems, certifications.

### Pillar 8 — Sustainability, Ethics & Environmental Concerns

- Livestock farming contributes ~14.5% of global GHG emissions (FAO data).
- **Resulting Need:** Plant-based proteins, upcycled foods, biodegradable packaging, low-carbon supply chains.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Driver of NPD | Specific Trigger / Metric | Resulting Product Trend | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time poverty | Working women in urban India (>$15B market) | RTE / RTC meals | MTR Ready-to-Eat, iD Fresh |
| Health consciousness | Diabetes affects 77M+ Indians | Low-GI, sugar-free, high-fibre | Sugar-Free Green, Kellogg's Zero |
| Ageing population | 10% of India is over 60 by 2030 | Soft-texture, fortified geriatric foods | Protinex, Ensure |
| Tech enablement | HPP, MAP, PEF processing | Fresh-like chilled RTE foods | Cold-pressed juices, HPP guacamole |
| Sustainability | Plant-based protein demand | Meat analogues, dairy alternatives | Beyond Meat, So Good, GoodDot |
| Regulation | FSSAI 2006 + amendments | Clean labels, no synthetic colours | Organic Tattva, Slurrp Farm |
| Competition | Retail delisting risk | Line extensions, limited editions | Maggi's 100+ variants in India |
| Demographics | Gen Z / Millennials | Fusion flavours, exotic superfoods | Quinoa, kombucha, acai bowls |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "Why is NPD needed?", a 7-mark KTU answer should cover **at least 4–5 drivers** with **one real-world Indian example** for each. The examiner rewards concrete examples over generic statements.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- NPD is the **commercial engine** that converts food science research (shelf-life extension, encapsulation, novel proteins) into a product a consumer will pay for.
- It links R&D labs (where students often begin their careers) with marketing, regulatory, and supply chain functions.
- Failure to innovate causes **brand decay** — e.g., many legacy Indian brands lost market share to modern FMCG disruptors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Matrix — Quantifying the Need for NPD

While "need" is qualitative, food companies quantify it through measurable indices. Below is the analytical matrix KTU students should master.

## 3.1 The Market-Driven Quantitative Model

The **demand pull for new products** can be expressed as:

$$
D_{NPD} = f(P_c, P_s, I, T, R, S, H)
$$

Where each variable is defined below:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{NPD} &= \text{market demand intensity for new food products} \\
P_c &= \text{consumer perception of product convenience (1--10 scale)} \\
P_s &= \text{perceived safety score} \\
I &= \text{disposable income index (real, per capita)} \\
T &= \text{available cooking time per household (hours/week)} \\
R &= \text{regulatory strictness index} \\
S &= \text{sustainability score (0--100)} \\
H &= \text{health awareness index (0--100)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The function $f$ is monotonically increasing in $I$, $H$, $S$, $R$, and $P_c$, and monotonically decreasing in $T$. In simple terms, the wealthier, more health-aware, more time-poor, and more regulation-conscious the market, the higher the demand for new products.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A food company observes that in city X, the average household cooking time dropped from **12 hours/week to 6 hours/week** over five years, while disposable income rose by **40%** and health awareness index rose from 50 to 75. Quantify the relative change in demand for new convenience food products, assuming a linear additive model:
>
> $$D_{NPD} = 0.3\,I + 0.25\,H + 0.2\,R + 0.15\,P_c - 0.1\,T + 0.1\,S$$

**Step 1 — Normalise all indices to 0–100 scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{old} &= 60 \quad (\text{normalised}), \quad I_{new} = 60 \times 1.4 = 84 \\
H_{old} &= 50, \quad H_{new} = 75 \\
T_{old} &= 12, \quad T_{new} = 6 \\
R_{old} &= 70, \quad R_{new} = 70 \text{ (no change assumed)} \\
P_c &= 65 \text{ (assume constant)} \\
S_{old} &= 40, \quad S_{new} = 55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $D_{NPD}$ before and after.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{old} &= 0.3(60) + 0.25(50) + 0.2(70) + 0.15(65) - 0.1(12) + 0.1(40) \\
&= 18 + 12.5 + 14 + 9.75 - 1.2 + 4 \\
&= 57.05
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{new} &= 0.3(84) + 0.25(75) + 0.2(70) + 0.15(65) - 0.1(6) + 0.1(55) \\
&= 25.2 + 18.75 + 14 + 9.75 - 0.6 + 5.5 \\
&= 72.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage change in demand.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\,\Delta D_{NPD} &= \frac{D_{new} - D_{old}}{D_{old}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{72.60 - 57.05}{57.05} \times 100 \\
&= 27.26\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The market demand for new convenience food products in this city has grown by approximately **27.3%** over five years, driven primarily by the twin forces of rising income and falling cooking time. This quantitatively justifies the company's decision to launch a new RTE product line.

## 3.3 The Stage-Gate Funnel (Decision Matrix)

Food companies filter NPD ideas through a stage-gate funnel. Each gate removes a proportion of ideas. A typical industry model:

| Stage | Purpose | Survival Rate | Cumulative Success |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idea generation | Brainstorm, market research | 100% | 100% |
| Gate 1 — Screening | Strategic fit, technical feasibility | 20% | 20% |
| Concept development | Define product, target consumer | 50% of survivors | 10% |
| Gate 2 — Business analysis | ROI, market size | 40% | 4% |
| Product development | Formulation, prototype | 60% | 2.4% |
| Gate 3 — Test marketing | Limited launch, feedback | 50% | 1.2% |
| Commercialisation | Full launch | 80% | 0.96% |

> The 1% final success rate illustrates why the *need* for new product development must be backed by rigorous need analysis — failed launches are expensive, often costing **$1–10 million** for a national FMCG launch.

## 3.4 Analytical Insight — Why "Need" is the Foundation

If a company skips the *need analysis* and proceeds directly to product development, the failure rate jumps to **80–90%**. Conversely, projects that begin with structured consumer need analysis (focus groups, ethnography, conjoint analysis) succeed at **2–3× the rate** of those that don't. This is the KTU-highlighted link between consumer research and successful NPD.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Eight Drivers of New Product Development (Radial Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NEED FOR NEW PRODUCT DEVELOPMENT"] --> B["Lifestyle Shifts"]
    A --> C["Health & Wellness"]
    A --> D["Demographic Change"]

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define new product development in the context of convenience foods. Mention any two drivers of NPD in India today.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1a]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** New product development (NPD) in convenience foods is the systematic, science-driven process of creating, testing, and launching novel or significantly improved food products that offer consumers benefits such as reduced preparation time, enhanced nutrition, longer shelf life, or improved sensory appeal. It encompasses ideation, formulation, packaging, regulatory clearance, and market introduction.
- **Two drivers (1 mark):** (i) Changing urban lifestyles and time poverty of working households; (ii) Rising health and wellness awareness leading to demand for low-sugar, low-salt, functional foods.

---

### Question 2: List any three reasons why established food companies must continuously innovate.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1b]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Shelf Space Scarcity (1 mark):** Modern retail has finite shelf space; products that do not sell are delisted within 12–24 months, forcing brands to keep refreshing their portfolio.
2. **Consumer Preference Evolution (1 mark):** Tastes, dietary patterns, and ethical concerns evolve rapidly — a brand stagnant in flavour or formulation loses relevance.
3. **Competitive Pressure (1 mark):** Domestic and multinational competitors launch variants frequently, eroding market share of legacy products.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Discuss the major factors necessitating the development of new consumer and convenience food products. (a) Explain with examples (7 marks). (b) Describe the stage-gate model of NPD with reference to convenience foods (7 marks).

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Q2A]**

#### Part (a) — Major Factors Necessitating NPD (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

New product development in convenience foods is not optional — it is a strategic necessity driven by the convergence of multiple factors. The major factors are:

1. **Changing Lifestyles and Urbanisation (1 mark):** With increasing urbanisation and dual-income households, the time available for cooking has shrunk dramatically. Working women in metropolitan cities now spend only 30–45 minutes per day on meal preparation. This has created a large market for ready-to-eat (RTE) and ready-to-cook (RTC) products such as MTR's ready meals and ITC's Aashirvaad instant mixes.

2. **Health and Wellness Consciousness (1 mark):** Modern consumers are increasingly aware of diet-related diseases such as diabetes, hypertension, and obesity. WHO data shows that **~71%** of global deaths are from non-communicable diseases linked to diet. This drives demand for low-sugar, low-sodium, high-fibre, and fortified foods. For example, Kellogg's launched high-fibre variants in India after studies showed Indians consume only half the recommended daily fibre.

3. **Demographic Shifts (1 mark):** India's population is ageing, and by 2030, over **10%** will be over 60 years of age. Geriatric consumers need soft, easily chewable, nutrient-dense foods. Simultaneously, Gen Z consumers drive demand for novel flavours and exotic superfoods. Brands like Protinex and Ensure target the elderly segment, while Slurrp Farm and Yoga Bar target younger consumers.

4. **Technological Advancement (1 mark):** Modern processing technologies such as **High Pressure Processing (HPP)**, **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**, **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)**, and **ohmic heating** enable fresh-like convenience foods with extended shelf life. Cold-pressed juice brands like Raw Pressery use HPP to deliver safe, additive-free products.

5. **Globalisation and Competitive Pressure (1 mark):** Multinational brands entering India and aggressive regional players create intense competition. Brands must continuously launch line extensions — for example, Maggi has over 100 variants in India — to retain shelf space and consumer attention.

6. **Regulatory and Food Safety Drivers (1 mark):** The FSSAI Act 2006 and its amendments have set strict standards for additives, contaminants, and labelling. Post the 2015 Maggi ban over lead and MSG concerns, consumers now demand clean labels and full disclosure, forcing reformulation.

7. **Sustainability and Ethical Concerns (1 mark):** Environmental concerns about livestock emissions, plastic waste, and food loss drive demand for plant-based proteins, biodegradable packaging, and upcycled foods. GoodDot in India, for example, offers plant-based "chicken" and "mutton" alternatives.

> **Conclusion (0.5 mark, optional):** Together, these factors make NPD a strategic imperative rather than a choice. Companies that fail to innovate risk losing relevance in a rapidly transforming food market.

#### Part (b) — Stage-Gate Model of NPD (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Stage-Gate model** (popularised by Robert G. Cooper) is a structured framework that divides the NPD process into discrete stages separated by decision gates. Each gate is a checkpoint where the project is evaluated for feasibility, fit, and financial viability before proceeding.

**Stages of the Model (3.5 marks):**

1. **Stage 1 — Idea Generation:** Broad collection of ideas from consumer insights, R&D, competitor analysis, and market trends. Example: identifying that urban consumers want high-protein snacks.

2. **Stage 2 — Idea Screening (Gate 1):** Ideas are filtered for strategic fit, technical feasibility, and brand alignment. Most ideas are dropped here.

3. **Stage 3 — Concept Development and Testing:** Surviving ideas are developed into detailed product concepts with defined target consumer, positioning, and preliminary formulation. Concepts are tested through focus groups and concept boards.

4. **Stage 4 — Business Analysis (Gate 2):** Detailed financial analysis — projected sales, ROI, market size, cost of goods sold. Only commercially viable concepts proceed.

5. **Stage 5 — Product Development and Prototyping:** The actual formulation, process development, packaging design, and shelf-life testing are conducted.

6. **Stage 6 — Test Marketing (Gate 3):** The product is launched in a limited geographic area to validate consumer acceptance, pricing, and supply chain readiness.

7. **Stage 7 — Commercialisation:** Full-scale national or regional launch, supported by marketing, distribution, and trade promotion.

8. **Stage 8 — Post-Launch Review:** Performance is monitored; learnings feed back into the next NPD cycle.

**Application to Convenience Foods (2 marks):**

- **Idea:** Indian consumers want a healthy, 5-minute breakfast option.
- **Gate 1:** Screening eliminates ideas that need cold chain or have high sodium.
- **Concept:** Multi-grain, no-added-sugar instant upma mix.
- **Gate 2:** Business analysis confirms a ₹500 crore addressable market.
- **Development:** Formulation with millets, MAP packaging, 6-month shelf life validated.
- **Test Market:** Launched in Bangalore, achieving 70% repeat purchase.
- **Launch:** National rollout in 3 SKUs.

**Key Advantages (1.5 marks):**

- Reduces risk through early-stage go/kill decisions.
- Ensures cross-functional alignment between R&D, marketing, and supply chain.
- Improves time-to-market and resource allocation.

> **Conclusion:** The stage-gate model provides food companies with a disciplined, risk-mitigated pathway to convert consumer needs into successful commercial products.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): "New product development in convenience foods is driven primarily by consumer and market forces." Critically discuss. (a) List and explain any five drivers of NPD (7 marks). (b) With the help of a suitable example, explain how a leading Indian food company used consumer insight to develop a new convenience product (7 marks).

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Q2B]**

#### Part (a) — Five Drivers of NPD (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Changing Lifestyles and Time Scarcity (1.4 marks):** Urbanisation, nuclear families, and working professionals have reduced the time and inclination for home cooking. Consumers need quick, easy-to-prepare meals. The proliferation of ready-to-eat (RTE) idlis, parathas, and dals in Indian retail directly addresses this driver.

2. **Rising Health and Wellness Awareness (1.4 marks):** The surge in lifestyle diseases has made consumers more calorie-, sugar-, and salt-conscious. The demand for functional foods fortified with vitamins, probiotics, or omega-3 fatty acids is rising. Examples: fortified milk, probiotic dahi, multigrain atta.

3. **Demographic and Generational Shifts (1.4 marks):** Ageing populations in some markets and young, aspirational consumers in others create contrasting needs. Geriatric consumers need soft, easy-to-digest, nutrient-dense foods, while Gen Z demands bold flavours, sustainable sourcing, and Instagram-worthy packaging.

4. **Technological Progress (1.4 marks):** Innovations in food processing, packaging, and preservation — such as HPP, MAP, retort processing, and aseptic packaging — enable the creation of new convenience products with longer shelf life and minimal preservatives. Smart packaging with QR codes for traceability is a recent addition.

5. **Regulatory and Safety Pressures (1.4 marks):** Stringent FSSAI regulations, increasing consumer awareness, and global food safety standards require brands to reformulate, eliminate artificial colours, and ensure traceability. This has driven clean-label innovations across categories.

> **Conclusion:** These five drivers, while not exhaustive, demonstrate the multi-dimensional nature of forces compelling food companies to innovate.

#### Part (b) — Indian Case Study (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Case: ITC's Aashirvaad — Launch of Instant Upma and Poha Mixes**

**Background (1 mark):** ITC identified through consumer research that urban Indian households, especially in southern and western India, were seeking convenient South Indian breakfast options. Traditional upma required semolina, vegetables, tempering, and 20–30 minutes of cooking — incompatible with time-pressed mornings.

**Consumer Insight (1.5 marks):** Focus groups revealed that consumers wanted:
- Authentic taste matching home-cooked upma.
- Preparation time under 5 minutes.
- Clean-label ingredients without artificial colours or preservatives.
- Long shelf life suitable for ambient storage.

**Development Process (2 marks):** ITC's R&D team:
- Selected pre-roasted semolina and freeze-dried vegetables to retain flavour and colour.
- Used a spice mix pre-balanced for regional taste preferences.
- Employed multi-layer laminated pouches with nitrogen flushing for **9–12 months** of ambient shelf life.
- Conducted extensive sensory trials across Bangalore, Chennai, and Mumbai.

**Launch and Outcome (2 marks):** The product was launched in 2018 under the Aashirvaad brand with strong TV and digital marketing. Within 18 months, Aashirvaad Instant Upma became one of the top three RTC mixes in its category, with retail distribution exceeding **200,000 outlets** nationally.

**Key Takeaway (0.5 mark):** This case illustrates how consumer-driven insight, technological enablement (freeze drying, MAP packaging), and disciplined NPD execution can transform a traditional recipe into a scalable national convenience product.

> **Conclusion:** Indian food companies that anchor their NPD in deep consumer understanding and apply modern food technology to address unmet needs consistently outperform those who do not.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Generic answers without examples:** Writing "consumer needs are changing" without naming a specific driver, segment, or product costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the link between driver and product:** Always connect "why" (driver) to "what" (resulting product) explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring Indian context:** The examiner rewards Indian examples (MTR, Maggi, iD Fresh, ITC, Britannia) over foreign-only examples.
> 4. **Forgetting regulatory angle:** Failing to mention FSSAI, clean labels, or the 2015 Maggi ban is a frequent deduction point.
> 5. **No block diagram in 14-mark answers:** A 14-mark answer without a single diagram or table typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Muddling drivers and stages of NPD:** Drivers explain *why* we need NPD; stage-gate explains *how* we execute it. Do not confuse the two.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** New product development in convenience foods is the science-driven, market-aligned process of creating and launching novel or significantly improved food products.
- **Eight Key Drivers (C.H.A.N.G.E.S.):** Consumer lifestyle shifts, Health awareness, Ageing demographics, New technologies, Globalisation, Environmental concerns, Safety regulations.
- **Quantification:** NPD demand can be modelled as a function of income, time poverty, health awareness, regulation, and sustainability indices.
- **Stage-Gate Funnel:** Most ideas are filtered; only ~1% reach commercialisation, making pre-launch screening essential.
- **Why Need Analysis Matters:** Skipping consumer need analysis raises failure rates to 80–90%; structured analysis doubles or triples success.
- **Indian Examples to Remember:** Maggi (line extensions), ITC Aashirvaad (instant mixes), iD Fresh (RTE idli/dosa), MTR (RTE meals), GoodDot (plant-based meat), Raw Pressery (HPP juices).
- **Technologies That Enable New Products:** HPP, PEF, MAP, retort, freeze-drying, ohmic heating, 3D food printing.
- **Regulatory Anchor in India:** FSSAI Act 2006, FSSAI Regulations 2011 (and amendments), BIS standards, AGMARK.
- **Diagrams in Exams:** Always include a block diagram of the stage-gate process OR a cause-and-effect diagram of NPD drivers in 14-mark answers.
- **Mnemonic for Quick Recall:** **"CHANGES"** (Consumer, Health, Ageing, New tech, Globalisation, Environment, Safety) — list whenever asked "Why NPD?"
- **Closing Thought:** New product development is not a luxury — it is the **lifeblood** of any food company operating in a dynamic, consumer-driven, and rapidly evolving market.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Strategies in Product Development

# Strategies in Product Development

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategies in Product Development

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Product Development Strategy** is a structured, long-term plan that guides a food company in conceiving, designing, formulating, testing, and commercializing new convenience and consumer food products that meet evolving market needs, regulatory frameworks, and technological capabilities. In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, it integrates **market-pull**, **technology-push**, and **consumer-led** approaches within a **Stage-Gate New Product Development (NPD)** funnel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEFTT725 – Module 1):**
> *Product development strategies refer to the systematic methods — such as concept generation, idea screening, prototype formulation, sensory evaluation, shelf-life testing, scale-up, and commercialization — used by food industries to translate consumer needs, technological innovations, and business objectives into successful convenience food products.*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu Reinvention"

Imagine a restaurant owner, **Mr. Joseph**, who has run a traditional Kerala breakfast joint for 25 years. One day, he notices that working couples want quick, ready-to-eat *puttu* and *kadala curry* that they can heat in a microwave at the office. He cannot just throw the idea together. He must:

1. **Sense** the need (market research).
2. **Generate ideas** (retort pouches? frozen? ready-to-mix?).
3. **Screen** them (cost vs. shelf-life vs. taste).
4. **Prototype** (laboratory batches with FSSAI-compliant ingredients).
5. **Test** (sensory panels, consumer trials).
6. **Launch** (distribution, branding, pricing).

This entire **journey of structured decisions** is the **Product Development Strategy** at work. Without a strategy, Mr. Joseph would either over-invest in a flop product or miss a market window.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is strategy needed in food product development?**
> - **Failure rate of new food products: 70–80%** within the first year (IFT estimate).
> - Average development cost: **₹15–50 lakhs** for a single SKU.
> - Time-to-market: **12–36 months** for convenience foods.
> - Strategy reduces risk, optimizes R&D spend, and ensures regulatory compliance (FSSAI, Codex, BIS).

---

### 1.3 Strategic Classification of Product Development

Product development strategies are broadly classified into **three philosophical drivers**:

| Strategy Type | Driver | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Market-Pull (Consumer-Driven)** | Unmet consumer need identified via market research | Maggi 2-Min Noodles addressing busy professionals |
| **Technology-Push (R&D-Driven)** | New processing technology enables innovation | Retort-processed ready-to-eat biryani in pouches |
| **Hybrid / Co-Development** | Combines consumer insight with technological capability | Functional probiotic yogurt with regional fruit flavors |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "What is a product development strategy?", always mention the **Stage-Gate model** as the operational backbone. Examiners reward answers that show a *systematic process* rather than a vague list of steps.

---

### 1.4 Core Strategic Frameworks in Food NPD

1. **Booz-Allen & Hamilton NPD Funnel** (Idea → Concept → Development → Launch)
2. **Stage-Gate Process (Cooper, 1988)** — 5 stages, 4 gates
3. **Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality**
4. **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** — applied to food formulation conflicts
5. **Open Innovation / Co-Creation** — partnering with consumers, suppliers, universities

> [!NOTE]
> The **Stage-Gate Process** is the **single most important model** for KTU 2024 answers on this topic. Memorize its 5 stages (Discovery, Scoping, Build Business Case, Development, Launch) and 4 gates (Idea Screen, Second Screen, Go to Development, Go to Market).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cooper Stage-Gate Process (Most Exam-Relevant Model)

**Dr. Robert G. Cooper's Stage-Gate Process** is the gold standard for new food product development. It consists of **5 Stages** (action phases) separated by **4 Gates** (decision points).

### 2.1.1 The 5 Stages

**Stage 1: Discovery / Idea Generation**
- **Why:** Generate a broad pool of new product ideas from multiple sources.
- **How:** Market research, consumer focus groups, ethnography, salesforce feedback, competitor benchmarking, technology scouting, in-house brainstorming (SCAMPER, morphological analysis).
- **Output:** 30–100 raw ideas; opportunity identification.

**Stage 2: Scoping / Preliminary Assessment**
- **Why:** Quickly filter ideas before heavy investment.
- **How:** Preliminary market sizing, technical feasibility, regulatory check (FSSAI), rough cost estimate.
- **Output:** 5–10 qualified concepts.

**Stage 3: Build Business Case / Concept Development**
- **Why:** Validate the winning concept with rigorous data.
- **How:** Detailed consumer concept tests, prototype sensory evaluation, financial modeling (NPV, payback), pilot plant trials, shelf-life protocol design.
- **Output:** 1–3 concepts with full business case.

**Stage 4: Development / Product Formulation**
- **Why:** Convert the validated concept into a market-ready product.
- **How:** Lab-scale formulation, scale-up trials, packaging development, nutritional labeling, FSSAI product approval, sensory descriptive analysis.
- **Output:** Finalized product, process specs, packaging specs.

**Stage 5: Launch / Commercialization**
- **Why:** Introduce the product to the market with full marketing support.
- **How:** Test marketing, full-scale production, distribution rollout, advertising, post-launch monitoring.
- **Output:** Product in market; performance tracking.

### 2.1.2 The 4 Gates (Go/Kill Decision Points)

| Gate | Decision Question | Pass Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **Gate 1 – Idea Screen** | Is the idea strategically aligned and technically feasible? | Strategic fit score > 6/10 |
| **Gate 2 – Second Screen** | Does the concept have market potential? | TAM > ₹10 cr, growth > 5% CAGR |
| **Gate 3 – Go to Development** | Is the business case financially sound? | NPV > 0, IRR > 15% |
| **Gate 4 – Go to Market** | Is the product ready and competitive? | All FSSAI approvals, shelf-life ≥ 6 months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Each gate functions as a **Go/Kill/Hold/Recycle** checkpoint. Roughly **1 in 7 ideas** that pass Gate 1 eventually reach market launch.

---

## 2.2 Types of Innovation Strategies in Food Product Development

| Strategy | Definition | Risk Level | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incremental Innovation** | Minor modifications to existing products | Low | New flavor variants of an existing chips brand |
| **Platform Innovation** | Develops a new product platform serving multiple variants | Medium | Tetra Pak ready-to-drink platform for juices, milk, soup |
| **Line Extension** | Adds new sizes, flavors, or packaging to existing line | Low | Amul cheese slices → Amul cheese cubes |
| **Disruptive Innovation** | Creates an entirely new market with low-cost, simple solutions | High | Maggi Magic Cubes; ITC e-Choupal 4G food processing |
| **Radical Innovation** | Breakthrough technology or scientific discovery | Very High | 3D-printed food, insect protein bars, cultured meat |
| **Architectural Innovation** | Repackages existing technology into a new application | Medium-High | Retort pouches adapted from military MREs |
| **Frugal Innovation** | Affordable innovation for price-sensitive markets | Low | Tata Salt Lite; low-cost fortified biscuits |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic: "I-P-L-D-R-A-F"** → Incremental, Platform, Line-extension, Disruptive, Radical, Architectural, Frugal.

---

## 2.3 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — The "House of Quality"

**QFD** is a structured planning tool that translates **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into specific product and process requirements. For convenience foods, QFD ensures the developed product aligns with consumer expectations on **taste, convenience, nutrition, shelf-life, and price**.

### 2.3.1 Steps in Building a House of Quality (HoQ)

1. **Identify Customer Requirements (WHATS)** — e.g., ready in 3 min, good taste, low calories, no preservatives.
2. **Identify Technical Requirements (HOWS)** — e.g., retort temperature, salt level, water activity, packaging type.
3. **Build Relationship Matrix** — strong (9), medium (3), weak (1) correlations.
4. **Prioritize Technical Requirements** — using calculated weights.
5. **Identify Trade-offs** — negative correlations in the roof matrix.

### 2.3.2 The QFD Priority Weight Formula

The **technical requirement priority weight (W_j)** is calculated as:

$$W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_i \times R_{ij} \right)$$

Where:
- $W_j$ = priority weight of technical requirement *j*
- $C_i$ = importance rating of customer requirement *i* (typically 1–5)
- $R_{ij}$ = relationship strength between customer requirement *i* and technical requirement *j* (1, 3, or 9)
- $n$ = total number of customer requirements

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of QFD in Food Industry:** Eliminates over-engineering, reduces reformulation cycles, and provides a **defensible design rationale** for FSSAI audits and ISO 22000 reviews.

---

## 2.4 Sensory Evaluation as a Strategic Tool

Sensory testing is integrated into **every gate** of the Stage-Gate process. The strategic selection of the right test method is critical:

| Gate | Recommended Sensory Test | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Gate 1 | None (concept testing only) | Avoid premature optimization |
| Gate 2 | Focus group + Hedonic 9-point test | Validate consumer acceptance |
| Gate 3 | Discrimination tests (Triangle, Duo-Trio) | Confirm prototype vs. target |
| Gate 4 | Descriptive analysis (QDA) + Consumer test | Final product validation |
| Post-Launch | In-home usage tests (IHUT) | Real-world acceptance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Statistic Frequently Tested:** The minimum number of consumer panelists for a **9-point hedonic test** is **n ≥ 30** for stable mean scores; for **discrimination tests**, the table values (e.g., α=0.05, β=0.10) dictate minimum n (typically 20–80 panelists).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hedonic Score Mean** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{n}$ | Average consumer liking score |
| **Purchase Intent Index (PII)** | $PII = \sum (p_k \times k) / n$ | Weighted purchase intent rating |
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Investment viability check at Gate 3 |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Compare NPD project returns |
| **Break-Even Volume** | $BEV = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Minimum units to recover dev cost |
| **Shelf-Life (Accelerated)** | $t_{real} = t_{accel} \times Q_{10}^{\frac{\Delta T}{10}}$ | Predict shelf-life from accelerated studies |
| **QFD Weight** | $W_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \times R_{ij}$ | Prioritize technical specs from VOC |
| **Concept Screening Score** | $S = \sum w_k \times r_k$ | Weighted score of NPD concept |
| **Fruity Maturation (Darroch) Index** | $A \times B$ matrix | Market attractiveness vs. competitive position |
| **Diffusion Rate (Bass Model)** | $\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = (p + q \frac{N(t)}{m})(m - N(t))$ | Forecast adoption of new convenience food |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Reminder:** When using the NPV or IRR formula, always state the **discount rate (r)** clearly. Board examiners deduct 1 mark if the rate is not specified. A typical food industry hurdle rate is **r = 12–15%**.

---

## 2.6 Strategic Pillars Summary

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Strategic Pillars to Memorize:**
> 1. Stage-Gate Process (5 stages, 4 gates)
> 2. Market-Pull vs. Technology-Push
> 3. Types of Innovation (I-P-L-D-R-A-F)
> 4. QFD / House of Quality
> 5. Sensory evaluation aligned to each gate
> 6. HACCP integration at Stage 4
> 7. FSSAI regulatory checkpoints
> 8. Scale-up strategies (lab → pilot → plant)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) for a New Convenience Food Product

The NPV calculation is a **mandatory component of Gate 3 (Go to Development)**. The following is an exhaustive, KTU-board-style derivation.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A food company is developing a **retort-processed ready-to-eat paneer butter masala** in a 200 g pouch. The project requires:
- Initial development cost: **₹20,00,000** (Year 0)
- Pilot plant cost: **₹5,00,000** (Year 1)
- Launch cost (marketing + FSSAI + packaging): **₹15,00,000** (Year 2)
- Expected annual cash inflows from Year 3 to Year 7: **₹12,00,000/year**
- Discount rate: **r = 12%**

**Objective:** Determine whether to pass Gate 3 (NPV must be > 0).

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

The NPV formula is:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

**Step 1: Identify the cash flows and timing**

| Year (t) | Cash Flow (CF_t in ₹) | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | –20,00,000 | Initial R&D investment |
| 1 | –5,00,000 | Pilot plant cost |
| 2 | –15,00,000 | Launch investment |
| 3 | +12,00,000 | Year 3 cash inflow |
| 4 | +12,00,000 | Year 4 cash inflow |
| 5 | +12,00,000 | Year 5 cash inflow |
| 6 | +12,00,000 | Year 6 cash inflow |
| 7 | +12,00,000 | Year 7 cash inflow |

**Step 2: Compute the present value of each cash flow**

The discount factor for year $t$ is:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1 + r)^t}$$

For r = 12% (0.12):

$$\begin{aligned}
DF_0 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^0} = 1.0000 \\
DF_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^1} = 0.8929 \\
DF_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^2} = 0.7972 \\
DF_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^3} = 0.7118 \\
DF_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^4} = 0.6355 \\
DF_5 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^5} = 0.5674 \\
DF_6 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^6} = 0.5066 \\
DF_7 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^7} = 0.4523
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute the present value of each cash flow**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_0 &= -20{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0000 = -20{,}00{,}000 \\
PV_1 &= -5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929 = -4{,}46{,}450 \\
PV_2 &= -15{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972 = -11{,}95{,}800 \\
PV_3 &= +12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118 = +8{,}54{,}160 \\
PV_4 &= +12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355 = +7{,}62{,}600 \\
PV_5 &= +12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674 = +6{,}80{,}880 \\
PV_6 &= +12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5066 = +6{,}07{,}920 \\
PV_7 &= +12{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4523 = +5{,}42{,}760
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Sum the present values**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_0 + PV_1 + PV_2 + PV_3 + PV_4 + PV_5 + PV_6 + PV_7 \\
NPV &= (-20{,}00{,}000) + (-4{,}46{,}450) + (-11{,}95{,}800) \\
&\quad + 8{,}54{,}160 + 7{,}62{,}600 + 6{,}80{,}880 + 6{,}07{,}920 + 5{,}42{,}760 \\
NPV &= -36{,}42{,}250 + 34{,}48{,}320 \\
NPV &= -1{,}93{,}930
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since **NPV = –₹1,93,930 < 0**, the project **fails the Gate 3 financial test**. The product should be **recycled or killed**.

**Step 6: Sensitivity analysis (for full marks)**

If the annual cash flow were **₹14,00,000** instead of ₹12,00,000:

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_{revised} &= -36{,}42{,}250 + (14{,}00{,}000 \times (0.7118 + 0.6355 + 0.5674 + 0.5066 + 0.4523)) \\
NPV_{revised} &= -36{,}42{,}250 + 14{,}00{,}000 \times 2.8736 \\
NPV_{revised} &= -36{,}42{,}250 + 40{,}23{,}040 \\
NPV_{revised} &= +3{,}80{,}790
\end{aligned}$$

> The project becomes viable if cash inflows rise by **₹2,00,000/year** or if discount rate drops to **r = 10%**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Concept Screening using Weighted Scoring

**Scenario:** A company has **5 new convenience food concepts** to screen at Gate 1. The strategic criteria and their weights (sum to 1.0) are:

| Criterion | Weight (w_k) |
|---|---|
| Market Potential | 0.30 |
| Technical Feasibility | 0.20 |
| Cost to Develop | 0.15 |
| Regulatory Ease (FSSAI) | 0.10 |
| Strategic Fit | 0.15 |
| Competitive Advantage | 0.10 |

Each concept is rated 1–10 on each criterion.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Collect the ratings matrix**

| Concept | Market | Tech | Cost | Reg | Strat | Comp |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1: RTE Biryani Pouch | 9 | 7 | 5 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| C2: Probiotic Lassi | 7 | 6 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 8 |
| C3: Vegan Sausage | 8 | 5 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| C4: Frozen Parotta | 9 | 8 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| C5: Fortified Atta | 8 | 9 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 6 |

**Step 2: Compute weighted score for each concept**

The screening formula is:

$$S = \sum_{k=1}^{m} w_k \times r_k$$

**Concept C1 (RTE Biryani):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C1} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.40 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 1.20 + 0.70 \\
&= 7.35
\end{aligned}$$

**Concept C2 (Probiotic Lassi):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C2} &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.10 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.10 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.70 + 1.05 + 0.80 \\
&= 6.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Concept C3 (Vegan Sausage):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C3} &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 1.05 + 0.90 \\
&= 6.55
\end{aligned}$$

**Concept C4 (Frozen Parotta):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C4} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.80 + 1.35 + 0.70 \\
&= 8.20
\end{aligned}$$

**Concept C5 (Fortified Atta):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{C5} &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.40 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 1.20 + 0.60 \\
&= 8.10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Rank the concepts**

| Rank | Concept | Score S |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | C4: Frozen Parotta | 8.20 |
| 2 | C5: Fortified Atta | 8.10 |
| 3 | C1: RTE Biryani Pouch | 7.35 |
| 4 | C2: Probiotic Lassi | 6.75 |
| 5 | C3: Vegan Sausage | 6.55 |

**Step 4: Decision**
Concepts **C4 and C5 advance to Gate 2** (Second Screen). Concept C1 may be **recycled** with cost re-estimation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Pattern:** When asked a "design / discuss" question on product development strategy, ALWAYS include a **weighted scoring table** like above. It demonstrates the "systematic" thinking the rubric rewards.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bass Diffusion Model for Adoption of a New Convenience Food

The Bass Diffusion Model forecasts how a new product is adopted over time:

$$\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[p + q \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \left[m - N(t)\right]$$

Where:
- $N(t)$ = cumulative adopters at time $t$
- $m$ = market potential (total eventual adopters)
- $p$ = coefficient of innovation (external influence, e.g., advertising)
- $q$ = coefficient of imitation (word-of-mouth influence)

**Simplified cumulative form:**

$$N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$$

**Numerical example for a Kerala organic ready-meal product:**
- $m = 1,00,000$ households
- $p = 0.02$ (moderate advertising-driven adoption)
- $q = 0.40$ (strong word-of-mouth — Kerala has tight social networks)
- $t = 3$ years

$$\begin{aligned}
N(3) &= 1,00,000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(0.02 + 0.40) \times 3}}{1 + \frac{0.40}{0.02} e^{-(0.42) \times 3}} \\
&= 1,00,000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-1.26}}{1 + 20 \times e^{-1.26}} \\
&= 1,00,000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.2837}{1 + 20 \times 0.2837} \\
&= 1,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.7163}{1 + 5.674} \\
&= 1,00,000 \cdot \frac{0.7163}{6.674} \\
&= 1,00,000 \times 0.1073 \\
&= 10{,}733 \text{ households}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** After 3 years, only ~10.7% of the market potential will have adopted the product. The product has a slow diffusion profile typical of new-to-market convenience foods in regional Indian markets.
