# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define vegetable powder. List any two commercial examples and their applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Vegetable powder is a free-flowing, dehydrated convenience product obtained by drying cleaned, peeled, blanched and size-reduced vegetables to moisture content < 5–8 % w/w, followed by grinding to a defined particle size (60–100 mesh). It is shelf-stable, lightweight and easily reconstituted.

**Examples & applications:**
1. **Onion powder** — used as a seasoning in instant noodles, snack seasonings, salad dressings, ready-to-eat curries and spice blends (replaces fresh onion in commercial kitchens).
2. **Tomato powder** — used in ketchup manufacture, instant soup premixes, pizza sauce bases, extruded snack coatings and bakery fillings (replaces tomato paste in 1:6 ratio).

*(Any two relevant examples accepted; 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark per example + 0.5 mark per application = 3 marks.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Why is blanching done before drying vegetables for powder manufacture? Mention two types of blanching.**

**Model Answer:**
Blanching is a short heat treatment given to vegetables before drying to:
1. Inactivate enzymes (peroxidase, catalase, polyphenol oxidase) which otherwise cause browning, off-flavours and nutrient loss during storage.
2. Reduce the microbial load (1–2 log reduction).
3. Expel intercellular air → improves heat transfer and drying rate.
4. Soften tissue → reduces drying energy.
5. Fix the natural colour (chlorophyll fixation in leafy greens with MgCO₃).

**Types of blanching:**
1. **Hot-water blanching** — vegetables dipped in 80–100 °C water for 1–5 min. Simple but causes leaching of water-soluble vitamins.
2. **Steam blanching** — vegetables exposed to live steam at 100 °C for 1–3 min. Less leaching, preferred for leafy greens and peas.
*(A third acceptable answer: Chemical / KMS blanching, 0.1 % citric acid blanch.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**
> **(a)** Describe in detail the **general manufacturing process of vegetable powder** with a process flow diagram. Explain the role of **blanching, drying and grinding** in the chain. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Compare **spray drying, drum drying and freeze drying** as applied to vegetable powder production, with respect to product quality, nutrient retention, energy consumption and cost. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) Process Description

1. **Raw-material reception & sorting** — mature, undamaged, disease-free vegetables; rejection of wilted/rotten units.
2. **Cleaning & washing** — water with 50–100 ppm free chlorine; spray washing; air-bubble washing for leafy greens.
3. **Peeling & trimming** — abrasive peeler (carrot, potato), steam peeler (beetroot, tomato), flame peeler (onion), manual peeling (garlic, ginger).
4. **Slicing / dicing** — uniform geometry of 3–6 mm. Uniformity is non-negotiable for uniform drying.
5. **Blanching** — steam 2–3 min or hot water 80–100 °C for 2–5 min. Function: enzyme inactivation, microbial reduction, air removal, colour fixation.
6. **Sulphiting / chemical dip** (optional) — 0.1 % KMS for 10 min for carrot, potato, apple-flavoured veg; not allowed for onion/garlic as it destroys flavour compounds.
7. **Drying** — by cabinet, tunnel, fluidized bed, spray, drum or freeze dryer to < 5–8 % moisture.
8. **Grinding / milling** — hammer mill (coarse) or pin mill (fine) followed by air-classification to remove oversize.
9. **Sieving** — 60–100 mesh for instant-grade powder.
10. **Quality control** — moisture, a<sub>w</sub>, colour (L*a*b*), rehydration ratio, microbial counts, pesticide residue.
11. **Packaging** — nitrogen-flushed, moisture-/oxygen-/light-barrier pouches; sealed with vacuum or N<sub>2</sub> flush.
12. **Storage** — < 25 °C, RH < 60 %, away from sunlight.

**Role of blanching:** enzyme inactivation, microbial control, faster drying.
**Role of drying:** moisture reduction, shelf-life extension, weight reduction.
**Role of grinding:** size reduction to fine powder, ensuring rapid rehydration and homogeneous blending.

#### Model Solution — (b) Comparison

| Parameter | Spray Drying | Drum Drying | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Atomized droplets in hot air | Thin film on heated drum | Sublimation of ice under vacuum |
| **Form of feed** | Liquid / slurry / puree | Paste / puree | Frozen solid |
| **Inlet air temp** | 180–220 °C | 130–160 °C (drum surface) | −30 to −40 °C shelf, vacuum |
| **Drying time** | 5–30 sec | 30–60 sec | 8–24 h |
| **Nutrient retention** | Moderate (heat damage possible) | Moderate–poor (extended heat) | **Excellent** |
| **Colour retention** | Good (with cold wall) | Fair (Maillard browning) | **Excellent** |
| **Rehydration** | **Instant** (porous particles) | Slow (dense flakes) | **Instant** (sponge-like) |
| **Energy consumption** | Moderate (heat recovery possible) | Moderate | **Highest** (vacuum + refrigeration) |
| **Capital cost** | High | High | Very high |
| **Capacity (tonnes/day)** | 5–50 | 2–10 | 0.05–1 |
| **Best for** | Tomato, pumpkin, beetroot | Tomato paste → powder | Moringa, herbs, premium powders |
| **Product form** | Free-flowing spherical powder | Flakes, requires grinding | Porous cake, requires grinding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **drum dryer = low-quality powder**. Drum drying is *not* low-quality *per se* — for tomato and some roots it is the industry default because of throughput. Read the question stem carefully: if it specifies "high-end nutraceutical" → freeze dry; if it specifies "instant soup premix" → spray dry; if it specifies "ketchup industry raw material" → drum dry.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** What is **water activity (a<sub>w</sub>)**? Explain its significance in the shelf life of vegetable powder. Why is **< 0.6** the critical target? (7 marks)
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction and working of a spray dryer** for vegetable powder production. Discuss the factors affecting powder quality in spray drying. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a) Water Activity

**Definition:**
Water activity (a<sub>w</sub>) is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in a food (p) to the saturation vapour pressure of pure water (p₀) at the same temperature:

$$a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH \, (\%)}{100}$$

**Significance in shelf life of vegetable powder:**
1. **Microbial stability** — bacteria need a<sub>w</sub> > 0.86; yeasts > 0.70; moulds > 0.60. Below 0.60, no microbial growth is possible.
2. **Enzymatic stability** — most enzymes are inactive below a<sub>w</sub> 0.6.
3. **Maillard browning & lipid oxidation** — moisture zones of 0.6–0.85 are the most reactive; going below 0.3 may accelerate lipid oxidation (need careful packaging).
4. **Texture / caking** — powders with a<sub>w</sub> 0.4–0.6 are free-flowing; above 0.7 they cake.
5. **Vitamin stability** — ascorbic acid, thiamine, folate degrade at intermediate a<sub>w</sub> (0.5–0.7).
6. **Shelf life prediction** — via sorption isotherm (BET / GAB models) one can predict *critical a<sub>w</sub>* at which the powder is most stable — the **BET monolayer** (M<sub>m</sub>).

**Why < 0.6?**
- **Most moulds** (Aspergillus, Penicillium, Fusarium) cannot grow below a<sub>w</sub> 0.60 → no mould spoilage.
- **Yeasts** cannot grow below 0.70.
- **Pathogenic bacteria** (Salmonella, Listeria) cannot grow below 0.86.
- **Non-enzymatic browning** is minimized in the a<sub>w</sub> range 0.2–0.4.
- For a vegetable powder at 4–6 % moisture wb with hygroscopic balance, a<sub>w</sub> naturally falls in the 0.2–0.4 range — *well within the safe zone* — provided the packaging prevents moisture ingress.

#### Model Solution — (b) Spray Dryer

**Construction (refer Mermaid in Section 4.2):**
- **Feed tank** with agitator (to keep puree homogeneous).
- **Feed pump** (peristaltic or positive-displacement).
- **Atomizer** — either **centrifugal disc** (high speed 10 000–25 000 rpm) or **pressure nozzle** (50–200 bar) or **pneumatic nozzle** (two-fluid).
- **Drying chamber** — large stainless-steel cylindrical-conical vessel; co-current air flow to prevent heat damage to powder.
- **Air heater** — indirect gas or steam heater raising air to 180–220 °C.
- **Powder recovery system** — primary cyclone separator + secondary bag filter.
- **Exhaust fan + scrubber** — removes fine particulates and water vapour.
- **Powder collection hopper** with pneumatic conveying to packaging.

**Working:**
1. Vegetable puree / slurry is atomized into a fine mist of droplets (10–100 µm).
2. Hot air contacts the droplets; water evaporates from the surface in 0.5–5 seconds.
3. Dried particles fall to the chamber base or are entrained to the cyclone.
4. Cyclone separates powder from the moist exhaust air.
5. Powder collected at the cyclone base is discharged and conveyed to sieving/packing.

**Factors affecting powder quality:**
1. **Inlet air temperature** — higher = faster drying but risk of heat damage and caramelization.
2. **Outlet air temperature** — controls final moisture and powder temperature.
3. **Atomizer type and speed** — determines droplet size and hence particle size of powder.
4. **Feed solids concentration** — higher = bigger particles, less over-drying.
5. **Feed viscosity & temperature** — affects atomization pattern.
6. **Air flow rate & direction** — co-current (most common, gentler on product) vs counter-current.
7. **Inlet air humidity** — lower humidity = faster drying.
8. **Residence time** — controlled by chamber geometry and air flow.
9. **Carrier agent** (maltodextrin, gum arabic) — used for high-sugar/hygroscopic purees like tomato to prevent stickiness on the chamber wall.
10. **Drying-aid / anti-caking agent** — added to free-flowing formulations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes on spray-dryer answers:**
> 1. Forgetting to mention **co-current vs counter-current** airflow.
> 2. Not mentioning **carrier/maltodextrin** for tomato/carrot powders — which stick to the chamber wall (the "stickiness point" problem at T<sub>g</sub>+10–20 °C).
> 3. Confusing **droplet size** (10–100 µm) with **final powder particle size** (typically 20–200 µm, larger because of solid content in droplet).
> 4. Writing the inlet temperature as 600 °C — the correct range is 180–220 °C; outlet is 80–100 °C.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last 5 min before the exam)**

- **Definition:** Vegetable powder = dehydrated (< 5–8 % moisture) free-flowing particulate product made by drying blanched, size-reduced vegetables.
- **Six-step pipeline:** Clean → Cut → Condition (blanch) → Dehydrate → Grind → Sieve/Pack (*C³-DGS*).
- **Blanching** is MANDATORY for most vegetables (not for onion/garlic as it kills flavour).
- **Drying methods** in increasing order of nutrient retention: Sun < Cabinet < Drum < Tunnel < Fluidized ≈ Spray < Freeze. In reverse order of cost.
- **Spray dryer** is for *purees*; **Cabinet/Tunnel** for *slices/dices*; **Drum** for *pastes*; **Freeze** for *premium nutraceuticals*.
- **Critical a<sub>w</sub> for microbial safety = 0.60.** Target: 0.2–0.4.
- **Moisture formulae:** $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ — must be memorized and applied both ways.
- **Rehydration Ratio (RR)** is THE quality indicator — typically 3–5 for good powders; > 4.5 for premium.
- **Case-hardening** is the #1 defect in hot-air drying — preventable by slow drying or staged drying.
- **Packaging** must be moisture + O₂ + light barrier — aluminium laminates (PET/AL/PE) are gold standard; N<sub>2</sub> flush for carotenoid-rich powders.
- **Storage:** < 25 °C, RH < 60 %, away from light, with desiccant sachet in retail packs.
- **Examples & uses** to remember: Onion (seasoning), Garlic (condiment), Tomato (ketchup/sauce), Spinach (nutraceutical), Moringa (iron supplement), Beetroot (natural colour), Mushroom (umami booster), Carrot (β-carotene), Curry leaves (curry blends).
- **FSSAI / IS** specifications: onion ≤ 5 % moisture, garlic ≤ 5 %, tomato ≤ 4 % — recall the limits.
- **Weight-reduction ratio** of vegetable powders is typically **6–12×** — a 1 kg powder = 6–12 kg fresh equivalent.
- **Maltodextrin** (5–15 % w/w) is the standard carrier for spray-drying tomato, beetroot, and other sticky purees.
- **"Stickiness point"** in spray drying = T<sub>g</sub> + 10–20 °C; powder sticks to chamber wall if inlet air T approaches this.
- **KMS** = potassium metabisulphite; used for retention of colour in carrot, potato, mushroom powders. *Not used for onion/garlic.*
- **MgCO₃ / Mg(OH)₂** blanching dip — fixes chlorophyll in leafy green vegetable powders.
- **Inlet T of spray dryer** = 180–220 °C; **Outlet T** = 80–100 °C. **Residence time** = 5–30 s. **Particle size** = 20–200 µm.
- **Freeze drying** is 8–24 h; **vacuum** < 1 mbar; **shelf T** = −30 to −40 °C. Best for premium moringa, herbal and mushroom powders.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMF Fruit Slices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IMF Fruit Slices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🍎 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF)** are defined as foods that contain sufficient moisture to be palatable and edible in their natural state without rehydration, yet have a water activity ($a_w$) low enough (typically **0.60 – 0.85**) to inhibit the growth of spoilage microorganisms, thereby conferring shelf stability without the need for thermal sterilization or refrigeration.

**IMF Fruit Slices** are a specific class of ready-to-eat convenience products prepared from fresh fruits, which are partially dehydrated (usually by combined **osmotic dehydration** and **air drying**) to an intermediate moisture range of **20 – 50 % (w.b.)**, with a water activity controlled between **0.65 and 0.80**, and stabilized using permitted **humectants** such as **glycerol, sorbitol, propylene glycol, sucrose, and sodium chloride**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Classification Criterion:**
> - **High Moisture Foods (HMF):** $a_w > 0.85$ → perishable, require refrigeration.
> - **Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF):** $0.60 \le a_w \le 0.85$ → shelf-stable, ambient storage.
> - **Low Moisture Foods (LMF):** $a_w < 0.60$ → microbiologically stable, dry, crisp texture.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Fruit Cake" Intuition

Imagine biting into a **traditional Christmas plum cake** 🍰 — it is soft, moist, chewy, sweet, and yet sits on your kitchen shelf for weeks without spoiling. Why? Because the baker added sugar (a humectant) and dried fruits (lowered $a_w$) into a controlled moisture environment — the microorganisms simply cannot grow because there isn't enough *free* water for them, even though the product *feels* moist to your tongue.

**IMF Fruit Slices work on exactly the same principle.** The fruit is sliced, then soaked in a concentrated sugar (or sugar + salt + glycerol) syrup. Water inside the fruit "leaks out" into the syrup (osmosis), and the syrup's solutes "leak in." The result is a slice that is:
- **Soft & pliable** (intermediate moisture) — *not rock-hard like a chip*.
- **Shelf-stable** (low $a_w$) — *no fridge required*.
- **Nutritious & flavorful** (concentrated natural fruit sugars and acids).

Think of IMF fruit slices as the **"Goldilocks zone"** of fruit preservation: not too wet (mouldy), not too dry (chips), but **just right** — moist enough to chew, dry enough to keep.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Range for IMF Fruit Slices |
|---|---|
| Moisture content | **20 – 50 % (w.b.)** |
| Water activity ($a_w$) | **0.65 – 0.80** |
| pH | **3.0 – 4.5** (for most acidic fruits) |
| Brix of osmotic syrup | **50 – 70 °Brix** |
| Storage temperature | **Ambient (≤ 30 °C)** |
| Shelf life | **6 – 12 months** |
| Humectants permitted | Glycerol, sorbitol, propylene glycol, sucrose, glucose, NaCl |
| Standard slice thickness | **5 – 15 mm** |

## 1.4 Why IMF Fruit Slices? — The Industrial Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Relevance:** This product class directly addresses three pillars of consumer convenience foods:
> 1. **Shelf-stability without thermal sterilization** → preserves heat-sensitive nutrients (Vitamin C, anthocyanins).
> 2. **Energy efficiency** → mild air-drying (50–70 °C) rather than hot air (above 100 °C) or freeze-drying.
> 3. **Eating-ready format** → no peeling, no slicing, no syrup to drain — a true "open-and-eat" snack or dessert topping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Science Behind IMF — Water Activity vs. Moisture Content

The fundamental property that governs the safety of IMF is **water activity ($a_w$)**, NOT total moisture content. Two slices can both contain 30 % water, but one may have $a_w = 0.92$ (spoils in days) while the other has $a_w = 0.72$ (stable for a year). The difference lies in **how that water is bound** to the food matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Activity ($a_w$) Definition:**
> $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH \,(\%)}{100}$$
> where $p$ is the partial vapor pressure of water in the food, and $p_0$ is the saturation vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature. $ERH$ = Equilibrium Relative Humidity.

### 2.1.1 The Critical $a_w$ Thresholds (Why 0.60 – 0.85?)

- **$a_w \ge 0.91$:** Almost all bacteria grow (Staphylococcus, Salmonella, Clostridium).
- **$a_w \approx 0.87$:** Most yeasts grow.
- **$a_w \approx 0.80$:** Most molds grow (Aspergillus, Penicillium, Byssochlamys — **the major spoilage threat for IMF**).
- **$a_w \approx 0.70$:** Osmophilic yeasts still grow; molds inhibited.
- **$a_w \le 0.60$:** No microbial growth — but texture is too dry for "intermediate."

**Therefore, IMF fruit slices target $a_w \approx 0.70 - 0.80$**, using humectants and solute binding to keep the moisture *bound* to the matrix.

## 2.2 The Sorption Isotherm — The Map of $a_w$ vs. Moisture

Every IMF product has a characteristic **moisture sorption isotherm (MSI)** — a sigmoidal curve plotting equilibrium moisture content (EMC) against $a_w$ at constant temperature. The shape is critical because it tells the formulator *how much solute is needed to achieve a target $a_w$*.

Key features of an MSI:
- **Region I (Bound water, $a_w < 0.20$):** Strongly adsorbed monolayer; unavailable for reactions.
- **Region II (Multilayer water, $a_w \approx 0.20 - 0.60$):** Still tightly bound.
- **Region III (Free water, $a_w > 0.60$):** Loosely bound; this is where microbial growth becomes possible.

> **KTU Practical Insight:** The BET (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller) and GAB (Guggenheim–Anderson–de Boer) equations are the two standard mathematical models used to fit the sorption isotherm data for IMF fruit systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize these for ESE.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description & Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_w = p/p_0$ | Water activity definition |
| 2 | $M_t = M_0 - (M_0 - M_e) \, e^{-kt}$ | **Page's model** for thin-layer drying kinetics; $M_t$ = moisture at time $t$, $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture, $k$ = drying constant |
| 3 | $MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e} = e^{-kt}$ | Moisture Ratio (dimensionless) used in drying analysis |
| 4 | $WL \,(\%) = \frac{W_0 - W_t}{W_0} \times 100$ | Water Loss during osmotic dehydration |
| 5 | $SG \,(\%) = \frac{S_t - S_0}{S_0} \times 100$ | Solid Gain (solute impregnation) |
| 6 | $WR = \frac{(WL - SG)}{W_0} \times 100$ | Weight Reduction (net mass change) |
| 7 | $m = \frac{M_0 \, m_0 - M_f \, m_f}{m_0}$ | Mass balance for water removal |
| 8 | $a_w = \frac{M}{M_0} \cdot a_{w,0}$ (approx.) | Approximate linear $a_w$–moisture relation for dilute systems |
| 9 | $R_a^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(M_{exp} - M_{pred})^2}{\sum(M_{exp} - \bar{M}_{exp})^2}$ | Goodness of fit (used in isotherm modelling) |
| 10 | $W_s = (1 - M_t) \cdot m$ | Solids mass remaining after drying to moisture $M_t$ |
| 11 | $\Delta a_w = a_{w,0} - a_{w,target}$ | Required $a_w$ reduction (formulation target) |

> **Note on Notation:** $M_0$ = initial moisture (kg water / kg product), $M_t$ = moisture at time $t$, $M_e$ = equilibrium moisture, $W_0$ = initial weight, $S_0, S_t$ = initial and final solids.

## 2.4 Why This Theory Matters in Food Engineering Practice

- **Product Formulation:** The sorption isotherm is the *roadmap* a food technologist uses to design an IMF — pick a target $a_w$ (say 0.75) and read off the required moisture content, then back-calculate humectant dosage.
- **Shelf-life Prediction:** Combined with $a_w$-dependent microbial growth models (e.g., the *Schoolfield* model for $Byssochlamys*), shelf life of an IMF fruit slice at ambient temperature can be predicted.
- **Packaging Design:** A properly formulated IMF slice must be packaged in a moisture-barrier film whose water vapor permeability (WVP) is calculated based on the **permissible moisture gain** before $a_w$ rises above 0.80.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Mass Balance for Osmotic Dehydration of a Fruit Slice

This is the **core KTU numerical** for the IMF fruit slice topic. We will derive the Water Loss, Solid Gain, and Weight Reduction step-by-step.

### Given Data (Typical KTU Problem Setup)
- Initial mass of fruit slice: $W_0 = 100$ g
- Initial moisture content: $M_0 = 85$ % (w.b.) → $M_0^{db} = \frac{85}{15} = 5.667$ kg water / kg dry solids
- Initial solids: $S_0 = 15$ g
- After osmotic treatment (4 h in 60 °Brix sucrose syrup at 50 °C):
- Final mass: $W_t = 65$ g
- Final moisture content: $M_t = 60$ % (w.b.)

### Step 1 — Express Initial and Final Moisture on Dry Basis

$$
M_0^{db} = \frac{85}{100 - 85} = \frac{85}{15} = 5.667 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry solids}
$$

$$
M_t^{db} = \frac{60}{100 - 60} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.500 \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry solids}
$$

### Step 2 — Final Solids Mass ($S_t$)

$$
S_t = W_t \times \left(1 - \frac{M_t}{100}\right) = 65 \times (1 - 0.60) = 65 \times 0.40 = 26.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

### Step 3 — Water Loss (WL)

Water loss is the difference between initial water mass and final water mass.

$$
W_{water,0} = W_0 \times \frac{M_0}{100} = 100 \times 0.85 = 85.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

$$
W_{water,t} = W_t \times \frac{M_t}{100} = 65 \times 0.60 = 39.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

$$
WL = W_{water,0} - W_{water,t} = 85.0 - 39.0 = 46.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

Expressed as a percentage of initial mass:

$$
WL \,(\%) = \frac{46.0}{100} \times 100 = 46.0 \, \%
$$

### Step 4 — Solid Gain (SG)

Solid gain equals the increase in total solids during osmotic treatment.

$$
S_0 = W_0 \times \left(1 - \frac{M_0}{100}\right) = 100 \times 0.15 = 15.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

$$
SG = S_t - S_0 = 26.0 - 15.0 = 11.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

$$
SG \,(\%) = \frac{11.0}{15.0} \times 100 = 73.33 \, \% \;\; \text{(on initial solid basis)}
$$

### Step 5 — Weight Reduction (WR)

$$
WR = WL - SG = 46.0 - 11.0 = 35.0 \;\; \text{g}
$$

$$
WR \,(\%) = \frac{35.0}{100} \times 100 = 35.0 \, \%
$$

### Step 6 — Verification of Mass Balance

$$
W_t = W_0 - WR = 100 - 35 = 65 \;\; \text{g} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> This **5-step numerical** is a classic KTU ESE Part B (a) question (7 marks).

## 3.2 Derivation of Page's Drying Model for the Air-Drying Stage

After osmotic dehydration, the partially dewatered fruit slice is finished in a **convective hot-air dryer** to reach the final IMF moisture (~30 % w.b.). The moisture loss during this stage is described by **Page's equation**.

### Starting Point: Newton's Law of Drying

For thin-layer drying under constant external conditions, the rate of moisture loss is proportional to the difference between current moisture and equilibrium moisture:

$$
-\frac{dM}{dt} = k \, (M_t - M_e)
$$

### Step 1 — Separate Variables

$$
\int_{M_0}^{M_t} \frac{dM}{M - M_e} = -k \int_0^t dt
$$

### Step 2 — Integrate

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}\right) = -kt
$$

### Step 3 — Define Moisture Ratio (MR)

$$
MR = \frac{M_t - M_e}{M_0 - M_e}
$$

### Step 4 — Exponential Form

$$
MR = e^{-kt}
$$

### Step 5 — Empirical Modification (Page's Model)

Page (1949) observed that the simple exponential under-predicted drying at long times for many foods. He modified it with a power-law exponent $n$:

$$
MR = e^{-k \, t^n}
$$

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When $n = 1$, Page's model reduces to the Lewis/Newton model. Typical $n$ values for fruit slices: **0.8 – 1.4**.

### Step 6 — Practical Use

Given experimental $MR$ vs $t$ data, linearize by taking $\ln$ twice:

$$
\ln\!\big(-\ln(MR)\big) = \ln(k) + n \, \ln(t)
$$

A plot of $\ln(-\ln MR)$ vs $\ln t$ gives slope $= n$ and intercept $= \ln k$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Variables vs. Final IMF Quality

| Process Variable | Effect on Water Loss | Effect on Solid Gain | Effect on Final $a_w$ | Effect on Texture |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Syrup Brix (50 → 70 °Bx) | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↑↑ Strong increase | ↓ Strong decrease | Firmer, denser |
| ↑ Temperature (30 → 60 °C) | ↑ Increase | ↑ Slight increase | ↓ Decrease | Softer (cell wall softening) |
| ↑ Immersion time (1 → 6 h) | ↑ (asymptotic) | ↑ (asymptotic) | ↓ (asymptotic) | Plateaus by ~5 h |
| ↑ Slice thickness (5 → 15 mm) | ↓ Decreases per unit mass | ↑ Slight increase | ↑ Increases | Chewy, soft |
| Adding NaCl (0 → 5 %) | ↑↑ Sharp increase | ↑↑ Sharp increase | ↓ Strong decrease | Firmer (cell membrane tightening) |
| Adding glycerol (0 → 5 %) | ↑ Moderate increase | ↑ Moderate increase | ↓↓ Sharp decrease | Pliable, soft (plasticizer) |
| Agitation (0 → 150 rpm) | ↑ Moderate increase | ↑ Slight increase | ↓ Slight decrease | More uniform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Manufacturing of IMF Fruit Slices

The following Mermaid block renders the complete unit-operation flow for industrial production of IMF fruit slices. Each block is a process unit; arrows represent material flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[R1 Ripe Fresh Fruits] --> B[R2 Sorting Grading Inspection]
    B --> C[R3 Washing in Chlorinated Water 100 ppm]
    C --> D[R4 Peeling and Coring Manual or Lye]
    D --> E[R5 Slicing 5 to 15 mm thickness]
    E --> F[R6 Optional Blanching 90 deg C 2 min]
    F --> G[R7 Osmotic Dehydration in 50 to 70 Brix Syrup 50 deg C 4 h]
    G --> H[R8 Surface Draining and Rinse]
    H --> I[R9 Hot Air Drying 50 to 70 deg C to reach 25 to 30 percent moisture]
    I --> J[R10 Humectant Dip Optional Glycerol 3 percent]
    J --> K[R11 Conditioning 25 deg C 24 h moisture equalization]
    K --> L[R12 Quality Check Moisture aw pH Brix Color]
    L --> M[R13 Packaging in Multilayer Moisture Barrier Film]
    M --> N[R14 Storage at Ambient 25 to 30 deg C]
    N --> O[R15 Distribution to Retail Shelf Life 6 to 12 months]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block is a sequential unit operation. The critical stage is **R7 (Osmotic Dehydration)** — this single step contributes ~60–70 % of total water removal, while **R9 (Air Drying)** contributes the remainder and fixes the final $a_w$.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Block Diagram — Interaction Map

This block diagram maps the **interactions between ingredients, process, and final product attributes**. Useful for KTU conceptual questions on "How do humectants, syrup, and drying together achieve the target $a_w$?"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Fruit Substrate] --> D[Osmotic Stage Water Out Solute In]
    B[Syrup Brix 50 to 70] --> D
    C[Humectant Glycerol Sorbitol NaCl] --> E[Air Drying Stage Further Water Removal]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Final Product aw 0.70 to 0.80]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Shelf Stability 6 to 12 months]
    F --> H[Texture Soft Pliable Chewy]
    F --> I[Flavor Concentrated Sweet Acidic]
    F --> J[Nutrition Heat Sensitive Vitamins Retained]
```

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A student should be able to trace *one* parameter (e.g., ↑ glycerol) through this architecture and predict its effect on $a_w$, texture, and shelf life. This is a frequent 3-mark Part A question.

## 4.3 Microbial Inhibition Map — Why $a_w$ 0.70 Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[aw 0.99] --> B[All bacteria grow]
    A2[aw 0.91] --> C[Most bacteria inhibited]
    A3[aw 0.87] --> D[Most yeasts inhibited]
    A4[aw 0.80] --> E[Most molds inhibited]
    A5[aw 0.70] --> F[Osmophilic yeasts and xerophilic molds inhibited]
    A6[aw 0.60] --> G[No microbial growth safe zone]
    H[IMF Fruit Slice Target aw 0.70 to 0.80] --> E
    H --> F
```

> **Quick Takeaway:** The narrow $a_w$ band **0.70 – 0.80** is the *strategic sweet spot* — it suppresses nearly all pathogenic and most spoilage microbes while preserving the *soft, edible texture* that defines an IMF.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF) and state the typical moisture content and water activity range.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Intermediate Moisture Foods (IMF) are foods that contain sufficient moisture (typically **20 – 50 % w.b.**) to be eaten in their natural form without rehydration, yet have a water activity ($a_w$) in the range of **0.60 – 0.85**, making them shelf-stable at ambient temperature without refrigeration or thermal sterilization. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. List any four humectants used in IMF fruit slice formulation and state their primary function.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Glycerol** – binds free water, lowers $a_w$, plasticizer for soft texture.
2. **Sorbitol** – polyol humectant, sugar replacer, reduces $a_w$.
3. **Sucrose** – osmotic agent, water-binding, sweetness enhancer.
4. **Sodium chloride (NaCl)** – osmotic agent, enhances water loss, lowers $a_w$, flavor modifier.
   (3 marks: 1 mark for list + 2 marks for function explanation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

### ✦ OR Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Describe in detail the manufacturing process of IMF mango slices. Include a process flow diagram and explain the role of each unit operation in controlling water activity. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Raw Material Selection (1 mark):** Ripe but firm Totapuri or Alphonso mangoes, TSS ≥ 14 °Brix, low fiber content, intact peel.

**2. Sorting, Washing & Peeling (1 mark):** Manual sorting, washing in chlorinated water (50–100 ppm), hand peeling or lye peeling (2 % NaOH at 80 °C for 2 min).

**3. Slicing (0.5 mark):** Slices of 8–10 mm thickness using a rotary slicer; uniform thickness is critical for uniform osmotic transfer.

**4. Osmotic Dehydration (2 marks):** Immersion in **60 °Brix sucrose syrup at 50 °C for 4 h** with fruit-to-syrup ratio 1:4. This step removes ~50 % of the water (water loss) and impregnates ~10 % solids (sugar gain). Achieves $a_w$ reduction from 0.98 → ~0.85.

**5. Surface Draining and Mild Rinse (0.5 mark):** Removes adhering syrup, prevents surface crystallization.

**6. Air Drying (1.5 marks):** Cabinet/tray dryer at **55–60 °C for 4–6 h** to final moisture of **25–30 % w.b.** and $a_w$ of **0.70–0.78**. Air velocity 1.5 m/s.

**7. Conditioning and Packaging (0.5 mark):** 24 h equilibration in sealed bins at 25 °C; packed in **metallized polyester / LDPE** pouches under nitrogen flush.

---

#### (b) With suitable equations, explain water activity and the role of humectants in lowering $a_w$ of IMF fruit slices. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Water Activity Definition (2 marks):**
$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH(\%)}{100}$$
Pure water has $a_w = 1.00$. Adding solutes reduces the vapor pressure of water above the solution, hence lowering $a_w$. In a fruit slice, sugars, salts, glycerol, and other dissolved solids bind water molecules and reduce their chemical potential.

**2. Microbial Stability Threshold (2 marks):** $a_w \ge 0.91$ supports bacterial growth; $a_w \approx 0.80$ supports mold growth (Aspergillus, Penicillium); $a_w \approx 0.70$ inhibits most molds and yeasts. Hence IMF fruit slices target $a_w = 0.70$–$0.80$.

**3. Role of Humectants (2 marks):** Humectants (glycerol, sorbitol, propylene glycol) are hygroscopic substances that bind free water via hydrogen bonding. The mass of humectant $H$ required to reach target $a_w$ is found from the moisture sorption isotherm (BET/GAB model):
$$M = \frac{M_0 \, C \, K \, a_w}{(1 - K \, a_w)(1 - K \, a_w + C \, K \, a_w)}$$
where $M$ = equilibrium moisture, $C, K$ = GAB constants, $M_0$ = monolayer moisture.

**4. Practical Dosage (1 mark):** Typical formulation — **3–5 % glycerol** + **40–60 % sucrose** in osmotic syrup → final $a_w$ 0.72–0.78.

---

### ✦ OR Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the principle of osmotic dehydration. Derive the equations for Water Loss (WL) and Solid Gain (SG). (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Principle (2 marks):** Osmotic dehydration is the process of immersing a porous food (fruit slice) in a concentrated solution of sugars/salts (hypertonic solution). Two counter-current mass transfer fluxes occur simultaneously:
- **Water moves OUT** of the fruit (high water chemical potential) into the syrup (low water chemical potential).
- **Solutes move IN** from the syrup into the fruit.

The driving force is the **chemical potential gradient** of water across the cell membrane.

**2. Water Loss (WL) Derivation (2.5 marks):**
$$WL \,(\%) = \frac{W_0 \cdot M_0 - W_t \cdot M_t}{W_0} \times 100$$
where $W_0, W_t$ = initial and final mass; $M_0, M_t$ = initial and final moisture (fraction, w.b.).
$WL$ measures the water removed from the product per 100 g initial mass.

**3. Solid Gain (SG) Derivation (2.5 marks):**
$$SG \,(\%) = \frac{S_t - S_0}{S_0} \times 100 = \frac{W_t(1-M_t) - W_0(1-M_0)}{W_0(1-M_0)} \times 100$$
where $S_t, S_0$ = final and initial solids mass.
$SG$ measures the solute impregnated per 100 g initial solids.

> **Memory aid for KTU:** "WL is on water, SG is on solids; both expressed as percentage of their respective initial mass."

---

#### (b) Discuss the various factors affecting osmotic dehydration of fruit slices. Add a note on the quality parameters of finished IMF fruit slices. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Factors Affecting Osmotic Dehydration (4 marks):**
1. **Concentration of osmotic solution** — ↑ Brix (50→70 °Bx) ⇒ ↑ WL & ↑ SG.
2. **Temperature** — ↑ T (30→60 °C) ⇒ ↑ WL; cell membrane becomes more permeable.
3. **Immersion time** — WL and SG follow asymptotic behavior; most gain occurs in first 2 h.
4. **Fruit-to-syrup ratio** — higher ratio (more syrup per fruit) ensures concentration does not change much.
5. **Agitation** — gentle agitation (50–100 rpm) increases mass transfer rate by reducing external film resistance.
6. **Slice geometry** — smaller thickness & larger surface area ⇒ faster dehydration.
7. **Type of solute** — sucrose vs. glucose vs. NaCl — different molecular sizes, different rates.

**Quality Parameters of IMF Fruit Slices (3 marks):**
1. **Moisture content** — 20–50 % w.b. (target ~30 %).
2. **Water activity ($a_w$)** — 0.70–0.80.
3. **pH** — 3.0–4.5 (acidic, inhibits pathogens).
4. **Color** — bright, characteristic of fresh fruit (L*, a*, b* measurement).
5. **Texture** — soft, pliable, non-sticky; measured by TPA (Texture Profile Analysis).
6. **Brix** — high, indicative of sugar concentration.
7. **Microbial counts** — TPC < 10⁴ CFU/g; yeast/mold < 10² CFU/g.
8. **Sensory score** — overall acceptability ≥ 7 on 9-point hedonic scale.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Conflating moisture content with water activity** — they are *not* the same. Always state $a_w$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems (g vs. kg, % w.b. vs. % d.b.). Always write the unit.
> 3. **Writing WL/SG as % of total mass** instead of % of *initial water/solids* — examiners deduct.
> 4. **Omitting the mass balance verification step** — always check $W_t = W_0 - WR$ at the end.
> 5. **Confusing "intermediate moisture" with "partially dried"** — IMF is a *formulation concept*, not just drying.
> 6. **Listing humectants without explaining their mechanism** (water binding, $a_w$ reduction, plasticization) — half-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **IMF = Moisture 20–50 % w.b. + $a_w$ 0.60–0.85** → shelf-stable, ambient storage.
- ⭐ **$a_w$ governs safety**, not total moisture — the only true "preservation" parameter.
- ⭐ **Three pillars of IMF technology:** *reduced $a_w$* + *controlled pH* + *microbial hurdles (antimicrobials like sorbate/benzoate if needed)*.
- ⭐ **Two-step dehydration:** Osmotic (primary, low energy) → Air drying (final moisture, final $a_w$).
- ⭐ **Key formulas:** $WL$, $SG$, $WR$, $MR$, Page's model $MR = e^{-k t^n}$ — must be memorized.
- ⭐ **Humectants:** Glycerol (3–5 %), sorbitol, sucrose, NaCl, propylene glycol.
- ⭐ **Molds are the main enemy** at $a_w$ 0.80 (Aspergillus, Penicillium, Byssochlamys) — target $a_w \le 0.78$ for safety margin.
- ⭐ **Sorption isotherm (BET/GAB model)** is the design tool for $a_w$–moisture relationship.
- ⭐ **Packaging:** moisture-barrier film (metallized PET/LDPE) is mandatory; nitrogen flush extends shelf life.
- ⭐ **Quality check trio:** Moisture, $a_w$, pH — measured in every batch.
- ⭐ **Common exam fruits:** Mango, pineapple, papaya, banana, apple, guava, jackfruit, kiwi.
- ⭐ **Industrial applications:** Ready-to-eat snacks, breakfast cereal toppings, bakery inclusions, ice-cream mix-ins, confectionery.
- ⭐ **Advantage over HMF:** No refrigeration, no thermal sterilization, no canning — minimal nutrient loss.
- ⭐ **Advantage over LMF (dried):** Soft, edible texture — no rehydration step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMF Fruit Bars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IMF Fruit Bars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IMF (Intermediate Moisture Food)?

An **Intermediate Moisture Food (IMF)** is a food product that contains moisture at a level intermediate between that of a fresh (high-moisture) food and a fully dried (low-moisture) food. IMFs are characterized by a **water activity ($a_w$) in the range of 0.60 – 0.90** and moisture content generally between **15% – 40%** (wet basis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Codex Alimentarius / FSSAI Aligned):**
> "IMF Fruit Bars are shelf-stable fruit-based products obtained by concentrating fruit pulp/juice with approved humectants (sugars, glycerol, sorbitol), acidulants, and pectin, followed by drying to a plastic/chewey consistency with $a_w$ controlled between 0.60 – 0.80, thereby preventing microbial spoilage without requiring refrigeration or thermal sterilization."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍫🍓

Imagine you have a fresh strawberry (water activity ≈ 0.99 — spoils in days) and a fully dried strawberry chip (water activity ≈ 0.30 — hard and brittle). Now imagine something **in between** — a chewy, pliable strip that tastes intensely fruity, doesn't need a fridge, yet is soft enough to bite into without cracking a tooth. That "Goldilocks zone" of moisture is what an **IMF fruit bar** occupies.

> **Key Visual Mnemonic:**
> Fresh Fruit (wet, $a_w$ ≈ 0.99) ←→ **IMF Fruit Bar (pliable, $a_w$ ≈ 0.70)** ←→ Dried Fruit (hard, $a_w$ ≈ 0.30)

## 1.3 Why Are IMF Fruit Bars Important in Food Engineering?

| Parameter | Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Water activity ($a_w$) | **0.60 – 0.80** |
| Moisture content (w.b.) | **15% – 30%** |
| Total Soluble Solids (TSS) | **65° – 75° Brix** |
| Sugar-Acid ratio | **20 : 1 to 40 : 1** |
| pH | **3.0 – 4.0** |
| Texture | Plastic, chewy, leathery |
| Shelf-life (unrefrigerated) | **6 – 12 months** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fruit bars are commercially known under several names — *fruit leathers*, *fruit rolls*, *fruit sheets*, *papitas* (in South American trade), and *am-papad* (in India). The standard commercial term preferred in the industry is **"Fruit Leather"** when thin and rollable, and **"Fruit Bar"** when cut into thick rectangular/square pieces.

## 1.4 Classification of IMF Fruit Bars

1. **Based on Single vs. Multiple Fruits**
   - **Mono-fruit bars** — e.g., mango bar, guava bar, pineapple bar.
   - **Mixed/poly-fruit bars** — e.g., mango-papaya, apple-berry blends.

2. **Based on Texture / Thickness**
   - **Thin leather (1–3 mm)** — flexible, rollable, marketed as "roll-ups."
   - **Thick bar (5–15 mm)** — chewy, sliceable, marketed as "energy/snack bars."

3. **Based on Sweetener System**
   - **Sucrose-based** (traditional).
   - **Invert sugar / glucose syrup-based** (anti-crystallization).
   - **Polyol / humectant-based** (sorbitol, glycerol) — for diabetic or "no-added-sugar" variants.

4. **Based on Processing Method**
   - **Solar-dried bars** (rural/traditional).
   - **Cabinet / tray-dried bars** (industrial).
   - **Drum-dried bars** (continuous industrial).
   - **Foam-mat dried bars** (modern, fast reconstitution).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### A. Water Activity ($a_w$) — The Master Variable

The single most critical parameter governing the safety and stability of an IMF fruit bar is **water activity**, not merely moisture content.

$$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$

Where:
- $p$ = partial vapor pressure of water in the food
- $p_0$ = saturated vapor pressure of pure water at same temperature
- $ERH$ = Equilibrium Relative Humidity (%)

**Microbial Inhibition Thresholds (must memorize for KTU):**

| Organism Group | Minimum $a_w$ for Growth |
|---|---|
| Most bacteria | **0.91** |
| Most yeasts | **0.88** |
| Most moulds | **0.80** |
| *Aspergillus glaucus* (xerophilic mould) | **0.70** |
| Osmophilic yeasts | **0.65** |
| No microbial growth possible | **< 0.60** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic:** This is why a target $a_w$ of **0.70 – 0.75** is chosen for fruit bars — it is **below the threshold for all bacteria, yeasts, and most moulds**, yet **high enough to maintain pliability and a soft bite** (avoiding the brittleness of true dehydration).

### B. Humectants and Water Binding

The "intermediate" moisture status is actively maintained by **humectants** — ingredients that hydrogen-bond with free water and reduce its escaping tendency:

- **Sugars** (sucrose, glucose, fructose) — primary humectants
- **Polyols** (sorbitol, glycerol, mannitol) — secondary humectants
- **Salt** (NaCl) — tertiary, used in savory variants

The moisture sorption isotherm of a fruit bar follows a **Type II BET isotherm** with a steep rise in the $a_w$ range of 0.65–0.85, indicating that small changes in moisture cause large $a_w$ shifts — hence the **critical control requirement** during drying.

### C. Role of Pectin and Acidulants

- **Pectin** (high-methoxyl, HM, DE > 50%) forms a **three-dimensional gel network** in the presence of sugar and acid. This gel is the structural skeleton of the bar.
- **Acidulants** (citric acid, malic acid, lactic acid) lower pH to **3.0 – 3.5**, ensuring:
  1. Pectin gelation (HM-pectin needs pH < 3.5)
  2. Microbial suppression
  3. Color stability (prevents enzymatic browning)
  4. Flavor balance

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 🧮

| # | Formula / Equation | Application / Variable Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_w = \dfrac{p}{p_0} = \dfrac{ERH(\%)}{100}$ | Water activity (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis), % |
| 3 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis), % |
| 4 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}/100}$ | Conversion wb ↔ db |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{MC_{initial,db} - MC_{final,db}}{t}$ | Drying rate, kg water / kg dry solid / h |
| 6 | $SSR = \dfrac{\%\text{Sugar (TSS)}}{\%\text{Titratable Acid}}$ | Sugar : Acid ratio |
| 7 | $TSS_{blend} = \dfrac{\sum (TSS_i \times m_i)}{\sum m_i}$ | TSS of mixed fruit pulp blend (°Brix) |
| 8 | $m_{final} = m_{initial} \times \dfrac{1 - MC_{i,wb}}{1 - MC_{f,wb}}$ | Mass balance — final bar mass from initial pulp |
| 9 | $W_{removed} = m_{initial} \times \left(\dfrac{MC_i - MC_f}{100 - MC_f}\right)$ | Water to evaporate (kg water/kg initial) |
| 10 | $Y_{product} = \dfrac{m_{bar}}{m_{pulp,initial}}$ | Process yield (decimal or %) |
| 11 | $a_w \approx 1 - \dfrac{X_m \cdot C \cdot a_w}{(1-a_w)(1-a_w + C \cdot a_w)}$ | BET equation (linearized form) |
| 12 | $\text{Sorbitol eq.} = \%\text{Sorbitol} \times 0.53$ | Sweetness equivalent vs. sucrose |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** When calculating the final mass of the bar or the water to evaporate, ALWAYS use the **wet-basis (w.b.)** moisture definition unless explicitly asked. Marks are frequently lost on this unit conversion.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

IMF fruit bar technology is a **microcosm of hurdle technology** — combining multiple preservation hurdles (low $a_w$, low pH, high sugar, preservatives) to achieve a safe, shelf-stable, ambient-stored product. The same principles underlie the manufacture of:

- **Dried pet food** (kibble)
- **Ready-to-eat (RTE) meat jerky** (using salt, smoke, drying)
- **Dried soup mixes** and **instant noodles**
- **Bakery fillings** (sugar-based, $a_w$ controlled)

A food engineer who masters fruit bar formulation can transition into **confectionery**, **dried bakery**, **pet food**, and **functional food R&D** industries seamlessly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mass Balance for Fruit Bar Production — Full Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Question Type):**
*100 kg of mango pulp at 18% moisture (w.b.) and 14° Brix is fortified with 25 kg of sugar, 2 kg of pectin, 1 kg of citric acid, 1.5 kg of glycerol, and 0.5 kg of potassium sorbate. After drying, the final fruit bar should have 20% moisture (w.b.) and $a_w$ ≈ 0.72. Calculate: (i) Total mass of ingredients; (ii) Mass of water to be evaporated; (iii) Final mass of fruit bar; (iv) Final TSS; (v) Process yield.*

---

### Step 1: Establish Initial Mass Inventory

Let:
- $m_{pulp} = 100 \text{ kg}$
- $MC_{pulp,wb} = 18\%$
- $TSS_{pulp} = 14°\text{ Brix}$

Mass of water in pulp:
$$m_{w,pulp} = m_{pulp} \times \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100} = 100 \times 0.18 = 18.0 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of total solids (TS) in pulp:
$$m_{s,pulp} = 100 - 18.0 = 82.0 \text{ kg}$$

TSS contribution from pulp (soluble solids):
$$m_{TSS,pulp} = m_{pulp} \times \dfrac{°Brix}{100} = 100 \times 0.14 = 14.0 \text{ kg}$$

(Insoluble solids in pulp = $82.0 - 14.0 = 68.0$ kg, e.g., fiber, insoluble polysaccharides.)

---

### Step 2: Total Mass of All Ingredients (Pre-drying)

$$m_{total,initial} = m_{pulp} + m_{sugar} + m_{pectin} + m_{citric} + m_{glycerol} + m_{sorbate}$$

$$m_{total,initial} = 100 + 25 + 2 + 1 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 130.0 \text{ kg}$$

---

### Step 3: Total Water Inventory (Pre-drying)

Pulp water = 18.0 kg. Sugar, pectin, citric acid, sorbate contain negligible water (assume 0.5% each, standard industrial assumption):

- Sugar water ≈ $25 \times 0.005 = 0.125$ kg
- Pectin water ≈ $2 \times 0.005 = 0.010$ kg
- Citric acid water ≈ $1 \times 0.005 = 0.005$ kg
- Glycerol water ≈ $1.5 \times 0.010 = 0.015$ kg
- Sorbate water ≈ $0.5 \times 0.005 = 0.0025$ kg
- **Subtotal added water ≈ 0.16 kg**

$$m_{w,total,initial} = 18.0 + 0.16 = 18.16 \text{ kg}$$

Total solids in initial mix:
$$m_{s,total,initial} = 130.0 - 18.16 = 111.84 \text{ kg}$$

Initial moisture content of blend (w.b.):
$$MC_{initial,wb} = \dfrac{18.16}{130.0} \times 100 = 13.97\%$$

---

### Step 4: Final Mass of Fruit Bar (after drying to 20% w.b.)

**Key Principle:** Solids are conserved during drying (no solids lost except possibly volatile flavor compounds, which we ignore).

$$m_{s,total,initial} = m_{s,total,final} = 111.84 \text{ kg}$$

In the final bar, solids are $(100 - 20) = 80\%$ of total mass:

$$m_{bar,final} \times (1 - 0.20) = 111.84$$

$$m_{bar,final} = \dfrac{111.84}{0.80} = 139.8 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: Final mass *increases* in formulation when ingredients carry mass but drying only removes water proportionally. We can also use the direct formula:
> $$m_{final} = m_{initial} \times \dfrac{1 - MC_{i,wb}}{1 - MC_{f,wb}} = 130.0 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.1397}{1 - 0.20} = 130.0 \times 1.0754 = 139.8 \text{ kg}$$ ✓

---

### Step 5: Water to be Evaporated

$$W_{removed} = m_{initial} - m_{final} = 130.0 - 139.8 \text{ kg} \;\; \text{(negative — recheck!)}$$

The negative sign above reveals a classic student error: drying REMOVES water, so the final mass must be LESS than initial. Our blend is already at 14% moisture w.b. — well below the target 20% w.b. This means we need to **ADD water**, not evaporate it.

**Realistic Reformulation:** Industrial pulp is typically at **80% moisture w.b.** (i.e., 20% TS, 14° Brix). Let us re-do the calculation correctly.

**Re-do with realistic pulp at 80% moisture:**

- $m_{w,pulp} = 100 \times 0.80 = 80.0$ kg
- $m_{s,pulp} = 20.0$ kg
- Added sugar: 25 kg, water negligible → 25 kg solids
- Pectin: 2 kg solids
- Citric acid: 1 kg solids
- Glycerol: 1.5 kg solids
- Sorbate: 0.5 kg solids
- **Total solids in blend = $20 + 25 + 2 + 1 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 50.0$ kg**
- **Total initial mass = $100 + 25 + 2 + 1 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 130.0$ kg**
- **Total initial water = $130.0 - 50.0 = 80.0$ kg**

$$MC_{initial,wb} = \dfrac{80.0}{130.0} \times 100 = 61.54\%$$

$$m_{bar,final} = 130.0 \times \dfrac{1 - 0.6154}{1 - 0.20} = 130.0 \times \dfrac{0.3846}{0.80} = 130.0 \times 0.4808 = 62.5 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{removed} = 130.0 - 62.5 = 67.5 \text{ kg of water to evaporate}$$

---

### Step 6: Final TSS (°Brix) Calculation

Initial TSS in blend:
- From pulp: 14.0 kg
- From sugar: 25.0 kg
- From pectin: 2.0 kg (soluble)
- From citric acid: 1.0 kg
- Total soluble solids in blend ≈ $14.0 + 25.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 42.0$ kg

(Note: glycerol and sorbate contribute some but are minor.)

$$TSS_{final,blend} = \dfrac{m_{soluble}}{m_{final}} \times 100 = \dfrac{42.0}{62.5} \times 100 = 67.2°\text{ Brix}$$

This is well within the target **65° – 75° Brix** range for IMF fruit bars. ✓

---

### Step 7: Process Yield

$$Y_{product} = \dfrac{m_{bar,final}}{m_{pulp,initial}} = \dfrac{62.5}{100} = 0.625 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 62.5\%$$

This means **62.5 kg of finished bar from 100 kg of fresh pulp** — typical industrial range is **50–70%**, so this is realistic. ✓

---

### Step 8: Sugar-Acid Ratio

$$\%\text{Sugar (TSS)} = 67.2\%$$
$$\%\text{Acid (as citric)} \approx 1.0 / 62.5 \times 100 = 1.6\%$$

$$SSR = \dfrac{67.2}{1.6} = 42.0$$

This is at the high end of the standard range (20–40), indicating a sweet-bar profile. To reduce sweetness, increase citric acid to 2 kg → acid = $2/62.5 \times 100 = 3.2\%$, giving $SSR = 67.2/3.2 = 21.0$ (a more balanced, tangy bar).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Ingredient Variation

| Variable | Increase Effect on Bar | Decrease Effect on Bar |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Sugar | ↑ TSS, ↑ $a_w$ depression, ↑ sweetness, ↓ tartness, possible crystallization | ↓ sweetness, ↑ sourness, possible microbial risk |
| ↑ Pectin | ↑ gel strength, ↓ stickiness, ↑ chewiness | ↑ softness, ↑ sticky surface, may slump during drying |
| ↑ Citric acid | ↓ pH, ↑ tartness, ↑ microbial safety, ↑ color brightness | ↑ pH, ↓ tartness, browning risk ↑ |
| ↑ Glycerol | ↑ plasticity, ↓ crystallization, ↓ $a_w$ | ↓ softness, ↑ brittleness, ↑ $a_w$ |
| ↑ Drying temp | ↓ drying time, ↑ Maillard browning risk, ↑ carmelization | ↓ browning, ↑ drying time, ↑ microbial risk if incomplete |
| ↑ Drying time | ↓ $MC$, ↓ $a_w$, ↑ shelf-life, ↑ case-hardening risk | ↑ $MC$, ↑ $a_w$, ↓ shelf-life |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Process Flow — IMF Fruit Bar Manufacturing (Industrial)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Fruit Receipt & Grading] --> B[Sorting, Washing & Peeling]
    B --> C[Pulping / Juicing / Milling]
    C --> D[Pasteurization 85C for 5 min]
    D --> E[Filtration / Refining]
    E --> F[Concentration by Evaporation to 30-40 Brix]

    J --> K[Cooling & Conditioning: 25C, 60 percent RH for 4-6 h]
    K --> L[Cutting / Slitting into bars or rolls]
    L --> M[Packaging: Aluminum laminate or metallized BOPP under N2]
    M --> N[Quality Check: aw MC TSS Color Texture]
    N --> O[Storage at Ambient 25-30C]
```
