<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Similar Model]**
**Define water activity ($a_w$). Explain its role in determining the shelf life of convenience foods with examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Water activity ($a_w$) is defined as the ratio of the partial vapor pressure of water in a food matrix ($p$) to the saturated vapor pressure of pure water ($p_0$) at the same temperature.
>
> $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$
>
> **Role in Shelf Life (1.5 marks):** Microbial growth, enzymatic activity, and non-enzymatic reactions (Maillard, lipid oxidation) are all strongly correlated with $a_w$. Reducing $a_w$ below critical thresholds (bacteria < 0.91, yeasts < 0.88, molds < 0.80) extends shelf life. Example: Spray-dried milk powder ($a_w$ ≈ 0.20) has 12–18 months shelf life, while fresh milk ($a_w$ ≈ 0.99) lasts only days.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Model]**
**What is the D-value in thermal processing of convenience foods? What does a 12-D process signify?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **D-value (1.5 marks):** The D-value (decimal reduction time) is the time, at a specified temperature, required to reduce the microbial population by 90% (1-log cycle). It is the reciprocal of the inactivation rate constant $k$, scaled by $\ln 10$.
>
> $$D = \frac{2.303}{k} \quad \text{(minutes)}$$
>
> **12-D process (1.5 marks):** A 12-D process ensures a 12-log reduction of the most heat-resistant *Clostridium botulinum* spores — the standard for low-acid canned foods. It guarantees the probability of a single surviving spore is < $10^{-12}$, providing commercial sterility and Botulism safety.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**A) (i)** Describe the **industrial production of instant noodles** with a flow diagram. Discuss the role of alkaline salts (kansui) and the quality parameters of the finished product. **(7 marks)**

**(ii)** Explain the **principles of extrusion cooking** as a unit operation in convenience food production. Discuss SME, expansion ratio, and product characteristics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**A(i) Industrial Production of Instant Noodles (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 – Dough Preparation (1 mark):** Wheat flour, salt, water, and alkaline agent (kansui: 0.3–0.5% Na₂CO₃ + K₂CO₃) are mixed in a high-speed mixer for 10–15 min. The alkaline environment develops a characteristic yellow color, firm texture, and unique flavor (helps convert flavones to flavins).

**Step 2 – Sheet Rolling (1 mark):** The dough passes through progressive roller pairs, reducing thickness from ~10 mm to 1.0–1.4 mm with controlled dough sheet orientation (perpendicular folding) for uniform gluten network.

**Step 3 – Steaming (1 mark):** The sheet is steam-cooked at 100 °C for 1.5–2 min to gelatinize starch (~70% gelatinization) and pre-hydrate the protein matrix.

**Step 4 – Slitting & Crimping (1 mark):** The cooked sheet is cut into noodle strands (1.2–1.5 mm × 0.6 mm) and crimped/waved for the iconic "instant" shape that aids rehydration.

**Step 5 – Frying (1.5 marks):** Deep-frying in palm oil at 140–160 °C for 1–2 min reduces moisture to < 8% and $a_w$ to < 0.65, creating a porous internal structure. Oil uptake is controlled at 18–22%.

**Step 6 – Cooling and Packing (1.5 marks):** Noodles are cooled, packed with a separate soup sachet (dehydrated vegetables, seasonings, oil) typically in polypropylene/polyethylene laminate pouches.

**Quality Parameters (extra credit):** Rehydration time ≤ 3 min, color, flavor, oil content, and absence of off-flavors (rancidity).

**A(ii) Principles of Extrusion Cooking (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 – Definition (1 mark):** Extrusion cooking is a high-temperature, short-time (HTST) process in which moistened, starchy, and/or proteinaceous feed is forced through a die under high pressure and shear, causing starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and structural expansion.

**Step 2 – Equipment (1 mark):** A single-screw or twin-screw extruder consists of a barrel, screw(s), preconditioner, and die. Twin-screw extruders allow independent control of shear and residence time.

**Step 3 – Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) (2 marks):**

$$SME = \frac{\tau \cdot \omega}{m} \quad \text{(kJ/kg)}$$

Where $\tau$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $m$ = mass flow rate. SME controls degree of starch gelatinization and molecular degradation (dextrinization). Higher SME → more degraded starch → higher expansion.

**Step 4 – Expansion Ratio (ER) (1.5 marks):**

$$ER = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$$

Typical ER for breakfast cereals: 4–8; for puffed snacks: 6–12. Higher ER implies lighter, crunchier texture.

**Step 5 – Product Characteristics (1.5 marks):** Extrusion creates porous, low-density, crispy products with extended shelf life (low $a_w$), high digestibility, and the ability to incorporate vitamins, flavors, and colors post-extrusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**B) (i)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **production of UHT milk**. Discuss aseptic packaging and the F₀ concept. **(7 marks)**

**(ii)** Explain the **Arrhenius model and Q₁₀ principle** for predicting shelf life of convenience foods. Compute the shelf life at 25 °C if a product shows 30% quality loss in 45 days at 40 °C, with $Q_{10} = 2.5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**B(i) Production of UHT Milk (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 – Raw Milk Reception (0.5 mark):** Milk is tested for antibiotics, somatic cell count, and bacterial load. Accepted milk is standardized to 3.5% fat and 8.5% SNF.

**Step 2 – Preheating & Homogenization (1 mark):** Milk is preheated to 75 °C, then homogenized at 15–20 MPa in a two-stage homogenizer to prevent creaming.

**Step 3 – UHT Treatment (2 marks):** Indirect or direct heating achieves 135–150 °C for 2–5 s. The D-value of thermophilic spores is significantly reduced. The **F₀** (sterilization value) is calculated as:

$$F_0 = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t) - 121.1) / Z} \, dt$$

For UHT milk, $F_0$ is typically 5–9 min with $Z = 10$ °C, ensuring commercial sterility.

**Step 4 – Cooling (0.5 mark):** Milk is rapidly cooled to 20 °C.

**Step 5 – Aseptic Packaging (2 marks):** UHT-treated milk is filled into pre-sterilized (H₂O₂ or UV-sterilized) Tetra Brik/PE/foil cartons in a sterile environment (HEPA-filtered air, ISO 7 cleanroom). Packaging machines (e.g., Tetra Pak) sterilize the carton, fill, and seal in a single integrated aseptic zone.

**Step 6 – Storage (1 mark):** Stored at ambient temperature (25–30 °C) for 6 months, with $a_w$ ≈ 0.97 but the product is shelf-stable due to absence of viable microorganisms.

**B(ii) Arrhenius Model & Q₁₀ (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 – Arrhenius Equation (2 marks):**

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$$

Where $k$ = rate of quality deterioration, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

**Step 2 – Q₁₀ Definition (1.5 marks):** The factor by which reaction rate changes for a 10 °C rise in temperature:

$$Q_{10} = \frac{k_{(T+10)}}{k_T}$$

For most foods, $Q_{10}$ is 2–3. For lipid oxidation, $Q_{10}$ ≈ 2.5; for non-enzymatic browning, $Q_{10}$ ≈ 3.

**Step 3 – Calculation (3.5 marks):**

Given: $Q_{10} = 2.5$, 30% quality loss in 45 days at 40 °C, find shelf life at 25 °C (ΔT = 15 °C).

Rate ratio between 40 °C and 25 °C:

$$Q_{15} = Q_{10}^{1.5} = (2.5)^{1.5}$$

$$(2.5)^{1.5} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{2.5} = 2.5 \times 1.5811 = 3.953$$

So the deterioration rate at 40 °C is **3.953 times** faster than at 25 °C. Therefore:

$$\text{Shelf life at 25°C} = 45 \text{ days} \times 3.953 = 177.9 \text{ days} \approx 178 \text{ days}$$

(Equivalent to ~5.9 months at 25 °C.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–4 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D$ and $Z$ values** — $D$ is in **time (min)** at a fixed temperature; $Z$ is in **temperature (°C)** for a 10-fold change in D.
> 2. **Using °C instead of K in Arrhenius** — Always convert to Kelvin: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
> 3. **Forgetting the food's $a_w$ window** — Baking chocolate ($a_w$ ≈ 0.35) is not "wet," hence mold-proof.
> 4. **Ignoring F₀ units** — It is in **minutes**, not dimensionless.
> 5. **Neglecting aseptic zone** in UHT — Aseptic packaging is the *heart* of UHT technology; mentioning sterile air/HEPA is mandatory.
> 6. **Mis-defining SME** — SME is energy per unit mass (kJ/kg), not torque or power.
> 7. **In extrusion, ignoring moisture effect** — Increasing feed moisture (from 15% → 30%) drastically reduces SME and expansion ratio.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Production**

- **Production** = Combination of unit operations to convert raw agri-commodities into shelf-stable convenience foods.
- **4-S Rule:** Safety, Stability, Sensory, Shelf-life (+ Sustainability).
- **Water activity ($a_w$)** = $p / p_0$; control it to control shelf life.
- **D-value** = time for 1-log microbial kill at constant $T$.
- **Z-value** = °C change for 10-fold change in D.
- **F₀ (sterilization value)** = equivalent minutes at 121.1 °C; required F₀ ≥ 3 for low-acid canned foods.
- **12-D principle** for *C. botulinum* in canned foods.
- **Arrhenius equation** and **Q₁₀ rule** are used to predict shelf life from accelerated studies.
- **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3** for most reactions in food.
- **SME** in extrusion = $\tau \omega / m$ (kJ/kg); controls expansion and texture.
- **Expansion ratio (ER)** = $D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$; typical 4–8 for cereals, 6–12 for snacks.
- **Instant noodle frying:** 140–160 °C, oil uptake 18–22%, $a_w$ < 0.65.
- **UHT:** 138 °C / 4 s with aseptic packaging → 6 months ambient storage.
- **Spray drying:** Inlet ~180 °C, outlet ~80 °C, final $a_w$ < 0.20 for milk powder.
- **Pasteurization (HTST):** 72 °C / 15 s for milk.
- **Aseptic packaging** uses H₂O₂/UV sterilization, HEPA-filtered air, ISO 7 cleanroom.
- **Freezing:** Blast freezing at –35 °C for RTC products; storage at –18 °C.
- **Critical control points:** $a_w$, moisture, $T$, pH, F₀, packaging integrity.
- **HTST** = High-Temperature Short-Time; **UHT** = Ultra-High Temperature.
- **Kansui** = alkaline salt blend (Na₂CO₃ + K₂CO₃) that gives noodles their yellow color and firm bite.
- **MAP** = Modified Atmosphere Packaging (usually N₂ + CO₂) for bakery, snacks, RTE meals.
- **Lewis drying model:** $MR = e^{-k_d t}$, valid for thin-layer drying.
- **Activation energies** of food spoilage reactions: 50–200 kJ/mol.
- **Moisture content target for stable powders:** < 4% (dairy), < 1% (freeze-dried coffee).
- **Single-screw vs. twin-screw extruder:** Twin-screw allows better mixing, higher SME control, and self-wiping.
- **End consumer receives** a product that satisfies **safety, nutrition, taste, and convenience** — the goal of every production engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Disadvantages. Ready to Eat Foods Available in India. Marketing and Future Prospects. Extruded Foods - Principle of Extruders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Disadvantages. Ready to Eat Foods Available in India. Marketing and Future Prospects. Extruded Foods - Principle of Extruders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ready-to-Eat (RTE) Foods — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (FSSAI Aligned)**
> *Ready-to-Eat (RTE) foods* are *any preparation (including processed and partially processed formulations) of food, dish, or meal that is intended to be consumed without further preparation or processing by the consumer, requiring only minimal heating or reheating to temperatures appropriate for the specific food product, prior to consumption.* They are pre-cleaned, pre-cooked, packaged, and often shelf-stable at ambient conditions.

**Sub-classes of Convenience Foods in the KTU context:**
1. **RTE Foods** — Direct consumption (e.g., instant noodles, canned rasam).
2. **Ready-to-Cook (RTC) Foods** — Require only thermal processing (e.g., frozen parathas, idli batter).
3. **Ready-to-Serve (RTS) Foods** — Pre-portioned, only need plating/heating (e.g., airline meals).
4. **Mix-to-Cook (MTC) Foods** — Powder/liquid mixes needing addition of water/milk (e.g., cake mix).

## 1.2 Extruded Foods — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extrusion** is a *thermomechanical process* in which food material is forced to flow under one or more varied conditions of mixing, heating, and shear, through a *die* of defined shape and size, to produce a product of uniform cross-section and desired shape, texture, and density. The device used is called an **Extruder**.

The two main industrial extruders are:
- **Single-Screw Extruder** — Used for low-shear, simple products (pellets, expanded snacks).
- **Twin-Screw Extruder** — Co-rotating / Counter-rotating; used for high-shear, complex formulations (breakfast cereals, baby foods, texturized vegetable protein).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **RTE Foods** | A *hygienically sealed, pre-cooked tiffin box* delivered from a restaurant — you just open the lid, heat for 2 minutes in a microwave, and eat. The cooking, spicing, and preservation are already done. |
| **Extrusion** | Imagine a giant, heated **stainless-steel syringe with a screw piston** (like a medical syringe but rotated). A semi-doughy food paste is pushed in. Inside, the screw *twists, squeezes, and heats* the material. When it exits through the *die* (the nozzle), the sudden pressure drop causes the moisture to flash-vaporize, *popping* the product into a porous, crunchy snack — just like a *cream gun* or *Play-Doh Fun Factory*. |

## 1.4 Standard Physical & Process Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Constants and Reference Values Used in Extrusion Calculations:**
> - **Standard Barrel Length-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D) for food extruders:** 20 : 1 to 30 : 1
> - **Typical Screw Speed (N):** 100 – 500 rpm
> - **Barrel Temperature Range:** 100 °C – 200 °C
> - **Die Pressure:** 20 – 200 bar (high-pressure extrusion)
> - **SME (Specific Mechanical Energy):** 100 – 500 Wh/kg
> - **Feed Moisture Content:** 12 % – 28 % (w.b.)
> - **Residence Time in Barrel:** 15 – 60 seconds

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Advantages of RTE / Convenience Foods

> [!NOTE]
> A *3-mark* direct question on advantages is **very common** in KTU ESE. Memorize at least **6 distinct advantages** with **keyword triggers**.

1. **Time-Saving & Convenience** — Reduces cooking time from 30–60 minutes to 2–5 minutes. Targets the working urban population and nuclear families.
2. **Consistent Quality** — Industrial processing under HACCP/ISO 22000 ensures uniform taste, texture, and nutrition in every batch.
3. **Extended Shelf Life** — Aseptic packaging, retort processing, and moisture control allow storage of 6–12 months at ambient temperature.
4. **Portion Control & Hygiene** — Pre-measured servings reduce food waste and contamination risk.
5. **Energy Conservation** — Removes the need for fuel (LPG) at consumer end — critical in modern apartments.
6. **Wide Product Diversity** — Indian cuisine adapted to RTE format (ready meals, curries, biryanis, snacks).
7. **Reduced Post-Harvest Losses** — Converts perishable farm produce into stable, storable value-added products.
8. **Employment & Export Revenue** — Boosts food processing sector, which contributes ~**11 %** to Indian manufacturing GVA.

## 2.2 Disadvantages of RTE / Convenience Foods

1. **Nutritional Degradation** — Heat-labile vitamins (Vitamin C, Thiamine B1, Folic acid) are lost during thermal processing.
2. **High Cost of Production** — Capital investment in retorts, pouch fillers, and aseptic lines.
3. **Use of Chemical Preservatives / Additives** — Class-II preservatives, antioxidants, and emulsifiers may raise consumer health concerns.
4. **Less Palatability vs. Fresh Food** — Sensory acceptability is generally lower than freshly home-cooked food.
5. **Packaging Waste** — Multi-layer laminates (PET/Al/PE) are non-biodegradable and create landfill burden.
6. **Loss of Microbial Safety Margin** — Botulism risk in low-acid RTE foods (pH > 4.6) if thermal process is inadequate.
7. **Reduced Fiber & Micronutrient Content** — Refined flours used in many RTE snacks are low in fiber.

## 2.3 RTE Foods Available in India (KTU Board-Important List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Category-wise products you MUST memorize for KTU 2024 (this is a frequent 7-mark descriptive question):**

### A. Snacks & Extruded Products
- **Kurkure**, **Lay's**, **Bingo!** — Fried/extruded potato/chip-based
- **Uncle Chipps** — Puffed potato
- **Puffcorn / Act II Popcorn** — Microwave popcorn
- **Crax / Cheetos** — Direct-expanded corn snacks

### B. Instant Noodles & Pasta
- **Maggi Noodles** (Nestlé), **Top Ramen** (Nissin), **Yippee Noodles** (ITC), **Patanjali Atta Noodles**
- **Ching's Secret Hakka Noodles** (pre-sauced)

### C. Ready Meals / Curries
- **ITC Kitchens of India** — Ready-to-eat *Dal Makhani, Paneer Butter Masala, Biryani*
- **MTR Ready-to-Eat Meals** — *Rasam, Sambar, Palak Paneer, Pongal*
- **Kissan Ready-to-Eat** — *Tomato Ketchup* family + curries
- **Tasty Bite** (Preferred Brands Inc.) — Indian restaurant-style curries in retort pouches

### D. Breakfast Cereals & Muesli
- **Kellogg's Corn Flakes, Chocos, All-Bran**
- **Bagrry's** — *White Oats, Multigrain Muesli*

### E. Instant Mixes (RTC)
- **MTR / Gits** — *Idli, Dosa, Upma, Halwa* ready mixes
- **Knorr** — *Tomato Soup, Sweet Corn Soup, Thick Tomato Soup*

### F. RTE Baked Goods
- **Britannia Marie Gold, Milk Bikis, NutriChoice**
- **Modern / Harvest Gold** — *White, Brown, Multigrain Bread* (partially RTE)

### G. Canned & Retort Foods
- **Tata Sampann Ready-to-Eat** curries
- **Haldiram's** — *Bhel Puri, Chana, Rajma* (retort-packed)

## 2.4 Marketing of RTE Foods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently-Asked Marketing Mix (4Ps/7Ps) for RTE Foods — Board Weightage: 7 Marks**

The marketing strategy uses the **extended 7Ps Marketing Mix**:

| Pillar | Strategic Action in RTE Context |
|---|---|
| **Product** | Taste variants, regional flavors (Sambar Maggi, Masala Bikaneri Bhujia), clean-label, fortification (iron, B12). |
| **Price** | Penetration pricing for entry; premium pricing for organic/gluten-free. MRP + GST (12 % / 18 % slab depending on category). |
| **Place** | Modern retail (Reliance Fresh, D-Mart), e-commerce (BigBasket, Blinkit, Amazon Fresh), general trade (kirana stores). Cold chain for chilled RTE. |
| **Promotion** | Celebrity endorsements (e.g., *Madhuri Dixit* for Maggi), social media, TV ads during IPL, sampling at metro stations. |
| **People** | Trained sales force, food technologists for QA at distribution. |
| **Process** | Online ordering, last-mile delivery, QR code-based traceability. |
| **Physical Evidence** | Branded packaging, FSSAI logo, veg/non-veg mark, nutrition label. |

### Key Marketing Challenges
1. **Consumer skepticism** about preservatives and nutritional value.
2. **FSSAI compliance** — License under FSSAI Act 2006, mandatory labelling.
3. **Brand trust restoration** — post-Maggi ban (2015) and quality scares.

## 2.5 Future Prospects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Future Growth Drivers (India RTE Market):**
> - **CAGR** of Indian RTE food market is projected at **12 – 14 %** (2024–2030) by IMARC, RedSeer reports.
> - **Market size** of Indian RTE / convenience food sector: **USD 50+ billion by 2030.**
> - Drivers: Rising nuclear families, working women (~25 % workforce), urbanization, dual-income households, e-commerce penetration.
> - Trends: **Plant-based RTE**, **high-protein convenience meals**, **regional authentic flavors**, **clean-label**, **fortified staples**, **functional beverages**, **3D-printed food prototypes**.

## 2.6 Extruded Foods — Detailed Theory

### Definition
Extrusion is a **HTST** (High-Temperature Short-Time) cooking process where food is simultaneously:
- **Mixed** by rotating screw(s)
- **Cooked** by external barrel heaters and viscous dissipation
- **Sheared** by screw geometry and restrictive elements
- **Shaped** by the die

### Classification of Extrusion
1. **Cold Extrusion** — Temperature < 70 °C; for pasta, dough shaping.
2. **Hot Extrusion** — Temperature 100–200 °C; for expanded snacks.

### Screw Anatomy (KTU high-yield diagram, see Section 4)
The screw has three distinct zones:

| Zone | Function | Length |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed Zone** | Conveys raw material, pre-conditioning | ~25 % of L |
| **Compression / Kneading Zone** | Compresses, shears, cooks material | ~50 % of L |
| **Metering Zone** | Develops uniform pressure for die discharge | ~25 % of L |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Extrusion Engineering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-examinable. Memorize the symbol legend.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot h \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{2}$ | Volumetric throughput (forward flow in a single-screw extruder, neglecting leakage). | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{leak}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot e^3 \cdot N \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{12 \cdot L}$ | Leakage (drag-induced back-flow) through the barrel-screw clearance. | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{pressure}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot h^3 \cdot \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$ | Pressure-induced back-flow. | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{drag}} - Q_{\text{leak}} - Q_{\text{pressure}}$ | Actual net throughput. | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $SME = \frac{\tau_{\text{net}} \cdot N}{Q_{\text{net}}} \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy input per kg of product. | $\text{J/kg}$ or $\text{Wh/kg}$ |
| 6 | $P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T$ | Power consumed by the screw, where $T$ is torque. | $\text{W (watts)}$ |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \frac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L \cdot Q}{W \cdot H^3}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille die pressure drop for rectangular die ($W \times H$). | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | $P_{\text{exp}} = 0.19 \cdot \rho_{\text{dry}}^{-0.84}$ | Bulk density of extrudate (Mohamed's equation). | $\text{g/cm}^3$ |
| 9 | $\text{ETI} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{feed}}}{H_{\text{product}}}$ | Expansion (Expansion Thickness Index) ratio. | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | Feed moisture content on wet basis. | % w.b. |

### Symbol Legend
- $D$ = Barrel internal diameter (m)
- $h$ = Screw flight depth (m)
- $N$ = Screw rotational speed (rev/s)
- $\theta$ = Helix angle of flight (rad)
- $e$ = Clearance between flight tip and barrel (m)
- $L$ = Length of extruder section (m)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure difference along extruder / die (Pa)
- $\eta$ = Apparent viscosity of melt (Pa·s)
- $\tau$ = Net torque (N·m)
- $W, H$ = Die width and thickness (m)
- $\rho_{\text{dry}}$ = Dry bulk density (g/cm³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These equations are used by **food process engineers** to (a) **size the extruder** for a given plant capacity, (b) **estimate motor power** for gearbox selection, and (c) **predict product expansion** during process scale-up from lab to commercial line.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drag-Induced Forward Flow Rate ($Q_{\text{drag}}$)

### Statement
For a Newtonian fluid in a single-screw extruder, the volumetric drag flow in the unwound rectangular channel (formed by unwrapping the screw flight at helix angle $\theta$) is given by Equation 1.

### Assumptions
1. The melt is **incompressible and Newtonian** (justified for the *drag flow component* alone).
2. The screw rotates and the barrel is **stationary** (coordinate transform).
3. The flight is **infinitely thin**.
4. The flow is **laminar and fully developed** in the z-direction (down-channel).
5. **No leakage** at the barrel-screw clearance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Coordinate Transform**
Unwrap the screw channel into a flat rectangular plate inclined at helix angle $\theta$. The barrel slides over the channel at a relative velocity equal to the linear velocity of the screw tip:

$$v_b = \pi D N$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the Velocity Vector**
Decompose the tangential velocity $v_b$ into two components in the channel coordinates:
- Down-channel component: $v_x = v_b \cdot \cos\theta = \pi D N \cos\theta$
- Cross-channel component: $v_y = v_b \cdot \sin\theta = \pi D N \sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Drag Flow in Channel**
For a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ and depth $H = h$, the *drag-induced volumetric flow per unit width* is given by the planar Couette flow solution:

$$q_{\text{drag}} = \frac{v_x \cdot h}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over Cross-Section**
Multiply by the channel width $W$:

$$Q_{\text{drag}} = q_{\text{drag}} \cdot W = \frac{v_x \cdot h}{2} \cdot (\pi D \sin\theta) = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Equation**

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{drag}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)

### Step 1 — Mechanical Power Supplied to the Screw**
From rotational dynamics:

$$P = 2\pi N \tau$$

where $\tau$ is the net torque on the screw shaft (N·m).

### Step 2 — Mass Throughput**
Convert volumetric throughput $Q_{\text{net}}$ to mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ using the bulk density $\rho$ of the dough:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{\text{net}}$$

### Step 3 — Energy per Unit Mass (Definition of SME)**

$$SME = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{2\pi N \tau}{\rho \cdot Q_{\text{net}}}$$

### Step 4 — Include Motor-Drive Efficiency $\eta_{\text{motor}}$**

$$\boxed{\,SME = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau \cdot \eta_{\text{motor}}}{\rho \cdot Q_{\text{net}}}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die Pressure Drop (Rectangular Die)

**Step 1 — Geometry of Die Slit:** Width $W$, Thickness $H$, Length $L_d$.

**Step 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille for Slit:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \eta L_d Q}{W H^3}$$

**Step 3 — Derivation Sketch (from Navier-Stokes):**
For 1-D steady laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a thin slit, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2}$$

Integrating twice with the no-slip condition $v_z(0) = v_z(H) = 0$:

$$v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \cdot y(y - H)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate for Volumetric Flow:**

$$Q = W \int_0^H v_z(y)\, dy = -\frac{W H^3}{12\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L_d}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Delta P$:**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \eta L_d Q}{W H^3}\,}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Variable vs. Product Response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU-favourite comparison question. Memorize the + (increases), – (decreases), = (no major change) relationships.**

| Process Variable | SME | Melt Temp | Expansion | Density | Crispness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Screw Speed ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Feed Moisture ↑** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Barrel Temp ↑** | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ initially, then ↑ | ↑ initially, then ↓ | ↓ |
| **Screw Speed ↓** | – | – | – | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Screw L/D Ratio ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| **Die Open Area ↓** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

**Problem:** A single-screw extruder has $D = 80\,\text{mm}$, $h = 8\,\text{mm}$, $N = 300\,\text{rpm}$, $\theta = 20°$. Calculate the drag-induced flow rate $Q_{\text{drag}}$ in m³/h.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Units to SI**
$D = 0.08\,\text{m}$, $h = 0.008\,\text{m}$, $N = 300/60 = 5\,\text{rev/s}$, $\theta = 20° = 0.349\,\text{rad}$.

**Step 2 — Compute Trigonometric Terms**

$$\sin(20°) = 0.342, \quad \cos(20°) = 0.940$$

**Step 3 — Substitute in Drag Flow Equation**

$$Q_{\text{drag}} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot (0.08)^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.008 \cdot 0.342 \cdot 0.940}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\pi^2 = 9.8696$$
$$(0.08)^2 = 0.0064$$
$$0.0064 \cdot 5 = 0.032$$
$$0.032 \cdot 0.008 = 0.000256$$
$$0.000256 \cdot 0.342 = 8.7552 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$8.7552 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.940 = 8.230 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 — Final Division and Unit Conversion**

$$Q_{\text{drag}} = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 8.230 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 4.061 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$Q_{\text{drag}} = 4.061 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.462\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{drag}} \approx 1.46\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This drag-flow rate represents the **maximum theoretical throughput**. In practice, leakage and pressure back-flow reduce this to ~50–70 % of $Q_{\text{drag}}$ for a well-designed die-restricted system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Single-Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper with Volumetric Feeder] --> B[Feed Throat with Water Injection]
    B --> C[Barrel Section 1 - FEED ZONE]
    C --> D[Barrel Section 2 - COMPRESSION KNEADING ZONE]
    D --> E[Barrel Section 3 - METERING ZONE with Steam Jacket]
    E --> F[Heating Cooling Jackets on Barrel]
    F --> G[Die Plate with 2 to 4 mm Openings]
    G --> H[Rotary Cutter Knife Assembly]
    H --> I[Expanded Extrudate Product]
    H --> J[Drier Cooler Conveyor]
    J --> K[Flavor Coating Drum]
    K --> L[Packaging Line]

    subgraph "Drive System"
        M[Variable Frequency Drive] --> N[Reduction Gearbox]
        N --> O[Thrust Bearing Assembly]
        O --> P[Screw Shaft]
    end

    P -. rotates .-> C
    P -. rotates .-> D
    P -. rotates .-> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Industrial Extrusion Process for RTE Snacks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw1[Raw Material Receiving] --> insp[Quality Inspection - FSSAI]
    insp --> grind[Grinding to 200 micron Mesh]
    grind --> cond[Pre-Conditioning with Water and Steam 12 to 18 percent Moisture]
    cond --> ext[Extrusion at 120 to 180 degree C 25 to 50 bar]
    ext --> cut[In-Line Rotary Cutting]
    cut --> dry[Drying to 2 to 3 percent Moisture]
    dry --> flav[Flavor Coating Oil and Powder]
    flav --> weigh[Check Weighing]
    weigh --> pack[Automatic Pouch Packing under Nitrogen]
    pack --> metal[Metal Detection]
    metal --> ship[Carton and Shipment]

    qc[Online QC Lab - SME Expansion Bulk Density] -.-> ext
    qc -.-> dry
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Twin-Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Twin Screw Extruder System"
        feeder1[Volumetric Feeder] --> throat1[Feed Throat]
        throat1 --> barrelA[Barrel Module 1 - Feeding]
        barrelA --> barrelB[Barrel Module 2 - Mixing and Cooking]
        barrelB --> barrelC[Barrel Module 3 - Vacuum and Venting]
        barrelC --> barrelD[Barrel Module 4 - Metering and Die]

        screw1[Co-Rotating Screw Shaft A] -.-> barrelA
        screw1 -.-> barrelB
        screw1 -.-> barrelC
        screw1 -.-> barrelD
        screw2[Co-Rotating Screw Shaft B] -.-> barrelA
        screw2 -.-> barrelB
        screw2 -.-> barrelC
        screw2 -.-> barrelD

        barrelD --> dieP[Die Plate]
        dieP --> cutt[Cutting Unit]
    end

    tempC[Temperature Controller] -.-> barrelB
    vac[Vacuum Pump] -.-> barrelC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. List any **six advantages** of Ready-to-Eat (RTE) foods.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> The six main advantages of RTE foods are:
> 1. **Convenience and time-saving** — Cooking time is reduced from 30–60 min to under 5 min, supporting working professionals and nuclear families.
> 2. **Consistent quality** — Industrial processing under HACCP and ISO 22000 guarantees uniform taste, nutrition, and safety in every pack.
> 3. **Extended shelf life** — Retort processing and aseptic packaging allow ambient storage of 6–12 months.
> 4. **Portion control and hygiene** — Pre-measured servings minimize food waste and cross-contamination.
> 5. **Energy conservation** — Eliminates consumer-end cooking fuel (LPG), making it apartment-friendly.
> 6. **Reduced post-harvest losses** — Converts perishable farm produce into stable, value-added products, boosting farmer income and export potential.

*(3 marks — ½ mark per correctly explained advantage)*

### Q2. Define *extrusion cooking* and state the principle of a single-screw extruder.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Extrusion cooking** is a *high-temperature short-time* (HTST) thermomechanical process in which a food material (powder/dough with 12–28 % moisture) is forced to flow, under controlled conditions of mixing, heating, and shearing, through a *die* of defined geometry to yield a product of uniform shape, texture, and density.
>
> **Principle of Single-Screw Extruder:** A single rotating screw inside a stationary barrel transports the food material from the feed hopper to the die. The screw geometry (varying flight depth) creates a *pressure gradient* that compresses, shears, and cooks the material. The combined effect of (i) *frictional drag flow* on the rotating barrel, (ii) *pressure back-flow* against the die restriction, and (iii) *leakage flow* through the screw-barrel clearance produces a net forward throughput, while the sudden pressure drop at the die outlet causes *flash vaporization* of moisture, resulting in product expansion (puffing).

*(3 marks — 1½ for definition + 1½ for principle)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 carries two ESE questions, either-or pattern. Each is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

#### (a) Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages of RTE foods** in detail. Add a note on the **marketing strategies** adopted by the RTE food industry in India. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Ready-to-Eat foods are pre-cooked, packaged convenience foods requiring minimal/no further preparation before consumption. The Indian RTE market is among the fastest-growing convenience food segments, valued at **USD ~15–20 billion** with a **CAGR of 12–14 %**.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. Time-saving, consistent quality, hygiene, portion control, energy-efficient, shelf-stable, export potential, urban-lifestyle fit, employment generation.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. Nutritional loss (vitamins C, B1, B6, folate), high cost, preservatives/additives, packaging waste, sensory inferiority to fresh, low fiber in refined-flour products.

**Marketing Strategies (1 mark):**
- **4Ps + 3Ps framework** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion + People, Process, Physical Evidence).
- Celebrity endorsements, digital marketing, regional flavor variants (e.g., *Sambar Maggi, Ching's Schezwan*), FSSAI compliance, e-commerce distribution.

#### (b) Explain the **principle, components, and working of a single-screw extruder** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of **barrel temperature and screw speed** on product quality. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
A single-screw extruder operates on the principle of *drag-induced forward flow* combined with *pressure-induced back-flow* through a helical screw in a stationary heated barrel. The unwound channel of the screw (inclined at helix angle $\theta$) acts as a rectangular duct in which food melt is simultaneously sheared, cooked, and pressurized for discharge through a die.

**Components (2 marks):**
1. **Hopper and Volumetric Feeder** — meter dry material.
2. **Feed Throat** — water/steam injection point.
3. **Barrel** — jacketed for heating/cooling; L/D = 20:1 to 30:1.
4. **Screw** — has three zones: feed, compression (kneading), metering.
5. **Die Plate** — defines product shape and restrains flow.
6. **Rotary Cutter** — cuts extrudate to length.
7. **Drive System** — motor, VFD, gearbox, thrust bearing.

**Working (2 marks):**
The feeder delivers raw material to the feed throat where water is added (12–28 % moisture). The screw conveys, compresses, and shears the mass. Frictional heat and external heaters raise the temperature to 120–180 °C, gelatinizing starch and denaturing protein. At the die, the sudden pressure drop flashes the superheated moisture into steam, puffing the product. The cutter then slices the extrudate.

**Role of Process Variables (1 mark):**
- **Barrel Temperature ↑** → melt viscosity ↓, SME ↓, expansion ↑ (up to an optimum), then ↓.
- **Screw Speed ↑** → SME ↑, shear ↑, product density ↓, crispness ↑.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)

#### (a) Enumerate the **various Ready-to-Eat food products available in India** under appropriate categories. Add a note on the **future prospects** of the RTE food industry. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

**Categories with Examples (5 marks):**

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Extruded Snacks** | Kurkure, Lay's, Bingo!, Cheetos, Act II Popcorn |
| **Instant Noodles** | Maggi, Top Ramen, Yippee, Ching's |
| **Ready Meals** | ITC Kitchens of India, MTR, Tasty Bite, Haldiram's |
| **Breakfast Cereals** | Kellogg's Corn Flakes, Chocos; Bagrry's Muesli |
| **Instant Mixes** | MTR Idli/Dosa Mix, Gits Halwa Mix, Knorr Soups |
| **RTE Baked Goods** | Britannia Marie Gold, NutriChoice, Harvest Gold |
| **Canned / Retort** | Kissan Curries, Tata Sampann, Tasty Bite |

**Future Prospects (2 marks):**
- Indian RTE market CAGR: **12–14 %** (2024–2030).
- Drivers: nuclear families, working women, urbanization, e-commerce.
- Trends: **plant-based RTE**, **regional authentic flavors**, **clean-label**, **fortified foods**, **functional beverages**, **3D-food printing**, **ready-to-drink protein shakes**.

#### (b) What is **extrusion**? Describe the **principle and types of extruders** used in food processing. Discuss the factors affecting the **quality of extruded products**. **(7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Extrusion is a thermomechanical process in which food material is forced to flow, under one or more conditions of mixing, heating, and shear, through a die of defined shape.

**Principle (2 marks):** Based on the simultaneous application of *mechanical shear*, *thermal energy*, and *pressure* to plasticize, cook, and shape food material. The screw-barrel geometry and die restriction create the required pressure-temperature-moisture profile for starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and final puffing.

**Types of Extruders (2 marks):**
1. **Single-Screw Extruder** — Cold or hot; L/D = 20:1; used for snacks, pasta.
2. **Twin-Screw Extruder** — Co-rotating (self-wiping) or counter-rotating; used for breakfast cereals, TVP, baby foods.
3. **Cold Extruder** — < 70 °C; pasta, dough shaping.
4. **Hot Extruder** — 100–200 °C; expanded snacks.

**Factors Affecting Quality (2 marks):**
- **Feed moisture** ↑ → lower SME, lower expansion, denser product.
- **Screw speed** ↑ → higher SME, finer cell structure, more crispness.
- **Barrel temperature** ↑ → lower viscosity, more expansion (optimum), darker color.
- **Die geometry** — Die L/D ratio affects expansion index.
- **Feed composition** — Starch content, protein, fiber, lipid.
- **Screw configuration** — Number of kneading discs, L/D.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse RTE with RTC** — RTE = no cooking; RTC = needs only heating. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Do NOT list only "Maggi"** as an example of RTE — Examiners expect 5–6 diverse products across categories.
> 3. **In extrusion derivations**, do not forget the **$\sin\theta \cos\theta$** term in the drag-flow equation. Dropping it is a 2-mark error.
> 4. **Do not skip the assumption list** in derivations — KTU 2024 specifically awards 1 mark for clearly stating assumptions (Newtonian fluid, fully developed flow, etc.).
> 5. **For marketing**, use the 4Ps/7Ps framework; a vague "advertising" answer fetches only ½ mark — be specific (e.g., "regional flavor variant strategy", "e-commerce channel expansion", "FSSAI-compliant labelling").
> 6. **Always** state units in the final answer of numerical problems; writing "1.46" without m³/h loses ½ mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 — High-Yield Rapid Revision**

- **RTE foods** need NO further cooking; **RTC foods** need only heating.
- **Indian RTE market CAGR** = **12–14 %**, projected to **USD 50+ billion by 2030.**
- **FSSAI** governs labelling (mandatory declaration of veg/non-veg mark, ingredients, FSSAI logo + license no.).
- **Top RTE brands in India:** Maggi (instant noodles), MTR (ready meals), ITC Kitchens of India, Haldiram's, Tasty Bite, Kellogg's, Britannia.
- **Extrusion = HTST thermomechanical process** (100–200 °C, 15–60 s residence).
- **Three zones of screw:** Feed → Compression (Kneading) → Metering.
- **Drag-flow equation:** $Q_{\text{drag}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$
- **SME** controls expansion; high SME = more expanded, lower density product.
- **L/D ratio** for food extruders: **20:1 to 30:1**.
- **Twin-screw extruders** are preferred for **complex, high-shear, high-moisture** products (breakfast cereals, TVP).
- **Single-screw extruders** are preferred for **low-cost, high-throughput** snack production.
- **Future trends:** Plant-based RTE, functional fortified foods, regional authentic flavors, 3D-food printing, sustainable biodegradable packaging.
- **Marketing:** 4Ps + 3Ps framework; celebrity endorsements, digital/e-commerce channels, FSSAI-compliant labelling, regional flavor variants.
- **Advantages:** Time-saving, consistent quality, shelf-stable, portion control, energy-conserving, export revenue.
- **Disadvantages:** Vitamin loss, preservatives, packaging waste, cost, lower sensory appeal vs. fresh.
- **Typical extruder parameters:** $D = 50$–$200$ mm, $N = 100$–$500$ rpm, $h = 5$–$15$ mm, $\theta = 17°$–$22°$, feed moisture = 12 %–28 % w.b.
- **Died pressure drop** for slit die: $\Delta P = \dfrac{12\eta L_d Q}{W H^3}$.
- **Expansion (ETI) $\uparrow$** with screw speed and feed protein content; **$\downarrow$** with feed moisture and lipid content.
- **Direct-expanded** snacks (e.g., *Crax, Kurkure*) use **high SME** extrusion; **pellet-extruded** snacks are cooked at low SME and re-expanded by frying.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Pasta - Noodle and Macaroni Products
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Production of Pasta - Noodle and Macaroni Products

# Production of Pasta, Noodle and Macaroni Products

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition – Pasta (FAO/WHO Codex Alimentarius)**
> *Pasta is the product obtained by extruding or sheeting, with or without drying, an unleavened dough prepared from durum wheat semolina (Triticum durum), common wheat flour (Triticum aestivum), or a combination thereof, with potable water, and optional inclusion of eggs, salt, or permitted additives.*

In simple terms, **pasta is a dough of wheat and water that has been shaped and (usually) dried** for long shelf-life storage. The Italian Codex standards (*Disciplinare della Pasta*) make it mandatory that true Italian pasta be made from **100 % durum wheat semolina**.

### 1.1 Classification Overview

The broad family of pasta-style products can be categorized by **geometry, raw material, and process route** as shown below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Pasta Identity (must remember for KTU viva):**
> 1. **Raw Material Base** – Durum wheat semolina vs. bread wheat flour
> 2. **Forming Method** – Extrusion (macaroni-type) vs. Sheeting (noodle-type)
> 3. **Final Preservation** – Hot-air drying (macaroni) vs. Steaming + Frying (instant noodles)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Clay-Pot vs. The Sun-Dried Brick"

Imagine two ways of preserving clay:

- **Pasta = Sun-Dried Bricks**: You take wet clay (dough), force it through a shaped hole (die), and slowly dry it. The brick keeps its form permanently and only re-softens when water is added back later (cooking).
- **Instant Noodles = Pre-Cooked & Flash-Fried Potato**: You cook the dough first (steaming), then flash-fry it to create a porous, shelf-stable structure that re-hydrates in **3 minutes** when hot water is added. This is the *convenience* dimension.

This analogy makes the fundamental difference obvious: **macaroni is preserved by drying the raw starch structure, while instant noodles are preserved by gelatinizing the starch first and then removing water via frying**.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics for Pasta Products

| Parameter | Standard Value (Codex/IS 14835) |
|---|---|
| Moisture content (final product) | **≤ 12.5 %** (w.b.) |
| Protein content | **≥ 12 %** (d.b.) for durum pasta |
| Ash content | **≤ 1.0 %** |
| Acidity (as lactic acid) | **≤ 0.05 %** |
| Cooking loss (acceptable) | **≤ 8 %** for good quality |
| Optimum cooking time | **8–12 min** for dry macaroni |
| pH of cooked water | **6.0 – 6.8** |

### 1.4 Definitions You Must Memorize for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Macaroni**: Generic term for dry pasta made from **durum semolina + water**, formed by **extrusion through a die** and **dried by hot air**. Examples: spaghetti, penne, fusilli, elbow macaroni.
>
> **Noodle**: Generic term for pasta made from **wheat flour (often with egg or alkaline salts) + water**, formed by **rolling/sheeting and slitting/cutting**. Examples: ramen, udon, chow mein, rice noodles, instant noodles.
>
> **Instant Noodle**: A noodle that has been **gelatinized (steamed) and dehydrated (deep-fried or hot-air dried)**, allowing **rehydration in < 5 min** by adding boiling water.
>
> **Durum Wheat (Triticum durum)**: A **tetraploid hard wheat** with vitreous, yellow endosperm; ideal for pasta due to **high protein (12–15 %) and strong gluten network**.
>
> **Semolina**: Coarsely ground **endosperm of durum wheat**, with particle size **150–500 µm**, distinctly granular.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Macaroni (Dry Pasta) Production – Step-by-Step Logic

The industrial macaroni line consists of **six major unit operations**. Each step has a specific *why* and *how*, which KTU frequently tests in descriptive questions.

### Step 1 – Raw Material Selection & Milling
- **Why?** Durum semolina's yellow carotenoid pigment (lutein) and strong gluten give pasta its characteristic **yellow color, firm texture (al dente), and cooking tolerance**.
- **How?** Durum wheat is cleaned, **tempered to 16 % moisture**, and milled through a roller-mill to yield semolina of uniform granulation (~350 µm).

### Step 2 – Dough Hydration & Mixing
- Water is added at **30–35 % of semolina weight** to form a stiff, crumbly dough.
- Mixing is performed under **partial vacuum (0.5–0.7 atm)** to:
  - Remove occluded air bubbles (prevents "white spots" and fractures).
  - Allow uniform hydration of semolina granules.

### Step 3 – Extrusion (The Heart of Macaroni Production)
- The hydrated dough is forced through a **bronze die or PTFE die** at pressures of **40–100 atm**.
- **Bronze die** → rough surface, sauce-friendly ("trafilata").
- **PTFE (Teflon) die** → smooth, shiny surface (industrial mass-market).
- Die temperature is held at **45–50 °C** to prevent starch pre-gelatinization.

### Step 4 – Pre-Drying (Critical Quality Stage)
- Just-extruded pasta is at **~30 % moisture**.
- Pre-drying at **50–60 °C with 70–80 % RH** for **30–60 min** removes the **surface moisture** and forms a rigid crust.
- This step **prevents checking (cracking)** later in drying.

### Step 5 – Final Drying
- Two regimes:
  - **Low-Temperature (LT)**: 50–60 °C, 18–24 h, traditional, premium quality.
  - **High-Temperature (HT)**: 80–120 °C, 4–8 h, faster, gives better microbiological stability.
- Final moisture reduced to **≤ 12.5 %**.

### Step 6 – Cooling & Packaging
- Pasta is cooled to **20–25 °C** to prevent condensation and microbial growth.
- Packaged in moisture-impermeable pouches or boxes.

## 2.2 Instant Noodle (Ramen) Production – Step-by-Step Logic

The instant noodle line differs at Steps 3–5 because the *purpose is gelatinization*, not drying.

### Step 1 – Flour Selection
- Use **bread wheat flour** (softer gluten, brighter noodle color) rather than durum.
- Alkaline salts called **Kansui (鹼水)** are added: typically **K₂CO₃ + Na₂CO₃ (9:1)**.
- Kansui raises dough pH to **9–10**, giving:
  - Yellow color (enhances lutein visibility).
  - Firm, springy texture.
  - Characteristic alkaline flavor.

### Step 2 – Mixing & Resting
- Flour + water + kansui + salt → mixed into a stiff dough.
- Rested **20–30 min** to relax gluten (maturation).

### Step 3 – Sheeting (Rolling) and Cutting
- Dough is passed through **paired rollers** to form continuous sheets of **1.0–1.5 mm thickness**.
- Sheets are cut into strands of desired width via a **slitter**.

### Step 4 – Steaming (Gelatinization)
- Cut noodles pass through a **continuous steamer at 100 °C for 5–10 min**.
- Steam fully **gelatinizes the starch**, locking in the cooked structure.

### Step 5 – Frying or Hot-Air Drying
- **Fried** (most common): submerged in **palm oil at 140–160 °C for 60–120 s**.
  - Reduces moisture from ~32 % to **2–4 %**.
  - Creates the porous "sponge" structure.
  - Shelf life: **6 months**.
- **Air-dried (non-fried)**: hot-air dryer at **80–100 °C** reduces moisture to **8–10 %**.
  - Healthier (no oil), but **slower rehydration (5–8 min)**.

### Step 6 – Cooling & Sachet Packaging
- Cooled noodles are portioned into brick-shaped blocks with **seasoning sachets**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** – know the variable definitions and units thoroughly.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (Wet Basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $W_w$ = wet weight (g); $W_d$ = dry weight (g) |
| 2 | Moisture Content (Dry Basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | $W_w, W_d$ as above |
| 3 | Conversion between bases | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100$ | Both in % |
| 4 | Water Absorption (cooking) | $WA = \dfrac{W_{cooked} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | $W_{cooked}$ = drained cooked wt; $W_{dry}$ = original dry wt |
| 5 | Cooking Loss | $CL = \dfrac{W_{dry} - W_{drained}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | $W_{drained}$ = wt after draining cook water |
| 6 | Rehydration Ratio (instant noodles) | $RR = \dfrac{W_{rehydrated}}{W_{dry}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Increase in Volume (IV) | $IV = \dfrac{V_{cooked} - V_{dry}}{V_{dry}} \times 100$ | Volumes in mL |
| 8 | Drying Rate | $N = \dfrac{dX}{d\theta}$ (kg water/kg dry solid·h) | $X$ = free moisture content |
| 9 | Gelatinization Temperature Range | **60 – 80 °C** for wheat starch | – |
| 10 | Optimum Kansui Level | **0.5 – 1.5 %** of flour weight | – |
| 11 | Frying Oil Absorption | $OA = \dfrac{W_{fried} - W_{steamed}}{W_{fried}} \times 100$ | Typical: 18–22 % |
| 12 | Throughput (extrusion) | $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | $A$ = die area; $v$ = velocity; $\rho$ = dough density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used in **QA/QC labs of pasta plants** (e.g., ITC's Sunfeast Yippee plant), in **ISO 7304 / AACC 16-50 standard tests**, and in **product development** for setting cooking instructions on packaging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Conversion Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content

The two bases arise because drying engineers naturally use dry solid mass as a reference (no zero divide), while food analysts and Codex standards use wet basis.

Let $M$ denote moisture % and let $S$ denote solids %.

**By definition (wet basis):**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Total mass}} \times 100$$

**By definition (dry basis):**

$$M_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{\text{Mass of water}}{\text{Mass of dry solid}} \times 100$$

**Step 1 – Express everything in terms of $M_{wb}$:**

Since $M_{wb}$ is in percentage, the ratio of water mass to total mass equals $M_{wb}/100$.

$$\frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100}$$

**Step 2 – Solve for the ratio $W_w / W_d$:**

$$1 - \frac{W_d}{W_w} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100} \implies \frac{W_d}{W_w} = 1 - \frac{M_{wb}}{100} = \frac{100 - M_{wb}}{100}$$

**Step 3 – Invert:**

$$\frac{W_w}{W_d} = \frac{100}{100 - M_{wb}}$$

**Step 4 – Substitute into $M_{db}$ formula:**

$$\frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} = \frac{W_w}{W_d} - 1 = \frac{100}{100 - M_{wb}} - 1 = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$$

**Step 5 – Multiply by 100:**

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$

This is the **master conversion equation**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 – Moisture Conversion (KTU pattern)

> **Question:** A pasta sample has moisture content of **12.5 % (w.b.)**. Convert this to dry-basis percentage.

**Solution:**

Apply the derived conversion formula:

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100 = \frac{12.5}{100 - 12.5} \times 100$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{12.5}{87.5} \times 100 = 0.14286 \times 100 = 14.29\%$$

**Reverse check (sanity check):**

$$M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}} \times 100 = \frac{14.29}{114.29} \times 100 = 12.5\% \ \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 2 – Water Absorption and Cooking Loss Relationship

Water absorption measures how much water the pasta takes up; cooking loss measures how much solid material leaches into the cooking water.

**Definitions (restated for clarity):**

$$WA = \frac{W_c - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$CL = \frac{W_d - W_r}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{...(2)}$$

where $W_c$ = weight of cooked drained pasta, $W_d$ = original dry pasta, $W_r$ = residue weight of cooking water (after evaporating to dryness).

**Mass balance argument:**
Before cooking we have only dry pasta of mass $W_d$.

During cooking, two things happen:
- **Gain:** Water enters the pasta, increasing mass to $W_c$.
- **Loss:** Some starch and protein leach into water, leaving residue $W_r$.

Therefore, the *apparent* water gained must be reduced by what was lost:

$$W_c = W_d + (\text{water absorbed}) - (\text{solid leached})$$

$$\text{water absorbed} = W_c - W_d + W_r$$

**Substituting into WA:**

$$WA_{true} = \frac{(W_c - W_d + W_r)}{W_d} \times 100 = WA_{apparent} + CL$$

This shows: **the apparent water absorption underestimates true water uptake by an amount equal to the cooking loss**. This is a *very high-yield concept* for KTU descriptive questions.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 – Combined Cooking Quality (KTU pattern)

> **Question:** 100 g of dry macaroni is cooked in boiling water.
> After draining, the cooked pasta weighs **220 g**.
> The cooking water, on evaporation to dryness, yields a residue of **5 g**.
> Calculate (a) Apparent water absorption, (b) Cooking loss, (c) True water absorption.

**Given:**
$W_d = 100$ g; $W_c = 220$ g; $W_r = 5$ g.

**(a) Apparent Water Absorption:**

$$WA = \frac{W_c - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{220 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 120\%$$

**(b) Cooking Loss:**

$$CL = \frac{W_d - W_r}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{5}{100} \times 100 = 5\%$$

*Note:* Some textbooks define cooking loss with respect to $W_c$; the KTU-recommended formulation uses original $W_d$ for absolute clarity.

**(c) True Water Absorption:**

$$WA_{true} = WA_{apparent} + CL = 120 + 5 = 125\%$$

**Interpretation:** A high-quality pasta should have **WA > 100 %** and **CL < 8 %**. Our sample (120 % WA, 5 % CL) is in the *premium quality band*.

## 3.5 Derivation 3 – Rehydration Ratio for Instant Noodles

For instant noodles, rehydration is the *inverse* of pasta cooking. The rehydration ratio ($RR$) is defined as:

$$RR = \frac{W_{rehydrated}}{W_{dry}}$$

For a fried instant noodle (moisture reduced from ~32 % to ~3 % during frying), the maximum theoretical rehydration (if all dried mass returns to cooked state) follows:

**Step 1:** Wet weight of freshly steamed noodle (pre-fry):

$$W_{steamed} = W_{dry} \times (1 + M_{steam})$$

where $M_{steam}$ is the steam moisture fraction (~0.32).

**Step 2:** After frying, the moisture falls to $M_f \approx 0.03$ but the *solid mass* remains unchanged (only water is removed, oil is added in parallel):

$$W_{fried} = W_{dry} \times (1 + M_f) + m_{oil}$$

where $m_{oil}$ is the absorbed oil (typically 18–22 % of $W_{dry}$).

**Step 3:** Rehydration ideal case – all absorbed oil is replaced by water and starch returns to its cooked moisture state:

$$W_{rehydrated}^{max} = W_{dry} + m_{oil} + (W_{dry} \times M_{steam})$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$RR_{max} = \frac{W_{dry}(1 + M_{steam}) + m_{oil}}{W_{dry}} = 1 + M_{steam} + \frac{m_{oil}}{W_{dry}}$$

For typical values $M_{steam} = 0.32$ and $m_{oil}/W_{dry} = 0.20$:

$$RR_{max} = 1 + 0.32 + 0.20 = 1.52$$

This matches empirical observations that **a standard ramen brick rehydrates to ~1.5× its dry weight** with boiling water.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Macaroni vs. Instant Noodles

| Attribute | Macaroni (Dry Pasta) | Instant Noodles |
|---|---|---|
| Wheat type | Durum semolina (Triticum durum) | Bread wheat flour (Triticum aestivum) |
| Forming method | Extrusion through die | Sheeting + slitting |
| Alkaline agent | Not used | Kansui (K₂CO₃ + Na₂CO₃) |
| Starch state in finished product | Ungelatinized (raw) | Fully gelatinized |
| Preservation method | Hot-air drying only | Steaming + Frying (or air-drying) |
| Final moisture | ≤ 12.5 % | Fried: 2–4 %; Air-dried: 8–10 % |
| Rehydration time | 8–12 min (boil) | 3–5 min (fried); 5–8 min (air-dried) |
| Shelf life | 2 years | 6 months (fried); 9 months (air-dried) |
| Texture on eating | Firm, chewy (al dente) | Soft, spongy |
| Protein required | ≥ 12 % | 8–10 % (sufficient) |
| Key quality parameter | Cooking loss < 8 % | Rehydration ratio ≥ 1.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A, Q1(c)]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **durum wheat semolina** and **bread wheat flour** as raw materials for pasta production.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Durum Wheat Semolina | Bread Wheat Flour |
|---|---|---|
| Botanical species | *Triticum durum* (tetraploid) | *Triticum aestivum* (hexaploid) |
| Protein content | 12–15 % | 8–11 % |
| Gluten strength | Very strong, elastic | Medium |
| Endosperm color | Yellow (carotenoids) | White |
| Granulation | Coarse (150–500 µm) | Fine (< 150 µm) |
| Preferred for | Macaroni (dry pasta) | Instant noodles, egg noodles |

**Mark distribution:** 1 mark for definition; 1 mark for protein/granulation difference; 1 mark for application context.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2(a)]**

**Q2.** What is **kansui**? Why is it added to instant noodle dough?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Kansui is a mixture of alkaline salts, traditionally containing **potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃) and sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃)** in a 9:1 ratio, sometimes with sodium polyphosphate.

**Functions (2 marks):**
1. **Color**: Raises dough pH to 9–10, enhancing the visibility of yellow lutein pigments, giving the noodle its characteristic bright yellow color.
2. **Texture**: Strengthens gluten network, producing firm, springy, and elastic noodles (called *q* texture in Chinese cuisine).
3. **Flavor**: Imparts a distinct alkaline taste characteristic of ramen and udon noodles.
4. **Preservation**: The alkaline pH inhibits microbial growth and improves shelf life.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pasta Questions:**
> - Students frequently **omit the "why"** behind each processing step – losing 2–3 marks.
> - Confusing **wet-basis and dry-basis** moisture in numericals is the #1 deduction trap (lose 1–2 marks).
> - In extrusion questions, students forget to mention **die type (bronze vs PTFE)** and **vacuum kneading** – lose 2 marks.
> - For instant noodles, omitting **kansui composition** is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q4(b)]*

**(a) Explain the industrial manufacturing process of macaroni products with a flow diagram. Highlight the role of raw material selection, extrusion, and drying stages.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Macaroni is a dry pasta produced from **durum wheat semolina and water**, formed by extrusion through a die and dried by hot air. The industrial process follows six stages: raw material preparation, dough mixing, extrusion, pre-drying, final drying, and packaging.

**Stage 1 – Raw Material Preparation (2 marks):**
Durum wheat (*Triticum durum*) is preferred due to its **high protein (12–15 %)**, **strong gluten**, and **yellow carotenoid pigments**. Wheat is cleaned, tempered to **16 % moisture** for 8–12 h, then milled through roller mills. The resulting **semolina** is sifted to a uniform granulation of ~350 µm, which ensures uniform hydration and a smooth pasta surface.

**Stage 2 – Mixing & Kneading (1 mark):**
Semolina is hydrated with **30–35 % water** and kneaded under **partial vacuum (0.5–0.7 atm)** to remove air bubbles. Air pockets would otherwise cause **white spots and fracture lines** in dried pasta.

**Stage 3 – Extrusion (2 marks):**
The dough is forced through a die at **40–100 atm pressure, 45–50 °C**. **Bronze dies** give a rough, sauce-friendly surface ("trafilata") preferred in artisanal pasta. **PTFE dies** give a smooth, glossy surface for industrial pasta. The die defines the pasta shape (spaghetti, penne, fusilli).

**Stage 4 – Pre-Drying & Final Drying (1 mark):**
Two-stage drying prevents **checking (cracking)**:
- **Pre-drying**: 50–60 °C, 70 % RH for 30–60 min forms a rigid outer crust.
- **Final drying**: LT (50–60 °C, 18–24 h) or HT (80–120 °C, 4–8 h).
- Final moisture reduced to **≤ 12.5 %** as per Codex standards.

**Conclusion (mark embedded in flow):** A simple block diagram (extruder → die → dryer → packer) should be drawn for 1 mark.

**(b) Describe the quality parameters used to evaluate macaroni quality. Calculate the cooking loss and water absorption for a sample where 50 g of dry pasta, after cooking, weighs 130 g drained and the cooking water residue weighs 3 g.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Quality Parameters (3 marks):**
1. **Cooking time** – optimum time for the pasta to reach *al dente* (8–12 min).
2. **Water absorption** – water uptake by the cooked pasta; higher = better tenderness.
3. **Cooking loss** – solids leached into cooking water; **≤ 8 %** for premium quality.
4. **Stickiness** – degree of noodle-to-noodle adhesion after cooking.
5. **Firmness (Texture Profile Analysis)** – measured by penetrometer or TPA; target 1.5–2.5 N peak force.
6. **Color** – yellow intensity using Minolta L*a*b* colorimeter; +b value indicates yellowness.
7. **Swell index** – increase in volume after cooking.
8. **Sensory evaluation** – trained panel for flavor, mouthfeel, appearance.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given:
$W_d = 50$ g (dry pasta), $W_c = 130$ g (cooked drained pasta), $W_r = 3$ g (cooking water residue).

**Step 1 – Water Absorption:**

$$WA = \frac{W_c - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{130 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{80}{50} \times 100 = 160\%$$

**Step 2 – Cooking Loss:**

$$CL = \frac{W_r}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{3}{50} \times 100 = 6\%$$

**Step 3 – Quality Judgment (1 mark):**
- Cooking loss of **6 %** is **below the 8 % threshold** → premium grade.
- Water absorption of **160 %** indicates good, soft texture suitable for sauce retention.
- The sample **passes both tests** and is commercially acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q5(a) alt]*

**(a) Discuss the production of instant noodles with a detailed flow diagram. Compare fried and non-fried varieties.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Instant noodles (typically ramen-style) are gelatinized, dehydrated noodle blocks requiring only **3–5 min of boiling water** for rehydration. The Japanese term *ramen* (拉麺) literally means "pulled noodles."

**Production Stages (4 marks):**

**Stage 1 – Dough Preparation:**
Bread wheat flour is mixed with water, salt, and **kansui (0.5–1.5 %)**. Kansui (a blend of K₂CO₃ and Na₂CO₃ in 9:1 ratio) imparts characteristic yellow color and springy texture.

**Stage 2 – Maturation:**
Dough is rested **20–30 min** to allow gluten relaxation and uniform hydration.

**Stage 3 – Sheeting:**
Dough is passed through **paired rollers** reducing thickness in stages to **1.0–1.5 mm**. Multiple passes ensure gluten alignment and uniform sheet.

**Stage 4 – Slitting & Waving:**
Sheets are cut into strands by **rotary slitters**. A **wave maker** presses alternating notches to form the characteristic wavy strands of ramen.

**Stage 5 – Steaming (Gelatinization):**
Strands pass through a **continuous steamer at 100 °C for 5–10 min**, fully gelatinizing starch.

**Stage 6 – Folding & Drying:**
Cut and folded into brick shapes, then either:
- **Fried** in palm oil at **140–160 °C for 60–120 s** → 2–4 % moisture.
- **Air-dried** at **80–100 °C for 30–60 min** → 8–10 % moisture.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Attribute | Fried Instant Noodles | Non-Fried (Air-Dried) |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture final | 2–4 % | 8–10 % |
| Rehydration time | 3–5 min | 5–8 min |
| Texture | Spongy, porous | Chewy, dense |
| Fat content | 18–22 % | 1–2 % |
| Shelf life | 6 months | 9–12 months |
| Caloric density | High (~470 kcal/100 g) | Lower (~370 kcal/100 g) |
| Health perception | Less healthy | Healthier |
| Examples | Maggi, Top Ramen | Soba, some udon varieties |

**(b) A noodle manufacturer uses semolina with 13.5 % moisture (w.b.). Calculate: (i) dry basis moisture, (ii) the mass of water in 250 g of this semolina, and (iii) the dry solid mass.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $M_{wb} = 13.5\%$, Total weight $W_w = 250$ g.

**Part (i) – Dry Basis Moisture (2 marks):**

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100 = \frac{13.5}{100 - 13.5} \times 100$$

$$M_{db} = \frac{13.5}{86.5} \times 100 = 0.15607 \times 100 = 15.61\%$$

**Part (ii) – Mass of Water (3 marks):**

Using wet-basis definition:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \implies 13.5 = \frac{250 - W_d}{250} \times 100$$

$$\frac{13.5}{100} \times 250 = 250 - W_d$$

$$33.75 = 250 - W_d \implies W_d = 250 - 33.75 = 216.25 \text{ g}$$

Therefore, **mass of water = 33.75 g**.

**Part (iii) – Dry Solid Mass (1 mark):**

$$W_d = 216.25 \text{ g}$$

**Part (iv) – Verification (1 mark):**

Check: $\dfrac{33.75}{216.25} \times 100 = 15.61\% \ \checkmark$ (matches $M_{db}$)

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
For pasta manufacturing, the **dry solid mass = 216.25 g** is the basis for ingredient scaling; water content at 33.75 g must be considered when calculating total dough hydration, because added water is in addition to this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Points – Must Remember for KTU 2024**

### Raw Materials
- Durum wheat (*Triticum durum*) → macaroni (high protein 12–15 %).
- Bread wheat (*Triticum aestivum*) → noodles (softer gluten).
- Semolina granulation: **150–500 µm**, prefer **350 µm**.
- **Kansui** = K₂CO₃ + Na₂CO₃ (9:1) at **0.5–1.5 %** of flour.

### Macaroni Process
1. Cleaning → Tempering (16 %) → Milling → Semolina.
2. Mixing with **30–35 % water** under vacuum.
3. Extrusion at **40–100 atm**, 45–50 °C.
4. Bronze die (rough) vs PTFE die (smooth).
5. Two-stage drying (LT 18–24 h or HT 4–8 h).
6. Final moisture **≤ 12.5 %**.

### Instant Noodle Process
1. Flour + Kansui + Water → Mix → Rest 20–30 min.
2. Sheeting to **1.0–1.5 mm** → Slitting.
3. **Steaming 100 °C, 5–10 min** (gelatinization).
4. Frying **140–160 °C, 60–120 s** OR Air-drying 80–100 °C.
5. Final moisture: **2–4 % (fried) / 8–10 % (non-fried)**.
6. Rehydration in **3–5 min (fried) / 5–8 min (non-fried)**.

### Key Numerical Formulas
- $MC_{wb} = (W_w - W_d)/W_w \times 100$
- $MC_{db} = (W_w - W_d)/W_d \times 100$
- $WA = (W_c - W_d)/W_d \times 100$
- $CL = W_r / W_d \times 100$ (acceptable ≤ 8 %)
- $RR = W_{rehydrated}/W_{dry}$
- $WA_{true} = WA_{apparent} + CL$

### Quality Thresholds to Memorize
- Cooking loss: **≤ 8 %** (premium), **≤ 10 %** (acceptable).
- Water absorption: **100–200 %** (typical range).
- Moisture: **≤ 12.5 %** (Codex pasta standard).
- Protein: **≥ 12 %** (Codex durum pasta standard).
- Optimum cooking time: **8–12 min** (macaroni), **3–5 min** (instant).

### Likely KTU 2024 Exam Traps
- Confusing wet-basis vs dry-basis → always verify by substitution.
- Forgetting vacuum kneading in macaroni → -2 marks.
- Not mentioning **Kansui composition** in noodle answers → -2 marks.
- Saying "frying cooks the noodle" → frying is **only for dehydration**; the noodle was already cooked by steaming → conceptual error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Extruders Used in Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Common Extruders Used in Food Industry ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Food Extrusion** is a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which a food material, typically a moistened and pre-conditioned mass of starch, protein, or fiber, is forced under high pressure and shear through a restricted opening (**die**) to form a product of definite shape and structure. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified under the broader family of **High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) processing technologies**, where the product is simultaneously cooked, mixed, sheared, and shaped in a single integrated unit operation. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot** > An **extruder** is a continuous-flow reactor consisting of a **barrel**, a rotating (or counter-rotating) **screw(s)**, a **die** at the discharge end, and auxiliary systems (feeder, preconditioner, cutter). The process simultaneously achieves conveying, mixing, cooking, texturization, and forming of food materials. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Mental Model Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste with a tiny round nozzle. As you apply pressure: - The thick paste (food dough) inside the tube (barrel) moves forward. - The narrow opening (die) controls the final shape. - Friction against the barrel wall heats the paste (shear heating). - The shaped ribbon that emerges (extrudate) cools and solidifies. This is **exactly** what happens inside a food extruder, but at industrial scales of **500 to 10,000 kg/hr**, with internal temperatures of **100 °C – 200 °C** and pressures reaching **3–15 MPa**. The "toothpaste tube" is essentially a single-screw extruder. ### 1.3 Classification of Food Extruders — Quick Map | Class | Sub-Type | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Single-Screw** | Cold Extruder | Pasta, Noodles | | | Hot Extruder | Snacks, Cereals | | **Twin-Screw** | Co-rotating, Intermeshing | Breakfast Cereals, TVP | | | Counter-rotating, Intermeshing | Confectionery, Pet Food | | **Interrupted-Flight (LEFM)** | Single-Screw | Co-extruded Snacks | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to (a) identify the components of each extruder type, (b) draw a labelled block diagram, and (c) compare single-screw vs twin-screw configurations — these are the three highest-frequency questions in KTU ESE papers on this topic. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder — Step-by-Step Operational Logic A single-screw extruder is a **barrel-within-a-rotating-screw** device. The food material, conditioned to **15–30 % moisture** (w.b.), enters via the feed hopper and is conveyed forward by a helical screw. Let us deconstruct the journey: **Step 1 — Feed & Solid Conveying Zone (Back of Barrel)** - Material enters as discrete particles. - Screw flights push the particles forward like a "solid plug" in an Archimedes screw. - Compaction begins; air is displaced backward and vented. **Step 2 — Transition (Melting / Cooking) Zone** - Temperature rises due to **external heating** (steam/electrical jacket) and **viscous dissipation** (friction from screw rotation). - Starch granules begin to **gelatinize**; proteins **denature**. - The material transforms from a particulate solid into a **plasticized melt**. **Step 3 — Metering (Pumping) Zone** - The fully plasticized melt behaves as a **non-Newtonian pseudo-plastic fluid**. - Screw flights of constant depth (shallower) act as a positive-displacement pump. - Pressure builds up rapidly to push the melt through the die. **Step 4 — Die & Shaping** - Pressure drops sharply at the die; melt is "squeezed" through the orifice. - The extrudate expands (**die swell / extrudate swell**), cools, and sets into the desired geometry. > [!TIP] > **Why it works:** The screw acts as a **pump** *and* a **cooker** simultaneously. The Archimedean geometry converts rotation into axial flow, while viscosity generates shear heating. ### 2.2 Single-Screw vs Twin-Screw — Operational Comparison | Parameter | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw | |---|---|---| | **Conveying** | Drag-induced (poor for sticky materials) | Positive displacement (excellent) | | **Mixing** | Limited (axial plug flow) | Superior (wiping action) | | **Self-Wiping** | No (residue stays) | Yes in co-rotating intermeshing | | **RPM Range** | 20–150 rpm | 100–800 rpm | | **SME (Specific Mechanical Energy)** | Low (50–100 kJ/kg) | High (150–800 kJ/kg) | | **Product Range** | Narrow (snacks, pasta) | Wide (cereals, TVP, pet food) | | **Cost** | Low (≈ ₹15–40 lakh) | High (≈ ₹1–3 crore) | ### 2.3 Twin-Screw Variants — Conceptual Map - **Co-rotating, Intermeshing (Most Common in Food):** Both screws rotate in the same direction. The "self-wiping" geometry forces material from one screw to the other, giving excellent mixing and minimal residue. Used for **breakfast cereals, TVP, infant foods**. - **Counter-rotating, Intermeshing:** Screws rotate in opposite directions. The material is "trapped" in the meshing zone, generating high pressure but poor conveying. Used for **conching chocolate, candy**. - **Non-Intermeshing:** Screws do not mesh. Used for highly viscous or reactive materials, similar to single-screw. ### 2.4 Cold vs Hot Extrusion — Crucial Distinction | Feature | Cold Extrusion | Hot Extrusion | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | Ambient to 50 °C | 100–200 °C | | **Moisture** | High (30–50 %) | Low (15–25 %) | | **Product Texture** | Dense, non-porous | Puffed, expanded | | **Screw Design** | Deep-flight, long L/D | Shallow-flight, L/D = 20–30 | | **Examples** | Pasta, noodles | Corn curls, breakfast cereals | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these five equations — they account for ~80 % of numerical and conceptual marks in KTU ESE Module 4.** | # | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi^{2} \, D^{2} \, N \, d \, \sin\theta \, \cos\theta$ | $D$ = screw diameter (m); $N$ = rpm (s⁻¹); $d$ = flight depth (m); $\theta$ = helix angle (°) | Maximum drag flow generated by screw rotation | | 2 | $Q_{pressure} = -\frac{\pi \, D \, d^{3} \, \sin^{2}\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure rise (Pa); $\eta$ = viscosity (Pa·s); $L$ = barrel length (m) | Back-flow due to die pressure | | 3 | $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ | m³/s | Actual extruder throughput | | 4 | $SME = \frac{2 \pi \cdot \tau \cdot N}{m}$ | $\tau$ = torque (N·m); $N$ = rpm (s⁻¹); $m$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) | Mechanical energy input per kg | | 5 | $\text{Residence Time } \bar{t} = \frac{V}{Q_{net}}$ | $V$ = effective barrel volume (m³) | Average time material spends in extruder | > **Real-world utility:** The **SME (Specific Mechanical Energy)** value is the single most important process parameter in food extrusion — it directly governs starch gelatinization degree, protein denaturation, and product expansion ratio. Snack-food manufacturers target **SME = 250–400 kJ/kg** for optimum puffing. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Net Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder The classical **Schenk and Fowler model** treats the melt inside the metering zone as a **Newtonian fluid flowing in a shallow rectangular channel** (a "unwrapped" representation of the screw channel). **Step 1: Geometric Setup** Unwrap the screw channel. The channel becomes a flat shallow trough of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (where $e$ is the flight width) and depth $d$. The barrel wall moves relative to the screw at a velocity $V_{x} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ along the down-channel direction. **Step 2: Apply Navier-Stokes Equation** For steady, fully-developed, isothermal flow, the Navier-Stokes equation simplifies to: $$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = \eta \left( \frac{\partial^{2} V_{z}}{\partial y^{2}} \right) $$ This is a one-dimensional pressure-driven (Poiseuille-type) flow superimposed on a drag-induced (Couette-type) flow. **Step 3: Solve the Coupled Flow Profile** Integrating the velocity field across the channel depth $y = 0$ to $d$ gives the total volumetric flow per channel: $$ Q_{channel} = \underbrace{\frac{V_{x} \, W \, d}{2}}_{\text{Drag Flow}} - \underbrace{\frac{W \, d^{3}}{12 \, \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}}_{\text{Pressure Flow}} $$ **Step 4: Substitute Geometric Back-Transformation** Replacing $V_{x} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$, then multiplying by $\cos\theta$ to convert from down-channel to axial flow, we obtain the classical twin equations: $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi^{2} \, D^{2} \, N \, d \, \sin\theta \, \cos\theta $$ $$ Q_{pressure} = \frac{\pi \, D \, d^{3} \, \sin^{2}\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L} $$ **Step 5: Net Throughput** $$ Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure} = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi^{2} \, D^{2} \, N \, d \, \sin\theta \, \cos\theta - \frac{\pi \, D \, d^{3} \, \sin^{2}\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L} $$ > **Logical note:** When the die is open (zero pressure, $\Delta P = 0$), throughput is maximum and equals $Q_{drag}$.
