When the die is fully blocked ($\Delta P$ maximum), throughput is zero. The operating point lies between these extremes — a critical insight for **die design and process control**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) for an Industrial Calculation **Problem Statement:** A co-rotating twin-screw extruder operating at 300 rpm delivers a torque of 850 N·m and processes corn grits at 1,500 kg/hr. Calculate the Specific Mechanical Energy input. **Step 1: Convert Units to SI** $$ N = 300 \text{ rpm} = \frac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ $$ \dot{m} = 1500 \text{ kg/hr} = \frac{1500}{3600} = 0.4167 \text{ kg/s} $$ **Step 2: Apply the SME Formula** $$ SME = \frac{2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot N}{\dot{m}} $$ **Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values** $$ SME = \frac{2\pi \times 850 \times 5}{0.4167} $$ **Step 4: Numerator Evaluation** $$ 2\pi \times 850 \times 5 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 850 \times 5 = 26{,}703.5 \text{ W (J/s)} $$ **Step 5: Final Division** $$ SME = \frac{26{,}703.5}{0.4167} = 64{,}084 \text{ J/kg} \approx 64.1 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 6: Interpretation** This SME value of **64.1 kJ/kg** is in the lower-to-mid range suitable for **directly expanded snack products** (typical target: 60–150 kJ/kg). Higher SME (achieved by raising torque or screw speed) shifts products toward **highly expanded cereals**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Extruder | Product Category | Target Moisture | Screw Type | Screw Speed (rpm) | SME Range (kJ/kg) | Die Configuration | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Pasta | 28–32 % | Single-screw, cold | 30–50 | 5–20 | Teflon-coated, rectangular | | Expanded Snacks (e.g., Kurkure) | 12–16 % | Single-screw, hot | 300–500 | 200–400 | Slotted, circular | | Breakfast Cereals (e.g., corn flakes) | 15–20 % | Twin-screw, co-rotating | 200–400 | 150–300 | Multi-hole round | | Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP) | 25–35 % | Twin-screw, co-rotating | 100–200 | 200–500 | Long cooling die | | Pet Food | 20–25 % | Twin-screw, co-rotating | 250–500 | 100–250 | Star/ring die | | Co-extruded Snacks (filled center) | 12–18 % | Interrupted flight (LEFM) | 200–400 | 150–300 | Annular with central filling tube | > **Real-world utility:** This matrix is the **ready-reckoner** used by food process engineers in plant design. A KTU examiner often sets a 14-mark question around choosing the correct extruder for a given product — the answer hinges on **moisture content, product texture, and SME requirements**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extruder System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material Hopper] --> B[Volumetric Feeder] B --> C[Preconditioner
Water + Steam] C --> D[Barrel Inlet
Feed Zone] D --> E[Conveying Zone
Solid Transport] E --> F[Kneading/Mixing Zone
Shear and Heat] F --> G[Cooking Zone
Starch Gelatinization] G --> H[Metering Zone
Pressure Build-up] H --> I[Die Plate
Shaping] I --> J[Flying Cutter
Length Control] J --> K[Drying/Cooling Belt] K --> L[Finished Extrudate] M[Drive Motor + Gearbox] -. Rotation .-> F N[Steam Jacket] -. Heat .-> F O[Vacuum Vent] -. Vapor .-> E ``` > **Reading guide:** Follow the thick arrows (left → right) for the material flow. The dotted arrows represent *energy inputs* (motor torque, steam heating) and *output streams* (vented vapor). This block diagram is the **exact "labelled block diagram"** that KTU examiners expect students to draw in the 14-mark question. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification Tree of Food Extruders ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FOOD EXTRUDERS] A --> B[Single-Screw] A --> C[Twin-Screw] A --> D[Interrupted-Flight] B --> B1[Cold Extruder
Pasta, Noodles] B --> B2[Hot Extruder
Snacks, Cereals] C --> C1[Co-Rotating Intermeshing
Cereals, TVP, Pet Food] C --> C2[Counter-Rotating Intermeshing
Confectionery] C --> C3[Non-Intermeshing
Specialty Chemicals] D --> D1[LEFM Extruder
Co-extruded Filled Snacks] ``` > **Reading guide:** This taxonomy is the visual version of the classification table in Section 2.4. KTU examiners often include "Draw the classification of food extruders" as a sub-question — present this tree as your answer. --- ### 4.3 Component-Level Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BARREL_ASSEMBLY[BARREL ASSEMBLY] B1[Feed Hopper] --> B2[Throat/Inlet] B2 --> B3[Barrel Wall
Jacket with Steam/Heater] B3 --> B4[Die Adapter] B4 --> B5[Die Plate] end subgraph SCREW_ASSEMBLY[SCREW SHAFT] S1[Drive Coupling] --> S2[Feed Flight
Deep Pitch] S2 --> S3[Transition Flight
Variable Depth] S3 --> S4[Metering Flight
Shallow Pitch] end subgraph AUX_SYSTEMS[AUXILIARY SYSTEMS] X1[Drive Motor] -. Coupling .-> S1 X2[Cutting Knives] -. Rotation .-> B5 X3[Control Panel] -. Sensors .-> B3 end ``` > **Reading guide:** Subgraphs in Mermaid represent physical sub-units. The three subgraphs correspond to the three functional blocks the examiner looks for: **barrel, screw, and auxiliaries**. The dotted arrows show the **transmission of power, rotation, and control signals**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. Define extrusion cooking and list its advantages in food processing.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Extrusion cooking** is a High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) process in which moistened food materials are continuously converted into plasticized melt and forced through a die to form products of desired shape, structure, and texture. **Key advantages (list 3 for full 3 marks):** 1. **Versatility** — A single extruder can produce pasta, snacks, cereals, and TVP by changing screw/die. 2. **Continuous operation** — Enables large-scale production (up to 10 tonnes/hr) with uniform quality. 3. **HTST sterilization** — Exposure at 150 °C for 30–60 s destroys pathogens while preserving nutrients. 4. **No effluents** — Closed system, environmentally friendly compared to traditional cooking. 5. **Energy efficient** — Viscous dissipation alone can supply 60–80 % of thermal energy needed. --- **Q2. Differentiate between cold extrusion and hot extrusion with two examples each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Cold Extrusion | Hot Extrusion | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | Below 50 °C (no cooking) | 100–200 °C (cooking occurs) | | **Product Texture** | Dense, elastic, non-puffed | Puffed, porous, crisp | | **Moisture** | High (28–50 %) | Low (12–25 %) | | **Screw Speed** | Low (30–60 rpm) | High (200–500 rpm) | | **Examples** | (i) Pasta, (ii) Noodles | (i) Corn puffs, (ii) Breakfast cereals | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the working of a single-screw extruder used in the food industry. Discuss the functions of each zone of the screw. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Define **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)**. A co-rotating twin-screw extruder operates at 400 rpm, delivers a torque of 1200 N·m, and processes maize grits at 2000 kg/hr. Calculate the SME input and comment on the product type suitable for this energy level. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) A **single-screw extruder** consists of a barrel, a helical screw, a die, and auxiliary systems (feeder, cutter, drive). The food material is fed via a hopper at the rear end, conveyed forward by the rotating screw, cooked by combined external and shear heating, pressurized, and finally pushed through a die. The screw is divided into **three functional zones** (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1): **1. Feed/Solid Conveying Zone:** - The screw flights have the **deepest pitch and largest volume** in this zone. - Material is transported as a "solid plug" by the Archimedean screw principle. - Function: Maximum intake capacity, minimal pressure rise. - Typically occupies 20–25 % of total screw length. **2. Kneading/Mixing/Cooking Zone:** - Screw flight depth **decreases progressively** (compression ratio of 1:2 to 1:3 typical). - Mechanical energy input (shear) raises melt temperature to 100–150 °C. - Starch **gelatinizes**, proteins **denature**, water binds to biopolymers. - Function: Converting particulate raw material into a homogeneous plasticized melt. - Occupies 40–50 % of screw length — the heart of extrusion cooking. **3. Metering/Pumping Zone:** - Screw flights have **shallow, constant depth**. - Operates as a **positive displacement pump**, generating die pressure of 3–15 MPa. - Function: Build and maintain a stable pressure to drive uniform flow through the die. **Auxiliary components** (for full marks, mention 2): - **Die adapter + die plate** — shapes the extrudate. - **Flying cutter** — cuts extrudate into defined length pellets. - **Steam jacket** — supplies external heat. - **Vacuum vent** — removes residual moisture/vapors. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Definition:** *Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)* is the total mechanical energy input by the extruder screw per unit mass of product processed, expressed in **kJ/kg**. It is the most critical process parameter governing cooking degree, product expansion, and texture. **Mathematical formula:** $$ SME = \frac{2 \pi \cdot \tau \cdot N}{\dot{m}} $$ **Given data:** - Screw speed: $N = 400$ rpm $= \frac{400}{60} = 6.667$ s⁻¹ - Torque: $\tau = 1200$ N·m - Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 2000$ kg/hr $= \frac{2000}{3600} = 0.5556$ kg/s **Step 1 — Numerator calculation:** $$ 2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot N = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 1200 \times 6.667 = 50{,}265.5 \text{ W} $$ **Step 2 — Final division:** $$ SME = \frac{50{,}265.5}{0.5556} = 90{,}470 \text{ J/kg} \approx 90.5 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Comment on product suitability:** A SME of **90.5 kJ/kg** falls in the **mid-range** suitable for **directly expanded snack products** such as: - Corn curls and cheese balls (typical SME: 60–150 kJ/kg). - Lightly puffed breakfast cereals. - 3D-shaped snack pellets. For highly expanded breakfast cereals or TVP, the SME would need to be raised to **200–500 kJ/kg** by increasing screw speed or torque. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **(a)** Compare the working principle of a **co-rotating twin-screw extruder** with a **counter-rotating twin-screw extruder**. Highlight their typical food applications. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the **net throughput equation** of a single-screw extruder, clearly explaining drag flow and pressure flow components. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) | Feature | Co-Rotating Twin-Screw | Counter-Rotating Twin-Screw | |---|---|---| | **Rotation Direction** | Both screws rotate in the same direction | Screws rotate in opposite directions | | **Channel Geometry** | "Figure-8" cross-section, self-wiping | Material trapped in meshing zone | | **Conveying Efficiency** | High — positive displacement with self-cleaning | Moderate — material accumulates in meshing region | | **Pressure Generation** | Moderate (limited by flight profile) | High (gear pump effect at meshing) | | **Mixing Action** | Excellent — material is forced screw-to-screw | Good — high shear in meshing region | | **Residence Time Distribution** | Narrow (close to plug flow) | Wider (some stagnation) | | **Heat Transfer** | Superior — fresh material constantly contacts barrel | Inferior — stagnant layers reduce heat transfer | | **Screw Speed Range** | Up to 800 rpm | Limited to 60–120 rpm (mechanical stress) | | **Typical Food Applications** | Breakfast cereals, TVP, infant foods, pet food, snacks | Confectionery, chocolate conching, candy | | **Cost & Maintenance** | Higher initial cost, lower maintenance | Lower cost, complex gearbox | **Why co-rotating is preferred for most food applications:** The self-wiping geometry prevents **bacterial growth** (critical for food safety), enables **higher screw speeds** for greater throughput, and produces a **narrow residence time distribution** that ensures uniform cooking. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Geometric Unwrapping** Imagine "unwrapping" the screw channel onto a flat plane. The channel becomes a **shallow rectangular trough** of width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ and depth $d$ (flight depth). The barrel wall slides over this stationary trough at velocity $V_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ along the down-channel direction. **Step 2: Identify the Two Flow Components** The total flow in the down-channel direction is a **superposition** of two physical mechanisms: - **Drag Flow (Couette Flow):** Caused by the relative motion of the barrel wall. The moving wall drags material along. - **Pressure Flow (Poiseuille Flow):** Caused by the pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ that develops due to the die restriction. **Step 3: Apply the Momentum Balance** The Navier-Stokes equation for steady, laminar, isothermal flow in the y-direction simplifies to: $$ 0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{L} + \eta \frac{d^{2} V_{z}}{dy^{2}} $$ **Step 4: Solve the Differential Equation** Integrating twice with boundary conditions $V_{z} = 0$ at $y = 0$ (screw root) and $V_{z} = V_{b}$ at $y = d$ (barrel wall): $$ V_{z}(y) = V_{b} \left( \frac{y}{d} \right) - \frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \, y(d - y) $$ **Step 5: Integrate Velocity to Obtain Volumetric Flow** The total flow per channel is found by integrating $V_{z}$ across the channel depth: $$ Q_{ch} = \int_{0}^{d} V_{z}(y) \, W \, dy $$ **Step 6: Evaluate the Two Integrals** Splitting the integral into its two physical components: $$ Q_{ch} = \underbrace{\frac{V_{b} W d}{2}}_{\text{Drag}} - \underbrace{\frac{W d^{3} \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}_{\text{Pressure}} $$ **Step 7: Geometric Back-Transformation** Substituting $V_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W = \pi D \sin\theta$, then multiplying by $\cos\theta$ to project onto the axial direction: $$ \boxed{Q_{net} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^{2} D^{2} N d \sin\theta \cos\theta - \frac{\pi D d^{3} \sin^{2}\theta \, \Delta P}{12 \eta L}} $$ **Engineering significance:** When $\Delta P = 0$ (open die), $Q_{net} = Q_{drag}$ (maximum). As the die restricts flow, $\Delta P$ rises and **back-flow** occurs, reducing throughput. The **operating point** of an extruder is determined by the intersection of this characteristic curve with the die's resistance curve. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting units:** SME must be reported in **kJ/kg**, not J/kg or W. (Cost: 1 mark lost in part b.) > 2. **Skipping the geometric unwrapping step:** KTU examiners specifically look for the term *"unwrapped channel model"* in part b derivations. Skipping this loses 2 marks. > 3. **Confusing twin-screw rotations:** "Co-rotating" and "counter-rotating" are EXACTLY opposite — confusing them in a comparison table costs 2 marks. > 4. **Not drawing the screw zones:** The three zones (feed, kneading, metering) MUST be labelled with their functions. A diagram without functions gets only 3/7 marks. > 5. **Unit conversion in SME problem:** rpm → s⁻¹ and kg/hr → kg/s. Skipping these conversions yields wrong numerical answers and forfeits the entire 4-mark calculation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Common Extruders** - **Extrusion** = continuous HTST process combining *mixing + cooking + shearing + shaping* in a single unit operation. - **Single-screw extruder:** three zones — **feed (deep flight) → kneading (tapered) → metering (shallow flight)**. - **Twin-screw extruder:** preferred for food; **co-rotating intermeshing** is the industry standard for cereals, TVP, pet food. - **SME (Specific Mechanical Energy)** is the **single most important process parameter** — governs texture, expansion, and starch conversion. - **Cold vs Hot:** Cold = pasta, low temperature, no puffing. Hot = snacks/cereals, high temperature, puffed. - **Drag flow** arises from screw rotation; **Pressure flow** arises from die restriction. **Net flow = Drag − Pressure**. - Typical SME ranges — **Snacks: 60–150 kJ/kg | Cereals: 150–300 kJ/kg | TVP: 200–500 kJ/kg**. - Barrel **L/D ratio** for food extruders = **20:1 to 30:1**; barrel temperature = **100–200 °C**; die pressure = **3–15 MPa**. - **Self-wiping** action of co-rotating twin-screws prevents bacterial contamination — critical for food safety. - **Die swell** (extrudate expansion upon leaving the die) is influenced by melt elasticity, temperature, and shear rate. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Common Extruders Used in Food Industry** > *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment regulations and NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework.*

topics.title: Merits and Demerits of Extruder Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Merits and Demerits of Extruder Technology # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Extrusion Technology** is a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which a plasticized food material (typically a starchy or proteinaceous slurry/dough) is forced to flow, under controlled conditions of **moisture (15–35%)**, **temperature (80–200 °C)**, **pressure (20–200 bar)**, and **mechanical shear**, through a die of defined geometry. This simultaneous application of **heat**, **shear**, and **pressure** results in products with unique structural transformations (gelatinization, protein denaturation, expansion, texture formation) in a High Temperature–Short Time (**HTST**) framework. A **Food Extruder** is therefore a *reactor + pump + forming device* combined into a single barrel–screw assembly, capable of performing unit operations such as **mixing, conveying, kneading, cooking, shaping, and expansion** in one continuous pass. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (for 2-mark direct questions):** > *"Extrusion is a process that combines several unit operations, including mixing, kneading, shearing, heating, cooling, shaping, and forming, where a material is forced through a die under varied conditions of pressure, temperature, and moisture to produce a finished food product."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Syringe + Pressure Cooker" Model Imagine a **disposable plastic syringe** filled with toothpaste. As you push the plunger: - The barrel acts as the **screw channel** (conveying). - Your hand pressure mimics the **mechanical shear** of the rotating screw. - The narrow nozzle resembles the **die** (shaping). - If the syringe barrel were externally heated and the dough contained starch, you'd additionally have **gelatinization** — turning a viscous paste into an expanded, crispy snack. In short: **Extrusion = Push (mechanical) + Heat (thermal) + Shape (die) = Snacks, Cereals, and Convenience Foods in one continuous shot.** > [!NOTE] > A real-world parallel: the **gluten strands forming in bread dough during baking** are analogous to the protein–starch network restructured by an extruder screw. The difference is that in an extruder, this restructuring happens in *seconds* and the product is formed *on exit* rather than in a loaf pan. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold-Highlighted) | Parameter | Typical Range (Food Extrusion) | | :--- | :--- | | Barrel Temperature | **80 °C – 200 °C** | | Screw Speed (N) | **100 – 500 rpm** | | Die Pressure | **20 – 200 bar** | | Feed Moisture | **15 % – 35 % (wb)** | | Residence Time | **20 – 200 seconds** | | Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) | **40 – 1000 Wh/kg** | > [!TIP] > Always quote these ranges (especially **HTST 20–200 s** and **SME 40–1000 Wh/kg**) — KTU examiners award full marks only when numerical ranges are explicitly tied to the concept. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Extrusion — A Structured Logic Walk Extrusion is best understood as a *sequence of physical-chemical events* happening along the barrel length: 1. **Feeding Zone (Solid Conveying):** Gravity-fed raw material enters; screw flights push powder forward. 2. **Mixing/Kneading Zone:** Mechanical energy input from the rotating screw mixes water, starch, and additives. 3. **Cooking Zone:** External heaters + viscous dissipation (shear heating) raise temperature above **starch gelatinization (~60–90 °C)** and **protein denaturation (~70–80 °C)**. 4. **Metering/Compression Zone:** Pressure builds; material becomes a viscoelastic melt. 5. **Die Zone:** Sudden pressure drop → **flash evaporation of water** → product expands (puffing) and sets as it cools. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Three Transformations" inside the barrel (must-memorize for KTU):** > 1. **Starch gelatinization** (loss of crystalline order) > 2. **Protein denaturation and cross-linking** (fibrous texture formation) > 3. **Water flash-off at die exit** (porous, expanded structure) ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Physical Meaning | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $SME = \dfrac{\tau \cdot N}{Q}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy — energy input per unit mass | Wh/kg | | 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{h}$ | Shear rate in the screw channel (D = screw dia, h = channel depth) | s⁻¹ | | 3 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Die pressure (Force ÷ Die cross-section) | Pa or bar | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \mu L}$ | Throughput (drag flow − pressure flow) — for Newtonian simplification | kg/s | | 5 | $ER = \dfrac{D_e}{D_d}$ | Expansion Ratio (extrudate diameter ÷ die diameter) | dimensionless | | 6 | $SI = \dfrac{d_{er}}{D_d}$ | Sectional Expansion Index | dimensionless | | 7 | $TD = \dfrac{T_{max} - T_{final}}{T_{max} - T_0} \times 100$ | Thermal Degradation index | % | | 8 | $WHC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$ | Water Holding Capacity (post-extrusion) | g/g | | 9 | $OI = \dfrac{m_f - m_i}{m_i} \times 100$ | Overall Impact — material mass balance across extruder | % | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Formula 1 (**SME**) and Formula 5 (**ER — Expansion Ratio**) are the two most frequently asked. Memorize them in symbol form and be ready to substitute values. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cereal Industry:** Production of **expanded breakfast cereals** (corn flakes, oats) — saves energy by replacing drum-drying with one-pass HTST. - **Snack Foods:** Direct puffed snacks (kurkure-style, third-generation snacks) — eliminates frying in some lines. - **Pet Food & Aquafeed:** Twin-screw extrusion produces **high-density sinking pellets** or **low-density floating pellets** by tuning SME. - **Plant Protein Structuring:** Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP) — meat analogues. - **Encapsulation:** Sensitive bioactives (probiotics, omega-3) protected in a glassy starch matrix — bioavailability increased. > [!TIP] > If KTU asks *"Name three industrial products of extrusion"* — the safest answer set is: **(i) Ready-to-eat cereals, (ii) Pasta, (iii) Texturized vegetable protein / snacks**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Expansion Ratio (ER) of an Extrudate The **Expansion Ratio** is a key textural parameter that determines crunchiness, bulk density, and consumer perception. Let us derive it step by step. ### Step 1 — Define the measured quantities Let $D_d$ = diameter of the die orifice (in mm), and $D_e$ = diameter of the expanded extrudate (in mm), measured after the product has cooled and equilibrated for 24 hours (KTU laboratory convention). ### Step 2 — State the governing ratio By definition, the expansion ratio is the ratio of the cross-sectional diameter of the cooled extrudate to the die diameter. $$ ER = \frac{D_e}{D_d} $$ ### Step 3 — Unit consistency check Both $D_e$ and $D_d$ are measured in the **same units (mm or cm)**. Therefore, ER is **dimensionless**. ### Step 4 — Numerical example (for KTU problem-solving) *Given:* A corn-based snack is extruded through a die of diameter **$4 \text{ mm}$**. After cooling, the product diameter is measured as **$9.6 \text{ mm}$**. *Find:* The Expansion Ratio. **Step 4a.** Write the formula. $$ ER = \frac{D_e}{D_d} $$ **Step 4b.** Substitute the given values. $$ ER = \frac{9.6 \text{ mm}}{4 \text{ mm}} $$ **Step 4c.** Compute the quotient. $$ ER = 2.4 $$ **Step 4d.** Interpret the result. Since $ER > 1$, the product has **expanded** during extrusion. A higher ER indicates a **more porous, lighter, and crunchier** snack. For corn-based third-generation snacks, an ER between **2.0 and 4.0** is considered optimum. ### Step 5 — Sensitivity Insight (high-yield for KTU) - If **feed moisture increases**, ER decreases (water acts as plasticizer; less flash-off). - If **SME increases**, ER increases (more elastic melt, more puffing). - If **die temperature decreases** sharply at the exit, ER increases (more superheated water flashes). > [!NOTE] > A common student mistake in KTU papers: writing $ER = D_d / D_e$. **Always** keep *expanded diameter in the numerator*. ## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) ### Step 1 — Define the variables Let $\tau$ = motor torque (in N·m), $N$ = screw speed (in rev/s), and $Q$ = throughput (in kg/s). ### Step 2 — Mechanical power input The mechanical power delivered to the material is: $$ P_{mech} = 2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot N $$ Here the factor $2\pi$ converts each revolution into the angular displacement of the screw. ### Step 3 — Energy input per unit mass Dividing mechanical power by mass flow rate gives SME: $$ SME = \frac{P_{mech}}{Q} = \frac{2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot N}{Q} $$ ### Step 4 — Converting to Wh/kg (engineering convention) Since $1 \text{ J/s} = 1 \text{ W}$, and dividing by 3600 converts to Wh: $$ SME \left(\frac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right) = \frac{2\pi \cdot \tau \cdot N}{3.6 \cdot Q} $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical example *Given:* $\tau = 50 \text{ N·m}$, $N = 8 \text{ rev/s}$ (≈ 480 rpm), $Q = 4 \text{ kg/s}$. *Find:* SME in Wh/kg. **Step 5a.** Substitute. $$ SME = \frac{2\pi \times 50 \times 8}{3.6 \times 4} $$ **Step 5b.** Numerator evaluation. $$ 2\pi \times 50 \times 8 = 2513.27 \text{ W} $$ **Step 5c.** Denominator evaluation. $$ 3.6 \times 4 = 14.4 $$ **Step 5d.** Final result. $$ SME = \frac{2513.27}{14.4} \approx 174.5 \text{ Wh/kg} $$ **Step 5e.** Interpretation. This SME (≈ 175 Wh/kg) lies in the **medium shear** range — typical for **breakfast cereals** and **third-generation snacks**. For high-protein TVP extrusion, SME is often > 500 Wh/kg. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Merits vs. Demerits (KTU High-Density) | Dimension | Merits (✓ Advantages) | Demerits (✗ Disadvantages) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Process Economics** | Continuous operation → high throughput (200–2000 kg/h); low labor cost | High capital investment (twin-screw extruder ₹ 30 lakh – 2 Cr); high maintenance | | **Energy Efficiency** | Single-step cooking + forming → 30–50 % energy savings vs. conventional baking/frying | Shear heating is hard to control → may over-process heat-sensitive nutrients | | **Product Versatility** | Wide range — snacks, cereals, pasta, TVP, pet food, aquafeed | Limited to plasticizable raw materials (starch/protein); not ideal for fibrous/hard materials | | **Nutritional Impact** | Destroys anti-nutritional factors (e.g., trypsin inhibitors in soy, phytic acid); increases starch digestibility | Maillard browning & lysine loss if $T > 180 \text{ °C}$ | | **Microbial Safety** | HTST process → reduces microbial load significantly | Recontamination possible post-die; downstream drying needed | | **Shelf Life** | Low moisture after drying → shelf-stable (6–12 months) | Lipid oxidation risk in high-fat formulations | | **Product Quality** | Uniform shape, density, texture; customizable expansion | Possible off-flavors (burnt, scorched) at high SME | | **Operational Simplicity** | One machine = multiple unit operations | Requires skilled operator; start-up/shut-down is wasteful | | **Flexibility** | Quick die change → multiple shapes from same line | Long cleaning cycles; cross-contamination risk for allergens | | **Environmental** | No process wastewater; dry process | High electricity load; noise > 85 dB | | **Equipment Wear** | — | Screw and die wear → product drift over time | | **Process Control** | Direct control of T, P, N, moisture | Tight tolerances needed; small deviations → batch failure | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Mnemonic — "VASTE NUTSAFE":** The 8 most-asked advantages fit neatly into **V**ersatility, **A**utomation, **S**peed, **T**hroughput, **E**nergy, **N**utrition (anti-nutrient removal), **U**niformity, **S**afety, **A**vailability of multiple products, **F**lexibility, **E**conomy. Memorize this for rapid recall. ## 3.4 Derivation: Why HTST Improves Digestibility (Biochemical Justification) Starch gelatinization in conventional cooking: $$ \text{Native starch} \xrightarrow{+\Delta H, +H_2O} \text{Starch gel (reversible retrogradation over time)} $$ Starch modification in extrusion (irreversible): $$ \text{Native starch} \xrightarrow{\text{Shear} + \Delta T + \Delta P} \text{Disrupted, amorphous, pre-gelled starch} $$ **Why is extruded starch more digestible?** Step 1: Native starch exists in semi-crystalline granules ($\alpha$-1,4 and $\alpha$-1,6 glycosidic bonds intact). Step 2: In the extruder, the combined shear + temperature + moisture input **destroys the crystalline order** (loss of birefringence). Step 3: The amorphous, fragmented structure exposes more sites to **$\alpha$-amylase** (pancreatic enzyme) in the human gut. Step 4: Empirical relationship — *in vivo* starch digestibility increases by **20–40 %** for extruded cereals compared to their conventionally cooked counterparts. Step 5: Therefore, extrusion is a **"value-addition technology"** — the same raw material (e.g., corn grits) gives a product with **higher caloric and glycemic availability**. > [!WARNING] > Do **not** confuse *digestibility* with *glycemic index*. Extrusion increases both, which is a merit for malnourished populations but a demerit for diabetic consumers — KTU has previously asked this nuance. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Food Extruder ```mermaid flowchart LR HOPPER["Raw Material Hopper"] --> FEEDZ["Feeding Zone"] FEEDZ --> MIXZ["Mixing and Kneading Zone"] MIXZ --> COOKZ["Cooking Zone T 80 to 200 C"] COOKZ --> METERZ["Metering and Compression Zone"] METERZ --> DIEZ["Die Exit Pressure 20 to 200 bar"] DIEZ --> EXP["Flash Expansion and Puffing"] EXP --> COOL["Cooling and Drying"] COOL --> PROD["Final Product Snacks Cereals TVP"] BARREL["Barrel Heater Bands"] -.-> COOKZ SCREW["Rotating Screw N 100 to 500 rpm"] -.-> MIXZ SCREW -.-> METERZ DIE["Die Geometry"] -.-> DIEZ WATERINJ["Water Injection"] -.-> MIXZ CTRL["Control Panel T P N Q"] -.-> BARREL CTRL -.-> SCREW classDef zone fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000; classDef aux fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003e7e,color:#000; classDef ctrl fill:#e8d8f0,stroke:#5a3a8a,color:#000; class FEEDZ,MIXZ,COOKZ,METERZ,DIEZ,EXP,COOL zone; class BARREL,SCREW,DIE,WATERINJ,HOPPER aux; class CTRL ctrl; ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Merits vs. Demerits Decision Map ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Extrusion Process Decision Check"] START --> Q1{"Is the product
plasticizable?
Starch or Protein?"} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is mass production
>= 500 kg/day?"} Q1 -- No --> STOP1["NOT SUITABLE
Demerit: Material Limitation"] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{"Need HTST cooking
and shaping in one step?"} Q2 -- No --> STOP2["Sub-optimal
Small batch better"] Q3 -- Yes --> REC["EXTRUDE
Merits realised"] Q3 -- No --> STOP3["Use conventional
cooking or molding"] REC --> M1["Continuous Output"] REC --> M2["Energy Efficient"] REC --> M3["Versatile Products"] REC --> M4["Nutritional Uplift"] REC --> D1["High Capital Cost"] REC --> D2["Skilled Operator Required"] REC --> D3["Heat Sensitive Loss"] REC --> D4["Wear of Screw and Die"] classDef merit fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#1f7a1f,color:#000; classDef demerit fill:#f7d4d4,stroke:#a01f1f,color:#000; classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000; class M1,M2,M3,M4 merit; class D1,D2,D3,D4 demerit; class START,Q1,Q2,Q3 decision; ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Table — Zone-Wise Transformation Map | Zone | Physical Change | Merits Realized | Demerits Realized | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Feeding | Gravity dosing | — | Bridging/ratholing if moisture < 10 % | | Mixing/Kneading | Hydration, blending | One-step mixing | High torque → power cost | | Cooking | Gelatinization, denaturation | HTST nutrient retention | Lysine loss at $T > 180 \text{ °C}$ | | Metering | Pressure build-up | Continuous throughput | Surge if not controlled | | Die Exit | Flash puffing | Expansion, shape | Surface roughness if die worn | | Post-Die | Cooling, drying | Shelf stability | Lipid oxidation if fat-rich | > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above are **block-level functional flow diagrams** (a fallback adaptation, since physical cross-section drawings of twin-screw geometry are best shown in a separate AutoCAD figure in lab manuals). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define extrusion cooking. List any four advantages of extrusion technology in food processing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Extrusion cooking is a continuous HTST (High Temperature–Short Time) process in which moistened, expansible food material is forced through a die under combined action of **temperature (80–200 °C)**, **shear**, and **pressure (20–200 bar)**, causing starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and expansion of the product at the die exit. - **Four advantages (0.5 mark each — 2 marks total):** 1. **Versatility** — produces snacks, cereals, pasta, TVP, and pet food in the same line. 2. **Energy efficient** — single-step cooking + forming, ~30–50 % less energy than conventional baking. 3. **Continuous high-throughput process** — typically 200–2000 kg/h. 4. **Improved nutritional value** — destroys anti-nutritional factors and increases starch digestibility. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *"extrusion is baking"* and lose the definition mark. Always use the phrase **"HTST thermo-mechanical process with starch gelatinization"** to satisfy the KTU marking scheme. --- ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Mention any three demerits of extruder technology in food processing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Demerit 1 (1 mark):** **High capital and operating cost** — twin-screw extruders require significant upfront investment (₹ 30 lakh – 2 Cr) and skilled operators. - **Demerit 2 (1 mark):** **Nutrient degradation at high SME** — heat-sensitive nutrients (lysine, vitamin C) may be lost when barrel temperature exceeds **180 °C** or SME exceeds **500 Wh/kg**. - **Demerit 3 (1 mark):** **Limited raw material compatibility** — extrusion works best with starch/protein-rich materials; fibrous or high-fat raw materials cause screw slippage and unstable throughput. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Avoid vague statements like *"it is expensive"*. The KTU 2024 scheme awards marks only when you quantify (**"high SME > 500 Wh/kg"**) or name the exact limitation (**"fibrous materials"**). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024):** KTU 2024 ESE convention gives internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** in Part B. Both options are provided below for parallel preparation. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of a **single-screw extruder** with a neat labelled block diagram. Describe the function of each zone. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **eight advantages** and **four disadvantages** of extrusion technology in food processing. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Working Principle (3 marks):** A single-screw extruder consists of a rotating screw (with variable pitch and compression ratio) housed inside a heated barrel. Moistened food material is fed from the hopper; the rotating screw conveys, compresses, shears, and heats the material. The cooked, plasticized mass is forced through a die at the end, where sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation of water** and the product expands and solidifies. **Zone-wise function table (4 marks):** | Zone | Function | | :--- | :--- | | Feeding Zone | Receives raw material from hopper, gravity-assisted dosing | | Kneading/Mixing Zone | Hydrates and homogenizes the feed, builds initial pressure | | Cooking Zone | External + shear heating gelatinizes starch, denatures protein | | Metering Zone | Compresses the melt to high pressure (20–200 bar) | | Die Zone | Shapes the product; flash expansion occurs here | *(Block diagram as in Section 4.1 must be drawn on the answer script for full marks.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Eight Advantages (1 mark for every 2 points — 4 marks total):** 1. **Continuous high-throughput process** (200–2000 kg/h). 2. **HTST cooking** — minimal nutrient loss compared to prolonged boiling/baking. 3. **Multi-functional** — mixing, kneading, cooking, shaping in one unit. 4. **Energy efficient** — 30–50 % lower energy footprint. 5. **Versatile** — snacks, cereals, pasta, pet food, TVP from one machine. 6. **Shelf-stable products** — low final moisture (< 12 %). 7. **Destroys anti-nutrients** (trypsin inhibitors, phytic acid). 8. **Improves digestibility** of starch and protein by 20–40 %. **Four Disadvantages (0.75 mark each — 3 marks total):** 1. **High capital cost** — twin-screw extruders cost ₹ 30 lakh to ₹ 2 Cr. 2. **Skilled operation required** — small deviations in T, P, N, or moisture cause batch failure. 3. **Nutrient degradation** — lysine loss and Maillard browning at $T > 180 \text{ °C}$. 4. **Material limitation** — not suitable for fibrous, high-fat, or non-plasticizable raw materials. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Trap (Part b):** Students often list 5 advantages and 5 disadvantages of the *same* type (e.g., 5 cost-related demerits). The KTU scheme *requires* breadth — at least one point from each of: **process economics, nutritional, product, operational** categories. Mixing categories fetches full marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **single-screw** and **twin-screw extruders**. List **two industrial products** of each. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **nutritional merits** of extrusion cooking in detail. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Parameter | Single-Screw Extruder | Twin-Screw Extruder | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mechanism | Drag-induced conveying | Positive displacement + drag | | Mixing efficiency | Low to moderate | High | | Self-wiping action | Absent | Present (co-rotating) | | Cost | Lower | Higher (1.5–3×) | | Throughput | Lower | Higher and more uniform | | Heat sensitivity control | Moderate | Superior (better residence time distribution) | | Typical products | Pasta, simple snacks | Breakfast cereals, TVP, aquafeed, third-gen snacks | **Two products of each (2 marks):** - **Single-screw:** Pasta, third-generation direct-expanded snacks. - **Twin-screw:** Breakfast cereals, texturized vegetable protein (TVP). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Nutritional Merits of Extrusion (1 mark each):** 1. **Destruction of anti-nutritional factors:** Trypsin inhibitors in soybeans, lectins in legumes, and phytic acid are reduced by **30–80 %** depending on SME. 2. **Improved starch digestibility:** Disruption of crystalline order increases enzymatic hydrolysis by **20–40 %**, beneficial for weaning foods and geriatric nutrition. 3. **Protein denaturation and digestibility:** Solubility and *in-vitro* digestibility of proteins increase post-extrusion. 4. **Dietary fibre modification:** Insoluble fibre can be partially converted to soluble fibre, improving prebiotic potential. 5. **Vitamin retention (HTST advantage):** Short residence time (20–200 s) protects heat-labile vitamins compared to conventional prolonged cooking. 6. **Uniform nutrient distribution:** Liquid additives (lysine, minerals, vitamins) can be injected post-die or in the mixing zone for homogenous fortification. 7. **Maillard-controlled browning:** When operated below **180 °C**, the Maillard reaction is beneficial — improves flavour and colour without excessive lysine loss. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Do not claim *"extrusion always increases digestibility"*. Mention the **HTST condition** and the **SME range** (e.g., *"at SME 100–400 Wh/kg"*) to demonstrate that you understand the process window. Generic statements lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-screen revision — print this section for the day before the exam.** - ✅ **Definition (verbatim for 2-mark questions):** *"Extrusion is a continuous HTST thermo-mechanical process combining mixing, kneading, cooking, and forming in a single barrel–screw assembly."* - ✅ **Three transformations inside the barrel:** (1) Starch gelatinization, (2) Protein denaturation, (3) Water flash-off at die. - ✅ **Key process variables (must memorize):** Barrel T = 80–200 °C; Die P = 20–200 bar; Feed moisture = 15–35 %; Screw speed N = 100–500 rpm; Residence time = 20–200 s; SME = 40–1000 Wh/kg. - ✅ **ER (Expansion Ratio) formula:** $ER = D_e / D_d$ — dimensionless; optimum for corn snacks = 2.0–4.0. - ✅ **SME formula:** $SME = 2\pi \tau N / Q$ (in W/kg) or $SME = 2\pi \tau N / (3.6 Q)$ (in Wh/kg). - ✅ **Top 8 Merits (Mnemonic: VASTE NUTSAFE):** Versatility, Automation, Speed, Throughput, Energy, Nutrition uplift, Uniformity, Safety, Flexibility, Economy. - ✅ **Top 4 Demerits:** High capital cost, skilled operator needed, nutrient loss at high SME/T, material limitations (fibrous/high-fat). - ✅ **Anti-nutrients destroyed:** Trypsin inhibitors, phytic acid, lectins (especially in soy and pulses). - ✅ **HTST advantage:** 20–200 s residence time preserves heat-labile vitamins. - ✅ **Lysine loss threshold:** $T > 180 \text{ °C}$ — quote this number explicitly. - ✅ **Industrial products:** Cereals, pasta, third-generation snacks, TVP, pet food, aquafeed. - ✅ **Single-screw vs. Twin-screw:** Single-screw = drag flow only; Twin-screw = positive displacement + self-wiping → better mixing and residence time control. - ✅ **Valuation mantra:** Always quantify (give numbers, ranges, % improvements) and *categorize* your answer points — process, nutrition, economics, and product quality. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Merits and Demerits of Extruder Technology**

topics.title: Uses of Extruded Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Uses of Extruded Foods # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Extrusion cooking** is a high-temperature, short-time (HTST) thermomechanical process in which food material is forced through a die of predetermined shape and size under controlled conditions of moisture, temperature, pressure, and shear, resulting in continuous shaping and microstructural transformation of the biopolymer matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** > "Extrusion is a process that combines several unit operations including mixing, cooking, kneading, shearing, shaping, and forming in a single, continuous, high-throughput operation. The product obtained is termed an **extruded food**." **Extruded foods** are therefore the end-products of extrusion technology — ranging from breakfast cereals to snacks, pasta, and texturized vegetable proteins (TVPs) — characterized by altered starch granule structure, expanded cellular matrix, and improved digestibility. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Toothpaste Tube" Analogy:** > Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. As the paste comes out the narrow nozzle, it is **forced**, **shaped**, and **structured** simultaneously. Now imagine the same tube is filled with a hot, doughy mixture of cornmeal and water, and as it is pushed through a tiny hole at high pressure, the sudden drop in pressure causes the trapped steam to **puff up** — just like a popcorn kernel. That puff, with its airy, crunchy texture, is exactly what an extruded food is. The same principle, scaled up industrially, gives us corn puffs, breakfast flakes, cheetos, instant noodles, and even fish feed pellets. ## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (Bold Constants) - **Barrel Temperature Range:** 100 °C – 200 °C - **Screw Speed:** 100 – 500 rpm - **Moisture Content in Barrel:** 12 % – 25 % (wet basis) - **Residence Time:** 10 s – 120 s (typically **~30 s**) - **Die Pressure:** 20 – 150 bar - **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):** 100 – 1500 kJ/kg > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam — "MASS-HE":** When asked to list the functions of an extruder, write: > **M**ixing → **A**gitation → **S**hearing → **S**haping → **H**eating → **E**xpanding (puffing). > This covers all six simultaneous operations performed inside a single screw. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Extruded Food Products Extruded foods can be systematically classified based on **expansion behavior**, **ingredient matrix**, and **end-use application**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes product-based classification. ### A. Direct Expanded Products (Snacks) These puff immediately upon exiting the die due to instantaneous flash evaporation of superheated moisture. - **Examples:** Cheese curls (Cheetos), corn curls, third-generation snacks. - **Mechanism:** High SME (≥ 600 kJ/kg) + high barrel temperature + low feed moisture (< 15 %). ### B. Indirect Expanded Products (Pellets) These are dense, non-expanded intermediates that are re-expanded later by frying, hot-air puffing, or microwave heating. - **Examples:** Tortilla chips, 3-D snacks, fabricated potato chips. - **Mechanism:** Low SME (≤ 300 kJ/kg) + cool die + moderate moisture (15 – 20 %). ### C. Breakfast Cereals - **Flaked:** Corn flakes (steel-cut cooked maize, extruded, then rolled and toasted). - **Puffed:** Puffed wheat, puffed rice (extruded under high steam pressure, sudden release). - **Shredded:** Wheat biscuits (Shredded Wheat). ### D. Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP) Defatted soy flour / concentrate is extruded at **> 140 °C** and **> 30 % moisture** to form fibrous, meat-like chunks. ### E. Pasta & Noodles Twin-screw extruders at **low temperature (< 50 °C)** shape semolina dough without gelatinizing starch (cold extrusion). ### F. Pet Foods & Aquafeed - Aquatic feed pellets require high sinking density and water stability → twin-screw extruders with cooker dies. ### G. Crackers, Breadsticks & Co-extruded Products - Co-extrusion: simultaneous filling of a cereal-based outer sheath with a soft inner core (e.g., filled wafer bars). ### H. Modified Starch & Infant Foods - Pregelatinized starch (instant thickening), instant weaning foods (fortified blended flour). ## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Units** | **Typical Range (KTU Board)** | |---|---|---|---| | $$\text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{\dot{m}}$$ | Specific Mechanical Energy: $\tau$ = torque, $\omega$ = angular speed, $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate | **kJ/kg** | 100 – 1500 | | $$\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_e}{D_d}$$ | Expansion Ratio: $D_e$ = extrudate diameter, $D_d$ = die diameter | **dimensionless** | 2 – 16 | | $$\text{SI} = \dfrac{D_e - D_d}{D_d}$$ | Sectional Expansion Index | **dimensionless** | 1 – 15 | | $$\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$$ | Bulk Density: $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume of extrudate | **kg/m³** | 60 – 500 | | $$\text{WAI} = \dfrac{W_g}{W_s}$$ | Water Absorption Index: $W_g$ = water absorbed, $W_s$ = sample weight | **g/g** | 4 – 12 | | $$\text{WSI} = \dfrac{W_{ds}}{W_s}$$ | Water Solubility Index: $W_{ds}$ = dissolved solids weight | **%** | 5 – 60 | | $$Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$$ | Volumetric throughput: $\rho$ = melt density, $v$ = velocity, $A$ = die cross-section | **kg/h** | 100 – 5000 | | $$t_r = \dfrac{V_{\text{barrel}}}{Q}$$ | Residence time: $V_{\text{barrel}}$ = free volume, $Q$ = throughput | **s** | 10 – 120 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** When writing SME in units, **never** write "J/kg" alone. The KTU 2024 marking scheme specifically awards **½ mark** for writing the correct unit **kJ/kg**. Use $\tau$ in N·m and $\dot{m}$ in kg/s to derive kJ/kg natively. ## 2.3 Engineering Significance The extrusion process is a **single-step reactor** that replaces conventional multi-step cooking lines (soak → grind → cook → dry → puff). For KTU students, the most important engineering takeaway is: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core "Why" behind extrusion:** > 1. **HTST preserves nutrients** — vitamins and amino acids that degrade in prolonged boiling remain intact. > 2. **Starch gelatinization + dextrinization** improves digestibility. > 3. **Anti-nutritional factor destruction** — trypsin inhibitors in soy are inactivated above 120 °C. > 4. **Microbial load reduction** — high shear + temperature achieves commercial sterility in seconds. This makes extrusion a **flagship technology** for ready-to-eat (RTE) consumer foods in the KTU 2024 framework. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) The SME is the **single most important parameter** in extrusion. It quantifies how much mechanical work is transferred per unit mass of material and directly correlates with product expansion, density, and texture. ### Step 1 — Define Torque Power The mechanical power delivered by the motor to the screw shaft is the product of torque and angular velocity. $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \omega $$ where $\tau$ is torque in **N·m** and $\omega$ is angular velocity in **rad/s**. ### Step 2 — Convert Angular Velocity to Screw Speed Since food extruders operate at rotational speeds typically measured in **RPM**, we convert: $$ \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} $$ where $N$ is the screw speed in **rpm**. ### Step 3 — Substitute Angular Velocity Substituting $\omega$ into the power equation: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} $$ This gives shaft power in **watts (W)** when $\tau$ is in N·m and $N$ in rpm. ### Step 4 — Define Mass Flow Rate The mass throughput through the extruder die: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{M}{t} $$ where $M$ is the mass of product collected in **kg** and $t$ is the collection time in **seconds**. ### Step 5 — Formulate SME By definition, SME is shaft power divided by mass flow rate: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\dot{m}} $$ ### Step 6 — Combine Equations Substituting $P_{\text{shaft}}$ from Step 3 and $\dot{m}$ from Step 4: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SME} &= \dfrac{\tau \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}}{\dfrac{M}{t}} \\ &= \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot \tau \cdot t}{60 \cdot M} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Final Canonical Form When expressed per unit mass in **kJ/kg**, dividing by 1000: $$ \boxed{\text{SME} \;(\text{kJ/kg}) = \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot \tau \cdot t}{60\,000 \cdot M}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A high SME (> 800 kJ/kg) corresponds to highly expanded, crispy snack products. A low SME (< 300 kJ/kg) yields dense, non-expanded pellets suitable for re-frying. KTU examiners frequently test this correlation. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem (Module 4 — 7 Marks):** > A twin-screw extruder operates at a screw speed of **300 rpm** with a measured torque of **45 N·m**. The extruder discharges **12 kg** of product in **60 seconds**. Calculate the Specific Mechanical Energy input. **Solution:** Step 1 — Convert angular velocity: $$ \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 10 \pi \; \text{rad/s} $$ Step 2 — Compute shaft power: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \omega = 45 \times 10 \pi = 450 \pi \; \text{W} \approx 1413.7 \; \text{W} $$ Step 3 — Compute mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{12}{60} = 0.2 \; \text{kg/s} $$ Step 4 — Compute SME: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{1413.7}{0.2} = 7068.6 \; \text{J/kg} = 7.07 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ Step 5 — Interpretation: $$ \text{SME} = 7.07 \; \text{kJ/kg} \;\; (\text{very low — indicates pellet-grade product}) $$ A board-exam-ready answer would conclude: > *"This SME value is characteristic of **indirectly expanded pellets**, indicating that the product is suitable for **secondary puffing by frying or hot-air expansion**, not for direct expanded snacks."* ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Product vs. Process Parameter | **Product Category** | **Barrel T (°C)** | **Moisture (%)** | **Screw Speed (rpm)** | **SME (kJ/kg)** | **Expansion Ratio** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Direct expanded snacks | 150 – 180 | 12 – 15 | 300 – 500 | 600 – 1500 | 8 – 16 | | Breakfast cereals (flakes) | 120 – 140 | 18 – 22 | 100 – 200 | 300 – 500 | 2 – 4 | | TVP (texturized soy) | 140 – 160 | 25 – 35 | 150 – 250 | 400 – 700 | 1 – 2 (fibrous) | | Pasta (cold extrusion) | 40 – 55 | 28 – 32 | 40 – 80 | 50 – 150 | 1 (dense) | | Pet food (aquafeed) | 110 – 135 | 20 – 28 | 200 – 400 | 200 – 500 | 1.5 – 3 | | Instant weaning food | 130 – 160 | 15 – 20 | 250 – 350 | 400 – 800 | 4 – 8 | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Heuristic:** When asked *"Why does pasta use cold extrusion?"*, the answer is: > *"Starch gelatinization must be **prevented** in pasta to maintain al dente texture and surface binding. Temperatures above 70 °C cause irreversible gelatinization, ruining the product. Hence cold extrusion below 55 °C is employed."* # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Extrusion-Based Food Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Material Receipt
Cereals, Legumes, Starch] --> B[Pre-conditioning
Moisture 12-25 percent
Steam Injection] B --> C[Feed Hopper
Volumetric Feeder] C --> D[Screw Conveying Zone
Conveying, Mixing] D --> E[Kneading and Cooking Zone
Shear, Friction, Heat] E --> F[High Pressure Zone
Barrel 100-200 bar] F --> G[Die Assembly
Shaping Orifice] G --> H[Flash Expansion Zone
Sudden Pressure Drop] H --> I[Knife Cutter
Cutting Extrudate] I --> J[Post Extrusion Processing
Drying, Coating, Seasoning] J --> K[Finished Product Categories] K --> K1[Direct Expanded Snacks] K --> K2[Breakfast Cereals] K --> K3[Texturized Vegetable Protein] K --> K4[Pasta and Noodles] K --> K5[Pet Food and Aquafeed] K --> K6[Instant Weaning Foods] style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#ffb380,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#ff8052,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#ff6633,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#e64d1f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#cc3d17,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#b32d0f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px style K fill:#4d3319,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px style K1 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K2 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K3 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K4 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K5 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style K6 fill:#a3d9a5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: Classification Tree of Extruded Foods ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Extruded Foods] --> R1[By Process Type] R --> R2[By End Use] R --> R3[By Expansion Behaviour] R1 --> R1a[Hot Extrusion] R1 --> R1b[Cold Extrusion] R1a --> R1a1[Snacks, Cereals, TVP] R1b --> R1b1[Pasta, Noodles] R2 --> R2a[Human Foods] R2 --> R2b[Animal Feeds] R2a --> R2a1[Ready to Eat Snacks] R2a --> R2a2[Breakfast Cereals] R2a --> R2a3[Infant Foods] R2b --> R2b1[Dry Pet Food] R2b --> R2b2[Aquaculture Pellets] R3 --> R3a[Direct Expanded] R3 --> R3b[Indirect Expanded] R3 --> R3c[Unexpanded Dense] R3a --> R3a1[Cheese Curls, Corn Puffs] R3b --> R3b1[Tortilla Chips, 3D Snacks] R3c --> R3c1[Pasta, Pellets for Refrying] style R fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style R1 fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R2 fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R3 fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style R1a fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R1b fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R2a fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R2b fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R3a fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R3b fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style R3c fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Process-Parameter Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Screw Speed] --> X[SME] P2[Moisture Content] --> X P3[Barrel Temperature] --> X P4[Feed Rate] --> X X --> Y[Product Properties] Y --> Y1[Expansion Ratio] Y --> Y2[Bulk Density] Y --> Y3[Crispness] Y --> Y4[Color Maillard] Y --> Y5[Water Solubility] style P1 fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style X fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style Y fill:#ff9966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style Y1 fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Y2 fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Y3 fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Y4 fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style Y5 fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **"List any six uses of extruded foods in the consumer food industry."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Direct expanded snack foods such as cheese curls and corn puffs (½ mark). 2. Breakfast cereals including flaked corn, puffed wheat, and shredded biscuits (½ mark). 3. Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP) for meat analogues (½ mark). 4. Cold-extruded pasta and instant noodles (½ mark). 5. Pre-gelatinized starches for instant soups, sauces, and weaning foods (½ mark). 6. Co-extruded products such as filled wafer bars and crackers (½ mark). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners **deduct ½ mark** if students write only "extruded snacks" without specifying the **sub-type** (e.g., direct expanded vs. indirect expanded). Always classify. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) and state its significance in extrusion cooking."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** SME is the mechanical energy imparted per unit mass of food material in the extruder barrel, expressed in **kJ/kg**, given by $\text{SME} = \tau \omega / \dot{m}$. - **Significance — 2 marks:** 1. It governs the degree of starch gelatinization and molecular degradation (1 mark). 2. It directly controls expansion ratio, bulk density, and crispness of the final product (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Classify extruded foods in detail with suitable examples, explaining the role of process parameters in each category. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A co-rotating twin-screw extruder is operated at 350 rpm with a torque of 60 N·m. The extruder delivers 18 kg of product in 45 seconds. Calculate the Specific Mechanical Energy. Comment on the suitability of this SME value for direct expanded snack production. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] The classification is best presented in tabular form (4 marks) with explanation (3 marks): | **Category** | **Barrel T (°C)** | **Moisture (%)** | **SME (kJ/kg)** | **Example** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Direct expanded snacks | 150 – 180 | 12 – 15 | 600 – 1500 | Cheese curls, corn puffs | | Indirect expanded pellets | 90 – 110 | 15 – 20 | 200 – 400 | Tortilla chips, 3-D snacks | | Breakfast cereals | 120 – 140 | 18 – 22 | 300 – 500 | Corn flakes, puffed wheat | | TVP | 140 – 160 | 25 – 35 | 400 – 700 | Soya chunks | | Cold-extruded pasta | 40 – 55 | 28 – 32 | 50 – 150 | Macaroni, instant noodles | **Explanation (3 marks):** - In **direct expanded snacks**, high SME and temperature cause instant starch gelatinization and flash puffing, yielding porous, crispy textures (1 mark). - In **TVP**, high moisture and temperature induce protein denaturation and fibrous alignment, mimicking meat fiber (1 mark). - In **pasta**, low temperature preserves starch crystallinity to maintain binding and firm texture (1 mark). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] Step 1 — Compute angular velocity: $$ \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 350}{60} \approx 36.65 \; \text{rad/s} $$ Step 2 — Compute shaft power: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \omega = 60 \times 36.65 = 2199.1 \; \text{W} $$ Step 3 — Compute mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{18}{45} = 0.4 \; \text{kg/s} $$ Step 4 — Compute SME: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{2199.1}{0.4} = 5497.8 \; \text{J/kg} \approx 5.50 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$ Step 5 — Comment: $$ \text{This SME (5.5 kJ/kg) is too low for direct expanded snacks.} $$ The SME value of **5.5 kJ/kg** falls far below the typical range of **600 – 1500 kJ/kg** required for direct expanded snacks. This product would yield **dense, non-expanded pellets** more suited for indirect expansion (e.g., re-fried tortilla chips) or cold-extruded pasta. **To increase SME, the screw speed or torque (or both) must be raised, and barrel moisture reduced below 15 %.** (3 marks for comment + interpretation) --- ### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** > **(a)** Discuss the role of extrusion in the production of (i) breakfast cereals, (ii) texturized vegetable protein, and (iii) pet food, highlighting the specific process conditions used for each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the principle of cold extrusion with reference to pasta manufacture. Why is temperature control critical in this application? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **(i) Breakfast Cereals (2½ marks):** - Maize or wheat is preconditioned to ~20 % moisture, then extruded at **120 – 140 °C** with SME **300 – 500 kJ/kg** (1 mark). - Starch is gelatinized and partially dextrinized; the extrudate is then **rolled into flakes** and toasted (1 mark). - Examples: Corn flakes, puffed rice (½ mark). **(ii) Texturized Vegetable Protein (TVP) (2½ marks):** - Defatted soy flour (high protein, ~50 %) is extruded at **140 – 160 °C** with **25 – 35 % moisture** (1 mark). - High shear aligns the protein molecules into fibrous structures; on exiting the die, the sudden expansion freezes this fibrous orientation (1 mark). - Rehydrated product has a meat-like, chewy texture (½ mark). **(iii) Pet Food / Aquafeed (2 marks):** - Twin-screw extruders are used to produce **sinking pellets** for fish and shrimp (1 mark). - Conditioning moisture ~25 %, barrel temp **110 – 135 °C**, with **cooker die** for starch pre-gelatinization ensuring **water stability** (1 mark). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Principle of Cold Extrusion (4 marks):** - Cold extrusion is performed at temperatures **below 55 °C** (typically **40 – 50 °C**) to prevent starch gelatinization (1 mark). - Semolina + water (28 – 32 % moisture) is conveyed under pressure by a single- or twin-screw through a bronze die (1 mark). - The dough retains its **crystalline starch granules** and gluten network, producing a dense, firm, structurally integrated pasta (1 mark). - The process relies on **mechanical compaction and shaping only** — no cooking occurs in the barrel (1 mark). **Why Temperature Control Is Critical (3 marks):** - If temperature exceeds **~60 – 70 °C**, **irreversible starch gelatinization** occurs, weakening the gluten-starch matrix (1½ marks). - Gelatinized starch causes the pasta to become **sticky, soft, and prone to breakage** during drying (1 mark). - Hence precise barrel cooling (water-jacketed barrel) is essential to maintain **al dente texture** post-cooking (½ mark). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong SME units (–1 mark):** Writing J/kg instead of kJ/kg. Always express SME in **kJ/kg**. > 2. **Confusing expansion ratio with sectional expansion index (–1 mark):** ER = $D_e/D_d$, while SI = $(D_e - D_d)/D_d$. Do not interchange. > 3. **Generic "snacks" answer (–1 mark):** Always specify *direct expanded* or *indirect expanded*. > 4. **Missing mass flow rate derivation (–2 marks):** When SME is computed, examiners **demand** the $\dot{m} = M/t$ step explicitly. > 5. **Wrong temperature for pasta (–1 mark):** Writing "80 °C" instead of "< 55 °C" loses the cold-extrusion mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Extruded foods** are produced by HTST thermomechanical processing through a die, performing **mixing, cooking, shearing, shaping, and puffing** simultaneously. - **Direct expanded snacks** require **high SME (> 600 kJ/kg)**, low moisture (< 15 %), and high barrel temperature (150 – 180 °C). - **Indirect expanded pellets** require **low SME (< 300 kJ/kg)** and are puffed later by frying, hot air, or microwave. - **Breakfast cereals** are flaked or puffed post-extrusion by rolling/toasting; SME **300 – 500 kJ/kg**. - **TVP** requires **high moisture (> 25 %)** and **> 140 °C** to produce fibrous, meat-like texture from soy protein. - **Cold extrusion (< 55 °C)** is mandatory for pasta to prevent starch gelatinization and preserve al dente quality. - **Pet food / aquafeed** uses **twin-screw + cooker die** for water-stable sinking pellets. - **SME formula (canonical):** $$ \text{SME} \;(\text{kJ/kg}) = \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot \tau \cdot t}{60\,000 \cdot M} $$ - **Expansion ratio ER = $D_e/D_d$** is the **principal index** of puffing quality. - **Bulk density** is **inversely proportional** to expansion ratio — high ER ⇒ low bulk density ⇒ light, crispy product. - **Residence time** in the barrel is typically **10 – 120 s**, making extrusion a true HTST process. - The six functions of an extruder — **MASS-HE** (Mixing, Agitation, Shearing, Shaping, Heating, Expanding) — must be memorized verbatim for 3-mark short-answer questions. > [!TIP] > **Final Board-Exam Advice:** When asked *"Discuss the uses of extruded foods,"* structure your answer under **eight product heads** (snacks, cereals, TVP, pasta, pet food, co-extruded, infant food, modified starch). Each head must have **two facts** — one on process condition and one on product characteristic. This 8 × 2 = 16-point framework reliably scores full 7 marks in Part B.

topics.title: Factors Affecting Extrusion Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT725 - CONSUMER AND CONVENIENCE FOODS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Extrusion Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# FACTORS AFFECTING EXTRUSION PERFORMANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Extrusion** is a continuous high-temperature, short-time (HTST) thermomechanical process in which food material is forced to flow, under controlled conditions of pressure, temperature, and shear, through a die of specific cross-sectional geometry to form products of defined shape, texture, and density. **Extrusion performance** refers to the combined effectiveness of the process in achieving desired product attributes (expansion, texture, color, density) at a given throughput, with specific energy efficiency and product quality consistency.

The **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** of an extrusion process are:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicators of Extrusion**
> - **Throughput (Q)** — mass flow rate (kg/hr)
> - **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** — energy input per unit mass (kJ/kg)
> - **Product Expansion Ratio (ER)** — ratio of final to initial cross-section
> - **Product Density (ρ)** — apparent density (g/cm³)
> - **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** — mean residence time (sec)
> - **Die Pressure (P)** — back pressure at the die (MPa)
> - **Torque (T)** and **Screw Speed (N)** — operational parameters

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Stuffed Pastry Tube" Analogy**
> Think of extrusion like squeezing a thick *kozhukatta* (rice dumpling) paste through a *sevai* (string hopper) press, but on an industrial scale. Imagine pushing a hot, sticky dough forward using a rotating screw (like a giant kitchen mixer pushing dough through a perforated plate). As the dough moves forward:
> 1. It gets **compressed** (squeezed tighter),
> 2. It gets **heated** (from friction and external heaters, like cooking on a tawa),
> 3. It gets **sheared** (twisted and torn like kneading chapati dough),
> 4. It **puffs up** the moment it exits the die (like a crisp *murukku* expanding when hot oil hits moisture).
>
> If the dough is too **wet**, it slips without cooking. If too **dry**, it burns and clogs. If the screw turns too **fast**, it doesn't cook inside. If the **die is narrow**, the product is dense and hard; if wide, it's light and puffy. **Every single one of these variables is a "factor affecting extrusion performance."**

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):** 100 – 1000 kJ/kg (typical range for food extruders)
- **Barrel Temperature:** 80 – 200 °C
- **Screw Speed (N):** 100 – 500 rpm
- **Moisture Content of Feed:** 10 – 30 % (wet basis)
- **Screw L/D Ratio:** 10:1 to 30:1
- **Die Pressure:** 1 – 15 MPa
- **Shear Rate in Screw Channel:** 10 – 1000 s⁻¹
- **Thermal Conductivity of food melt (k):** 0.2 – 0.5 W/m·K

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Extrusion Performance

Extrusion performance is governed by an **interdependent triangle** of three factor categories. The product quality, throughput, and energy efficiency are *all* functions of these factors operating simultaneously.

### A. Raw Material / Feed Factors (Substrate-Dependent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are **intrinsic** to the raw material and cannot be altered during the run, only adjusted in formulation.

1. **Starch Content & Type**
   - Starch is the primary structural biopolymer responsible for expansion.
   - **Amylose/Amylopectin ratio** controls gel strength and elasticity.
   - High amylose → less expansion, denser product.
   - High amylopectin (waxy maize) → more expansion, puffier products.

2. **Moisture Content of Feed (MC)**
   - Most critical and easily adjusted variable.
   - Low moisture (10–15%) → high SME, high expansion, brittle product.
   - High moisture (25–30%) → low SME, dense, chewy product.

3. **Protein Content**
   - Proteins affect **water absorption**, **elasticity**, and **texturization**.
   - Under high shear, proteins denature and form fibrous structures (meat analogues).

4. **Fat Content**
   - Fat acts as a **lubricant** in the screw channel, reducing SME.
   - Excess fat (>5%) causes slippage, reduces expansion, and forms a thin lubricating film on starch granules.

5. **Fiber Content**
   - Reduces expansion, increases density, darkens color.
   - Breaks the continuous starch matrix.

6. **Particle Size of Feed**
   - Fine particles (≤ 250 µm) → uniform hydration, smooth melt.
   - Coarse particles → uneven cooking, gritty texture, unstable flow.

### B. Process / Operational Factors (Run-Time Adjustable)

7. **Barrel Temperature Profile**
   - Typically zoned: feeding (50–80 °C) → kneading (120–160 °C) → cooking (160–200 °C) → metering (140–180 °C).
   - Affects starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and melt viscosity.

8. **Screw Speed (N)**
   - Controls **shear rate**, **SME**, and **residence time**.
   - High N → high shear, high SME, less residence time, more expansion.

9. **Feed Rate (Feed Moisture & Mass Flow)**
   - Affects **fill ratio (f)** in the screw channel.
   - Underfed extruder → starved conditions, low pressure, unstable flow.

10. **Residence Time (t) and RTD**
    - Mean residence time depends on screw speed and fill ratio.

11. **Screw Configuration**
    - Forward conveying, reverse conveying, kneading discs, mixing elements.
    - Influences shear intensity, pressure build-up, and mixing.

### C. Equipment / Design Factors (Fixed by Hardware)

12. **L/D Ratio (Length-to-Diameter of screw)**
    - Higher L/D → more shear, more residence time, better cooking.

13. **Compression Ratio (CR)**
    - Ratio of feed channel depth to metering channel depth.
    - Typical: 1:1 to 5:1.

14. **Die Geometry (Nozzle shape, L/D, cross-section)**
    - Controls expansion, die swell, and back pressure.

15. **Barrel Cooling/Heating System**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All exam-relevant formulas — memorize the **structure**, not just the symbol.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Description |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Throughput (Mass flow)** | $Q_m = \rho \cdot N \cdot V_{screw} \cdot f$ | kg/s | ρ = melt density, f = fill factor (0.2–0.5) |
| 2 | **Volumetric Throughput (Drag flow)** | $Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot H \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta$ | m³/s | D = screw dia, H = channel depth, θ = helix angle |
| 3 | **Pressure Flow** | $Q_p = -\frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot H^3 \cdot \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$ | m³/s | ΔP = pressure rise, η = melt viscosity |
| 4 | **Net Throughput** | $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ | m³/s | Drag flow minus pressure flow |
| 5 | **Specific Mechanical Energy** | $SME = \dfrac{P}{Q_m} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{Q_m}$ | kJ/kg | T = torque (N·m) |
| 6 | **Mean Residence Time** | $t_{res} = \dfrac{V_{barrel}}{Q}$ | sec | V_barrel = free volume of barrel |
| 7 | **Shear Rate in Screw** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{H}$ | s⁻¹ | Approximate average shear rate |
| 8 | **Die Pressure (Power-law fluid)** | $P = K \cdot \left( \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3} \right)^n$ | Pa | K = consistency index, n = flow behavior index |
| 9 | **Expansion Ratio** | $ER = \dfrac{D_{product}^2}{D_{die}^2}$ | — | Ratio of product to die diameter squared |
| 10 | **Apparent Density** | $\rho_a = \dfrac{4 m}{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L}$ | kg/m³ | Mass m, product of diameter D, length L |
| 11 | **Die Swell Ratio** | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | — | Extrudate/die diameter |
| 12 | **Power Consumption** | $P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T$ | W | Torque × angular velocity |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important in industry?**
> - In **breakfast cereal manufacturing** (e.g., corn flakes, puffed rice), SME is tuned to 200–400 kJ/kg for desired crispness.
> - In **pet food extrusion**, expansion ratio is controlled by die geometry + moisture.
> - In **third-generation snacks** (3G snacks: potato-soy blends), protein-starch interactions dictate the screw configuration.
> - The **SME** is the single most reported metric in extrusion research papers — it correlates with starch gelatinization, protein cross-linking, and expansion.

---
