# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)

The SME is the **most important derived quantity** in extrusion. It quantifies the mechanical work input per kilogram of product.

### Starting Definitions

Step 1 — Power delivered by the screw (mechanical energy per unit time):

$$
P_{screw} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T
$$

where $N$ is screw speed in revolutions per second (rev/s) and $T$ is torque in N·m. This is the standard rotational power formula, equivalent to *force × velocity* at the screw shaft.

Step 2 — Convert screw speed from rpm to rev/s:

$$
N_{rev/s} = \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{60}
$$

Substituting into the power equation:

$$
P_{screw} = 2\pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_{rpm}}{60}\right) \cdot T
$$

Step 3 — Throughput is the mass flow rate of product exiting the die:

$$
Q_m = \dfrac{m}{t} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}}\right]
$$

Step 4 — By definition, SME is power input per unit mass throughput:

$$
SME = \dfrac{P_{screw}}{Q_m}
$$

Step 5 — Substitute the expressions from Steps 2 and 3 into Step 4:

$$
SME = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N_{rpm} \cdot T / 60}{Q_m}
$$

Step 6 — Rearranging into the standard form used in literature:

$$
\boxed{\,SME = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60 \cdot Q_m}\,} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}}\right] = \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]
$$

Step 7 — Conversion check: $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$, so dividing W by kg/s gives J/kg. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU problems, the screw speed is usually given in **rpm**, while SME is required in **kJ/kg**. Always remember the **factor of 60** in the denominator and divide the result by 1000 if J/kg → kJ/kg.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Throughput (Drag Flow) in a Screw Extruder

This is the **Newtonian approximation** for an extruder screw channel, often tested in KTU exams.

### Setup

Consider a screw rotating inside a stationary barrel. The melt in the channel moves due to the **relative motion** of the barrel wall over the screw flight. We "unwind" the channel into a flat rectangular surface: width = $\pi D \sin\theta - e \approx W$ (channel width) and depth = $H$ (channel depth perpendicular to flight). The barrel wall moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ at angle $\theta$ to the channel direction.

### Components of Barrel Velocity

Step 1 — Velocity of barrel relative to screw (down-channel component):

$$
V_{bx} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \cos\theta
$$

Step 2 — Velocity of barrel relative to screw (cross-channel component):

$$
V_{bz} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \sin\theta
$$

### Down-Channel Drag Flow

Step 3 — For a Newtonian fluid in a channel of depth $H$ and width $W$, the down-channel volumetric flow is given by the standard flat-plate result (1/2 of plane Couette flow):

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{bx} \cdot W \cdot H
$$

Step 4 — Width of the channel is approximated as $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ (neglecting flight width $e$):

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot (\pi D N \cos\theta) \cdot (\pi D \sin\theta) \cdot H
$$

Step 5 — Apply the trigonometric identity $2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$:

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \pi^2 D^2 N H \cdot \sin(2\theta)
$$

Step 6 — Final drag-flow expression:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta\,} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}\right]
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Flow in the Screw Channel

When flow is opposed by a die at the end of the screw, a **pressure gradient** $dP/dz$ develops, driving a back-flow (Poiseuille component).

Step 1 — For a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel with pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ and depth $H$:

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{W \cdot H^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ and $L = L_{screw}$ (axial length of metering section):

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L_{screw}}
$$

Step 3 — This pressure flow is **opposite** to drag flow (hence the negative sign):

$$
Q_{p, \text{axial}} = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

Step 4 — Final expression:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}\,} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}}\right]
$$

---

## 3.4 Net Throughput and Operating Point

Step 1 — Net flow is drag flow minus pressure flow (the negative sign convention):

$$
Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p
$$

Step 2 — Substituting both expressions:

$$
Q_{net} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta - \left(-\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}\right)
$$

Step 3 — Therefore, the operating point of the extruder is given by:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{net} = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta + \dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When $\Delta P = 0$ (open die), throughput is **maximized** = drag flow only. As die resistance increases, $\Delta P$ rises, reducing net throughput. This is the fundamental reason **die design** is a critical factor in extrusion performance.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: SME Calculation

**Problem:** A twin-screw extruder operates at $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$ with a torque reading of $T = 50 \text{ N·m}$. The mass flow rate of product is $Q_m = 80 \text{ kg/hr}$. Calculate the SME in kJ/kg.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert screw speed to rev/s:

$$
N = \dfrac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ rev/s}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate power:

$$
P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T = 2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 50 = 1570.8 \text{ W}
$$

Step 3 — Convert mass flow to kg/s:

$$
Q_m = \dfrac{80}{3600} = 0.02222 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Step 4 — Compute SME:

$$
SME = \dfrac{P}{Q_m} = \dfrac{1570.8}{0.02222} = 70,684 \text{ J/kg}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to kJ/kg:

$$
\boxed{\,SME = 70.68 \text{ kJ/kg}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is in the lower end of typical food extrusion (100–1000 kJ/kg), suggesting a **high-moisture / low-shear** process.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: How Each Factor Changes Performance

| Factor | Increase → | Throughput (Q) | SME | Expansion (ER) | Density (ρ) |
|--------|-----------|----------------|-----|----------------|-------------|
| Feed moisture | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Screw speed (N) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (mild) | ↑ | ↓ |
| Barrel temperature | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ (then ↓) | ↓ |
| Fat content | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Starch (amylopectin) | ↑ | — | — | ↑ | ↓ |
| Protein content | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Fiber content | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Particle size (coarse) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Compression ratio | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| Die L/D ratio | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| L/D ratio of screw | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |

> [!TIP]
> The arrows show the *direction of change* in the response variable when the input factor is increased. This matrix is **exam gold** for KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME). What is its significance in food extrusion?**

**Model Answer:**

Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) is the mechanical energy input per unit mass of product during extrusion. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
SME = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot T}{Q_m} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]
$$

where $N$ is the screw speed (rev/s), $T$ is the torque (N·m) and $Q_m$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s).

**Significance:**
1. SME is a **single integrated indicator** of the combined effect of screw speed, torque, and feed rate.
2. It directly correlates with **starch gelatinization**, **protein denaturation**, and **product expansion**.
3. Typical values range from **100–400 kJ/kg** for snack extrusion and **400–1000 kJ/kg** for texturized protein products.
4. It is the **most reported parameter** in extrusion research for comparing process severity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List any six factors that affect the extrusion performance of food materials.**

**Model Answer:**

The six key factors are:

| # | Factor | Category |
|---|--------|----------|
| 1 | Feed moisture content | Material |
| 2 | Starch content and amylose/amylopectin ratio | Material |
| 3 | Barrel temperature | Process |
| 4 | Screw speed | Process |
| 5 | Screw L/D ratio | Equipment |
| 6 | Die geometry (nozzle L/D, cross-section) | Equipment |

**Effect Summary:** Higher moisture → lower SME, denser product. Higher screw speed → higher SME, more expansion. Higher L/D → better cooking, more shear.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Internal Choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**a)** Discuss in detail the **raw material factors** that influence extrusion performance. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A single-screw extruder is operated at $N = 250$ rpm with a torque of $T = 80$ N·m. The throughput is $Q_m = 120$ kg/hr. Calculate the **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** in kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Raw material factors** influencing extrusion performance are:

**1. Starch Content and Type**
- Starch is the **primary biopolymer** responsible for expansion in extruded products.
- **Amylose** chains retrograde, forming strong gels → **less expansion, denser** product.
- **Amylopectin** forms weak gels → **higher expansion, more porous** product.
- *Example:* Waxy maize (high amylopectin) gives more puff than regular maize.

**2. Moisture Content of Feed**
- The **most critical** feed variable. Range: 10–30% (w.b.).
- Low moisture (10–15%) → high SME, high expansion, brittle texture.
- High moisture (25–30%) → low SME, dense, chewy texture.
- Moisture acts as a **plasticizer** and **steam generator** (causing puff).

**3. Protein Content**
- Proteins undergo **denaturation and cross-linking** under heat + shear.
- High protein → fibrous, meat-like texture (texturized vegetable protein).
- Reduces expansion by competing with starch for water.

**4. Fat Content**
- Fat acts as an **internal lubricant**, reducing SME and friction.
- > 5% fat → forms a **lubricant film** on starch granules → poor expansion.
- Causes **die lip build-up** and **slip at barrel wall**.

**5. Fiber Content**
- High fiber → **breaks starch matrix** → low expansion, high density.
- Causes die wear and rough texture.
- *Example:* Whole-grain extrudates are denser than refined-flour extrudates.

**6. Particle Size of Feed**
- **Fine particles (≤ 250 µm):** uniform hydration, smooth melt.
- **Coarse particles:** uneven cooking, gritty texture, unstable flow.

**7. Sugar and Salt Content**
- Sugars caramelize and affect color (Maillard browning).
- Salt affects water activity and hence moisture migration during extrusion.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 250$ rpm
- $T = 80$ N·m
- $Q_m = 120$ kg/hr

**Step 1** — Convert screw speed to rev/s:

$$
N = \dfrac{250}{60} = 4.1667 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2** — Calculate power:

$$
P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T = 2\pi \cdot 4.1667 \cdot 80 = 2094.4 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3** — Convert mass flow rate to kg/s:

$$
Q_m = \dfrac{120}{3600} = 0.03333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute SME in J/kg:

$$
SME = \dfrac{P}{Q_m} = \dfrac{2094.4}{0.03333} = 62{,}832 \text{ J/kg}
$$

**Step 5** — Convert to kJ/kg:

$$
\boxed{\,SME = 62.83 \text{ kJ/kg}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (62.83 kJ/kg) is **below the typical snack-extrusion range (100–400 kJ/kg)**, indicating a low-shear, high-moisture process — likely a high-moisture extrusion (HME) for direct-expanded products or wet pet food.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**a)** Explain the **process parameters** that govern extrusion performance, with emphasis on barrel temperature, screw speed, and feed rate. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Derive the expression for **drag flow** and **pressure flow** in a single-screw extruder. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Process parameters** are operational variables that can be changed during the run:

**1. Barrel Temperature Profile**
- The barrel is divided into **3–5 zones**, each independently controlled.
- Typical profile:
  - **Zone 1 (Feeding):** 50–80 °C — preheating.
  - **Zone 2 (Kneading):** 120–160 °C — starch gelatinization.
  - **Zone 3 (Cooking):** 160–200 °C — peak temperature.
  - **Zone 4 (Metering):** 140–180 °C — stabilizes melt.
- High temperature → low melt viscosity, low SME, more expansion (up to a point).
- Excess temperature → product darkens, Maillard browning dominates.

**2. Screw Speed (N)**
- Typical range: **100–500 rpm**.
- Higher N → higher shear rate, higher SME, shorter residence time.
- Expansion ratio is **parabolic** with N (rises to a peak, then falls due to short residence).
- Affects RTD (residence time distribution) — higher N narrows RTD.

**3. Feed Rate (and Feed Moisture)**
- Determines the **fill factor (f)** in the screw channel.
- **Starved feeding** (f < 0.2): unstable, low pressure.
- **Flood feeding** (f = 0.3–0.5): stable, high pressure.
- Higher feed rate → higher throughput but lower SME (per unit mass).

**4. Residence Time and RTD**
- Mean residence time: $t_{res} = V_{barrel}/Q$.
- High moisture and low N → longer residence time → more cooking.

**5. Die Pressure**
- Back pressure from die determines melt temperature rise and expansion.
- Higher die pressure → more stored elastic energy → more puff on exit.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Assumptions for Single-Screw Extruder Flow Model:**

1. The screw channel is "unwrapped" into a flat rectangle of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta$ and depth $H$.
2. The melt is a **Newtonian fluid** (constant viscosity $\eta$).
3. Steady-state, isothermal, laminar flow.
4. The flight width is negligible compared to channel width.
5. No leakage flow over the flight (or it is neglected).
6. Gravity is neglected.

**Drag Flow Derivation:**

The barrel wall moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ relative to the screw. The down-channel component is $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$.

For Couette flow between two parallel plates with relative velocity $V_{bx}$ and gap $H$:

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{bx} \cdot W \cdot H
$$

Substituting $W = \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot \pi D \sin\theta \cdot H
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta\,}
$$

**Pressure Flow Derivation:**

When a die creates a back pressure $\Delta P$ across a screw of axial length $L$, the channel acts as a rectangular duct. For Poiseuille flow:

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

Substituting $W = \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

Since this flow opposes drag flow, we write:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L_{screw}}\,}
$$

**Net Throughput:**

$$
Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta + \dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "minus" sign in the boxed pressure-flow equation reflects that pressure flow acts **opposite** to drag flow — it is the back-flow driven by the die resistance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are LOST:**
> 1. **Wrong unit for screw speed:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s, giving SME off by a factor of 60. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 in drag flow** because students confuse the Couette flow formula. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up signs in pressure flow:** Writing $Q_{net} = Q_d + Q_p$ instead of $Q_d - Q_p$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Listing factors without classification:** Always group them into **Material / Process / Equipment** categories. Ungrouped lists lose structure marks. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing SME (kJ/kg) with power (kW):** SME has units of energy/mass, not power. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the assumptions** in derivations — KTU examiners *always* award 1 mark for "stating assumptions clearly."
> 7. **No diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a well-labeled block diagram of the factors (fishbone / hierarchical) carries **2 marks** that are easy to score.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read this 3 times before exam.**

### A. Core Concept
- **Extrusion performance** is judged by: **SME, throughput, expansion ratio, density, RTD, die pressure**.
- Three factor categories: **Material, Process, Equipment** — always classify.

### B. Material Factors (Intrinsic, formulation-controlled)
- **Starch** (amylose: dense; amylopectin: puffy)
- **Moisture** (high → dense; low → puffy, brittle)
- **Protein** (denatures → fibrous texture, reduces expansion)
- **Fat** (lubricant, > 5% → slip, poor expansion)
- **Fiber** (breaks matrix → dense, dark)
- **Particle size** (fine → uniform; coarse → gritty)

### C. Process Factors (Run-time)
- **Temperature** (zoned profile, peak in cooking zone)
- **Screw speed N** (↑ N → ↑ shear, ↑ SME, ↑ expansion up to a point)
- **Feed rate** (controls fill factor f)
- **Residence time** ($t_{res} = V_{barrel}/Q$)

### D. Equipment Factors (Fixed)
- **L/D ratio** of screw (10:1 to 30:1)
- **Compression ratio** (1:1 to 5:1)
- **Die geometry** (L/D of die nozzle, cross-section)
- **Screw configuration** (forward, reverse, kneading discs)

### E. Must-Memorize Formulas (Top 4)
1. $SME = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{Q_m}$ (kJ/kg, $N$ in rev/s)
2. $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta$
3. $Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$
4. $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$

### F. Numerical Conversions (Always check!)
- $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ rev/s}$
- $1 \text{ kg/hr} = \dfrac{1}{3600} \text{ kg/s}$
- $1 \text{ kJ/kg} = 1000 \text{ J/kg}$

### G. KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution (Module 4)
- **Part A (3 marks):** Definition + significance (Q1) OR List + classify (Q2).
- **Part B (14 marks):** Either (i) 7-mark theory + 7-mark numerical, or (ii) 7-mark theory + 7-mark derivation.

### H. One-Liner Takeaway
> *"Extrusion performance is the symphony of raw material, process, and equipment — change one, and you change everything."*
